title: Why Propagate Alone? Parallel Use of Labels and Features on Graphs

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper examines the "label trick" which enables the parallel propagation of labels and features and benefits various SOTA GNN architectures. The label trick has not yet been rigorously analyzed and so this paper studies it from a theoretical perspective. It first introduces a self-excluded simplification of the label trick by applying an extreme splitting strategy. It then shows that the random splits of the label trick can be regarded as regularization of self-excluded label weights. The paper also discusses broader applications of the label trick in order a) to empower graph-based methods with trainable weights and b) to get rid of the randomness effect by incorporating self-excluded propagation within GNNs composed of linear propagation layers.
reviews.main_review:
(1) The effectiveness of the label trick in improving GNN performance has already been demonstrated in prior work. Because of that, this paper does not repeat these efforts and instead tries to showcase the broader application scenarios from Sec 4. I understand this goal. However, results in the four application scenarios that the authors selected indicate little or no benefit of the label trick. In particular, closely examining, Sec 5.1-5.4 and Tables 1-4, I see that using the label trick gives performance comparable to the baseline methods. This happens in many (if not most) scenarios when I take into account the standard deviation across ten independent runs. 

(2) Following from the previous point, I would like to get more clarity on the role of label trick in GNN training. The study makes a very shallow remark that: "For example, at the time of this submission, the top 10 results spanning multiple research teams all rely on the label trick, as do the top 3 results from the recent KDDCUP 2021 Large-Scale Challenge MAG240M-LSC." That is okay, however, it does not mean that the label trick necessarily leads to better performance. To be able to argue that, one would need to run an ablation study comparing top models from the Challenge with and without the label trick. Are there other desirable effects that made the label trick so popular (e.g., faster convergence, more robust training etc.)?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: I appreciate the formal description of the label trick and the associated theoretical analysis. However, the novel use-cases motivated by that analysis are weak and empirical analysis on the role of the label trick is incomplete.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 1: The contributions are neither significant nor novel.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper examines the "label trick" which enables the parallel propagation of labels and features and benefits various SOTA GNN architectures. The label trick has not yet been rigorously analyzed and so this paper studies it from a theoretical perspective. It first introduces a self-excluded simplification of the label trick by applying an extreme splitting strategy. It then shows that the random splits of the label trick can be regarded as regularization of self-excluded label weights. The paper also discusses broader applications of the label trick in order a) to empower graph-based methods with trainable weights and b) to get rid of the randomness effect by incorporating self-excluded propagation within GNNs composed of linear propagation layers.

Main Review:
(1) The effectiveness of the label trick in improving GNN performance has already been demonstrated in prior work. Because of that, this paper does not repeat these efforts and instead tries to showcase the broader application scenarios from Sec 4. I understand this goal. However, results in the four application scenarios that the authors selected indicate little or no benefit of the label trick. In particular, closely examining, Sec 5.1-5.4 and Tables 1-4, I see that using the label trick gives performance comparable to the baseline methods. This happens in many (if not most) scenarios when I take into account the standard deviation across ten independent runs. 

(2) Following from the previous point, I would like to get more clarity on the role of label trick in GNN training. The study makes a very shallow remark that: "For example, at the time of this submission, the top 10 results spanning multiple research teams all rely on the label trick, as do the top 3 results from the recent KDDCUP 2021 Large-Scale Challenge MAG240M-LSC." That is okay, however, it does not mean that the label trick necessarily leads to better performance. To be able to argue that, one would need to run an ablation study comparing top models from the Challenge with and without the label trick. Are there other desirable effects that made the label trick so popular (e.g., faster convergence, more robust training etc.)?

Summary Of The Review:
I appreciate the formal description of the label trick and the associated theoretical analysis. However, the novel use-cases motivated by that analysis are weak and empirical analysis on the role of the label trick is incomplete.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary
This paper offers a clear formalization and theoretical analysis of the label trick, including a self-excluded simplification and an interpretation of stochastic splits as a form of regularization on the self-excluded label weights. It also discusses broader applications—enabling trainable weights and reducing randomness in linear-propagation GNNs—that could make the technique more principled and potentially more widely applicable. While I find the theoretical framing valuable and original, the empirical validation is not yet commensurate with the claims: across the four application scenarios in Sections 5.1–5.4 (Tables 1–4), the gains are often comparable to baselines once variance is taken into account, and the proposed use-cases are not convincingly demonstrated.

Strengths
- The paper provides a solid and useful formal description of the label trick, clarifying a practice that is widespread yet under-theorized.
- The self-excluded formulation and the view of stochastic label splits as an implicit regularizer are insightful, offering a principled lens on when and why the trick might help.
- The discussion on extending the trick to enable trainable weights and reduce randomness in linear-propagation GNNs is thought-provoking and could influence future method design.

Weaknesses
- Empirical results in Sec. 5.1–5.4 (Tables 1–4) generally show little or no consistent benefit over baselines once standard deviations across ten independent runs are considered; several reported improvements are within error bars.
- The novel use-cases motivated by the analysis are weakly supported; the experiments do not clearly isolate their contribution.
- The empirical analysis of the label trick’s role remains incomplete. In particular, the paper relies on the observation that many top leaderboard entries (top 10 OGB and top 3 KDDCUP MAG240M-LSC) use the label trick, but does not provide evidence that it is the source of their performance.
- There are no ablations on strong, competitive models that would isolate the effect of the label trick, limiting the practical takeaways.

Suggestions for Improvement
- Conduct targeted ablation studies on top-performing challenge models with and without the label trick to directly assess its contribution to performance.
- Provide clearer, quantitative evidence on how and when the label trick improves results, including conditions or regimes where its regularization effect is most beneficial.
- Investigate additional benefits that may explain its popularity—such as faster convergence or more robust training dynamics—even if accuracy gains are modest.

Overall Assessment
Theoretical contributions are strong and novel, and the paper does a good job of formalizing and interpreting a commonly used heuristic. However, the empirical support and the strength of the proposed use-cases are currently limited: reported gains are frequently comparable to baselines when uncertainty is considered, and leaderboard adoption alone does not establish a causal benefit. With the suggested ablations and a more comprehensive empirical analysis, the paper could make a compelling case for the practical value of the label trick.

Decision: 6 (marginally above the acceptance threshold)
I lean toward acceptance primarily for the clarity and utility of the theoretical framing and the potential impact on how the community understands and deploys the label trick, while noting that stronger empirical validation is needed to substantiate the claimed advantages.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors provide a thorough theoretical justification to the recently emerging and heuristically proposed label trick, i.e., adopting a random portion of node labels in the training set as the input node attributes. Specifically, the authors start from the simplest case of label trick and derive the general formulation of the label trick as a regularizer. Motivated by the theoretical analysis, the authors proposed novel use cases of the label trick in broader scenarios.

Main Review:
The work is solid with rigorous formulation, theoretical justification, and novel use cases of the label trick. The authors derive general formulations for the label trick as regularizations in both regression and classification cases. The derivation is presented in a smooth way and provides several inspiring and insightful remarks and conclusions. The proposed use cases based on the theoretical are interesting and promising according to the empirical evaluations. Overall, I recommend acceptance due to the thorough analysis and potentially broader use cases inspired by the work. However, my assessment might be inaccurate due to my limited expertise in this topic.

Summary Of The Review:
A solid and inspiring theoretical work. Novel use cases are introduced based on theoretical grounding and are justified by promising empirical results.

Rating:
8: accept, good paper
reviews.text:
Summary
This paper offers a rigorous theoretical treatment of the “label trick,” wherein a random subset of training node labels is used as input node attributes. The authors develop general formulations that interpret this mechanism as a regularizer applicable to both regression and classification. The derivations are presented clearly and smoothly, with insightful remarks that illuminate why and when the approach should work. Building on the theory, the paper proposes novel use cases, and the accompanying empirical studies are promising and consistent with the theoretical claims. Overall, the work is thorough and suggests potential for broader applications.

Strengths
- Provides a rigorous formulation and theoretical justification of the label trick.
- Derives general results showing the label trick functions as a regularizer in both regression and classification settings.
- The derivations are clear, well organized, and augmented with insightful commentary and conclusions.
- Introduces novel use cases motivated directly by the theoretical analysis.
- Empirical evaluations substantiate the effectiveness of the proposed use cases.
- The analysis is comprehensive and indicates potential for broader adoption and extensions.

Weaknesses
- My assessment may be limited by my own limited expertise in this specific topic area; readers should weigh this caveat accordingly.

Overall Assessment
The paper presents a compelling and well-argued theoretical foundation for the label trick, connects it convincingly to regularization in both regression and classification, and validates the ideas with promising empirical results. The contributions are clearly articulated and likely to be of interest to the community. Despite my limited expertise, the clarity and thoroughness of the analysis give me confidence in the claims.

Decision
8: accept, good paper

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The authors discuss the utilization of labels in graph neural networks (or conversely, the utilization of node features in collective classification algorithms, e.g., label propagation) for node prediction. It expounds on the stochastic 'label trick' whereby training labels are concatenated to node features and are then randomly masked to avoid trivial solutions. With simplifying assumptions (where there are no node features), they show that this procedure is reduced to minimizing fitting and regularization terms, which is used as motivation for a few practical methods.
reviews.main_review:
### Strengths (+) & Weaknesses (–)

(+) The problem that the authors tackle is fundamental/important. The community would benefit from research that connects the ideas of classical label propagation methods and message passing neural networks.

(+)  The approach is well-motivated in that one main issue of the label trick is the undesirable solution of the identity mapping, but only keeping off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix is a seemingly simple fix (whereas diagonal terms are fine for feature propagation). That is to say, Equation (10) makes intuitive sense.

(+) The work expounds upon the use of the label trick, and is right in saying that it has been mainly used as a heuristic, seeing effective use. Studying it further is a worthwhile pursuit.

(–) Some further emphasis/credit should be given to https://arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf who (I believe) first proposed the label trick.

(–) What is the intuition behind equation (3), namely why would one want to express the column space of $Y_{tr}$ by post-multiplying with a weight matrix $W$? I wonder similarly for the trainable Smoothing step in Correct & Smooth.

(–) Given that the paper has some 'theoretical flavor' to it, we should see some comparison to other works that compare label and feature propagation (even if these propagations are not used in parallel), e.g., Jia & Benson 2021 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf).

(–) Some equations are unclear, and the subscript of $\mathbb{E}_{splits}$ is vague/difficult to parse. I would appreciate some elaboration on Equation 5 in particular, how does one go from the second-last term to the last term? Since D_out has cardinality 1, should the expectation not be over a discrete uniform sample from $i \sim \{ 1 \dots n \}$, which would simplify to

$ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in D_{tr}} \ell (y_i, [P(Y_{tr} - Y_i W]_i ) $.

Could you please tell me what I'm missing/failing to see here? Thanks.

More generally, the paper is at times hard to read due to it being notationally dense (particularly the excessive use of subscripts), and the stream of the 'Remarks'. I feel the paper could benefit from one more pass of re-writing.

(–) For Table 2, R^2 value should be used to evaluate regression.

(–) Section 4.1 is unclear and should have greater detail. For example, how is this procedure trained? What is the final model (is it just supervision on the Neumann series with a weight matrix instead of a propagation matrix)? Further, some reference should be given to other papers who learn/modify the adjacency/propagation matrix.

### Questions:

- Why isn't the deterministic label trick used for comparison in the right plot in Figure 1? Further, which GNN is used? I believe this is unspecified in Section 5.3.
- In remark 4, if the regularization is shut off why is there "no chance for overfitting"? 
- If the derivation shows that we can eventually decompose with a regularization term in the deterministic case, is this used for results in Table 3? If it is not used (as it does not seem to be mentioned), why not?

Some less important nitpicks:
- typo in remark 2 "insure" → "ensure"
- inconsistency in use of parentheses vs square brackets in (4) and (5)
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper has strengths and tackles an important problem, but needs further work on the points mentioned above.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors discuss the utilization of labels in graph neural networks (or conversely, the utilization of node features in collective classification algorithms, e.g., label propagation) for node prediction. It expounds on the stochastic 'label trick' whereby training labels are concatenated to node features and are then randomly masked to avoid trivial solutions. With simplifying assumptions (where there are no node features), they show that this procedure is reduced to minimizing fitting and regularization terms, which is used as motivation for a few practical methods.

Main Review:
### Strengths (+) & Weaknesses (–)

(+) The problem that the authors tackle is fundamental/important. The community would benefit from research that connects the ideas of classical label propagation methods and message passing neural networks.

(+)  The approach is well-motivated in that one main issue of the label trick is the undesirable solution of the identity mapping, but only keeping off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix is a seemingly simple fix (whereas diagonal terms are fine for feature propagation). That is to say, Equation (10) makes intuitive sense.

(+) The work expounds upon the use of the label trick, and is right in saying that it has been mainly used as a heuristic, seeing effective use. Studying it further is a worthwhile pursuit.

(–) Some further emphasis/credit should be given to https://arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf who (I believe) first proposed the label trick.

(–) What is the intuition behind equation (3), namely why would one want to express the column space of $Y_{tr}$ by post-multiplying with a weight matrix $W$? I wonder similarly for the trainable Smoothing step in Correct & Smooth.

(–) Given that the paper has some 'theoretical flavor' to it, we should see some comparison to other works that compare label and feature propagation (even if these propagations are not used in parallel), e.g., Jia & Benson 2021 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf).

(–) Some equations are unclear, and the subscript of $\mathbb{E}_{splits}$ is vague/difficult to parse. I would appreciate some elaboration on Equation 5 in particular, how does one go from the second-last term to the last term? Since D_out has cardinality 1, should the expectation not be over a discrete uniform sample from $i \sim \{ 1 \dots n \}$, which would simplify to

$ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in D_{tr}} \ell (y_i, [P(Y_{tr} - Y_i W]_i ) $.

Could you please tell me what I'm missing/failing to see here? Thanks.

More generally, the paper is at times hard to read due to it being notationally dense (particularly the excessive use of subscripts), and the stream of the 'Remarks'. I feel the paper could benefit from one more pass of re-writing.

(–) For Table 2, R^2 value should be used to evaluate regression.

(–) Section 4.1 is unclear and should have greater detail. For example, how is this procedure trained? What is the final model (is it just supervision on the Neumann series with a weight matrix instead of a propagation matrix)? Further, some reference should be given to other papers who learn/modify the adjacency/propagation matrix.

### Questions:

- Why isn't the deterministic label trick used for comparison in the right plot in Figure 1? Further, which GNN is used? I believe this is unspecified in Section 5.3.
- In remark 4, if the regularization is shut off why is there "no chance for overfitting"? 
- If the derivation shows that we can eventually decompose with a regularization term in the deterministic case, is this used for results in Table 3? If it is not used (as it does not seem to be mentioned), why not?

Some less important nitpicks:
- typo in remark 2 "insure" → "ensure"
- inconsistency in use of parentheses vs square brackets in (4) and (5)

Summary Of The Review:
The paper has strengths and tackles an important problem, but needs further work on the points mentioned above.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
The paper focus on the label trick.
The label tricks is a method for graph-based semi-supervised learning.
In this setting, we have some labeled data points and some unlabeled data points, the goal is to label the unlabeled data.
The data points can form vertices of a graph, with edges built based on similarity of the points (eventually with a weighting scheme).
Labels can be diffused along the graph to label unlabeled data thanks to graph Laplacian.
A other way to leverage a graph structure is to build a graph neural networks and learn parameters of this networks to match labels of labeled data.
The authors states that the first method is utilizing labels and the second method is utilizing features based on a graphical model. They say that both should be done simultaneously, and argue that this is what the label trick is doing.

Eventually known labels can used along with features as the input of a GNN, but if we only ask to match labels of labeled data, than a trivial mapping can be learnt (the identity from known labels to the labels of labeled data). Therefore it is smart to use a drop-out strategy where we hide few labels (in particular the ground-truth label) when we want to learn the labels of a labeled data. This is the label trick.

The paper tries to understand what the label trick is about by looking at simple models.
It also discussed on its novel-case of applications.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
- It is nice to take a step back to tries to understand what might look like wizard engineering, and show that the labels tricks can be understood with some simple models.
- This paper is relatively easy to follow and well-written

Weaknesses:
- This results in this paper are not really satisfactory. The background and motivations are not really well explained. And the duality between stochasticity of the label trick and the deterministic regularization is not really back-up by "to-the-point" experiments. 
- The paper is lacking rigor as I will detail later.
- Some claims of the paper are weird to me, in particular, the way the graph is built should indeed use features knowledge. So graph-based Laplacian do leverage features (yet in an unsupervised way). Also if the node features are noisy or unreliable, which will downgrade the performance of GNN, I believe the graph structure than will also be noisy or unreliable for those nodes if the graph is build based on features similarity.
- For a paper that seeks a theoretical explanation of the usefulness of the label trick. I do not have the impression to have learn a lot from it. For example, why self-excluded prediction do not work as well as full execution of the label trick?
Also, why the label trick is better than GNN with a Laplacian regularization akin Pfau et al. or Cabannes et al. with a MixUp regularization akin Lopez-Paz et al.?


Notes

page 1:
1/ The readibility of the paper would be improved if the authors were to go straight to the point and more rigorous. For example of “straight to the point", at line 4 in the introduction, the fact that labels and features vary smoothly over the graph and i.i.d. are two differents problems. And talking about i.i.d. samples is only adding confusion in the mind of the reader (at least in my mind).
For example of “rigorous", in the abstract, talking about the identity mapping from input to output, I believe the authors talks about the transductive setting, in which the feature X is forgotten but a propagation rule is used, which can be null as explained for label leakage in Eq. (3). Clearly the "identity mapping" from inputs in R^d to outputs in R^c is not well defined.

page 2:
1/ It would be nice to explain more clearly the graph formalism. For example:
Each node is linked to a input point x in R^d, and a label in R^c, from which we can form the node feature matrix (or design matrix) X and the label matrix of the nodes (or label matrix) Y. The graph is a undirected and edge have weights. The adjacency matrix is the matrix of weights (symmetric because the graph is undirected), thus S is symmetric. 

2/ "Nodes connected by an edge […] are likely to share the same labels". If the graph is weighted this depend on the weight. If the graph is unweighted, it should be specified.
 
3/ Eq.(1) I believe the second F should be F_tr, otherwise, there is, using pythagoras a penalization pushing F towards zeros on testing data. A close form solution can still be written with this correct formulation as well as the power method. The fact that Y = 0 is an informative labels (e.g. because Y are logits) and that pushing F towards 0 does not bias the system should be specified.
Also it would be nice to specify F \in R^{n \times c}, and to specify also that this minimizing this objective can be done independently according to each coordinates of \Y = \R^c, if there is no constraints on F.

page 3:
1/ There is a mistake with P \approx PY on the second paragraph. 
Also the approximation can be written as an equality with an inifinite sum since \lambda S is contracting (equality with a series being here understood as the convergence in any norm).

2/ "where […] P smooths the training labels” is weird since P is not used to defined F^(k). This observation should be written after the definition of P = (1 - \lambda)(I - \lambda S)^{-1}.

page 4:
1/ "model from (…)”: the parenthesis should be removed

2/ A note on SGC would be highly appreciated, as well as an explanation on the relevance of such a model. Space can be found by condensing section 3.2. and 3.3.

3/ What is P in Eq.(3), P was defined as “a propagation matrix”, how is it fixed here?

Overall while having justifications of what people do in practice is nice, I think this paper fall short of providing a satisfactory analysis of the label tricks, especially since it does not rigorously explained it superiority compared to other methods.
I think this stream of research is valuable but this is too early for publication.
reviews.correctness: 4: All of the claims and statements are well-supported and correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper focus on the label trick.
The label tricks is a method for graph-based semi-supervised learning.
In this setting, we have some labeled data points and some unlabeled data points, the goal is to label the unlabeled data.
The data points can form vertices of a graph, with edges built based on similarity of the points (eventually with a weighting scheme).
Labels can be diffused along the graph to label unlabeled data thanks to graph Laplacian.
A other way to leverage a graph structure is to build a graph neural networks and learn parameters of this networks to match labels of labeled data.
The authors states that the first method is utilizing labels and the second method is utilizing features based on a graphical model. They say that both should be done simultaneously, and argue that this is what the label trick is doing.

Eventually known labels can used along with features as the input of a GNN, but if we only ask to match labels of labeled data, than a trivial mapping can be learnt (the identity from known labels to the labels of labeled data). Therefore it is smart to use a drop-out strategy where we hide few labels (in particular the ground-truth label) when we want to learn the labels of a labeled data. This is the label trick.

The paper tries to understand what the label trick is about by looking at simple models.
It also discussed on its novel-case of applications.

Main Review:
Strength:
- It is nice to take a step back to tries to understand what might look like wizard engineering, and show that the labels tricks can be understood with some simple models.
- This paper is relatively easy to follow and well-written

Weaknesses:
- This results in this paper are not really satisfactory. The background and motivations are not really well explained. And the duality between stochasticity of the label trick and the deterministic regularization is not really back-up by "to-the-point" experiments. 
- The paper is lacking rigor as I will detail later.
- Some claims of the paper are weird to me, in particular, the way the graph is built should indeed use features knowledge. So graph-based Laplacian do leverage features (yet in an unsupervised way). Also if the node features are noisy or unreliable, which will downgrade the performance of GNN, I believe the graph structure than will also be noisy or unreliable for those nodes if the graph is build based on features similarity.
- For a paper that seeks a theoretical explanation of the usefulness of the label trick. I do not have the impression to have learn a lot from it. For example, why self-excluded prediction do not work as well as full execution of the label trick?
Also, why the label trick is better than GNN with a Laplacian regularization akin Pfau et al. or Cabannes et al. with a MixUp regularization akin Lopez-Paz et al.?


Notes

page 1:
1/ The readibility of the paper would be improved if the authors were to go straight to the point and more rigorous. For example of “straight to the point", at line 4 in the introduction, the fact that labels and features vary smoothly over the graph and i.i.d. are two differents problems. And talking about i.i.d. samples is only adding confusion in the mind of the reader (at least in my mind).
For example of “rigorous", in the abstract, talking about the identity mapping from input to output, I believe the authors talks about the transductive setting, in which the feature X is forgotten but a propagation rule is used, which can be null as explained for label leakage in Eq. (3). Clearly the "identity mapping" from inputs in R^d to outputs in R^c is not well defined.

page 2:
1/ It would be nice to explain more clearly the graph formalism. For example:
Each node is linked to a input point x in R^d, and a label in R^c, from which we can form the node feature matrix (or design matrix) X and the label matrix of the nodes (or label matrix) Y. The graph is a undirected and edge have weights. The adjacency matrix is the matrix of weights (symmetric because the graph is undirected), thus S is symmetric. 

2/ "Nodes connected by an edge […] are likely to share the same labels". If the graph is weighted this depend on the weight. If the graph is unweighted, it should be specified.
 
3/ Eq.(1) I believe the second F should be F_tr, otherwise, there is, using pythagoras a penalization pushing F towards zeros on testing data. A close form solution can still be written with this correct formulation as well as the power method. The fact that Y = 0 is an informative labels (e.g. because Y are logits) and that pushing F towards 0 does not bias the system should be specified.
Also it would be nice to specify F \in R^{n \times c}, and to specify also that this minimizing this objective can be done independently according to each coordinates of \Y = \R^c, if there is no constraints on F.

page 3:
1/ There is a mistake with P \approx PY on the second paragraph. 
Also the approximation can be written as an equality with an inifinite sum since \lambda S is contracting (equality with a series being here understood as the convergence in any norm).

2/ "where […] P smooths the training labels” is weird since P is not used to defined F^(k). This observation should be written after the definition of P = (1 - \lambda)(I - \lambda S)^{-1}.

page 4:
1/ "model from (…)”: the parenthesis should be removed

2/ A note on SGC would be highly appreciated, as well as an explanation on the relevance of such a model. Space can be found by condensing section 3.2. and 3.3.

3/ What is P in Eq.(3), P was defined as “a propagation matrix”, how is it fixed here?

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper proposes a deterministic version for the label trick in GNNs (and other graph frameworks), that is based on a self-exclusion operator for the propagation process in order to avoid label leakage. The authors propose some applications in other label propagation settings. Experiments are shown to compare with regular label propagation, with and without label trick, effect of regularization term in the deterministic setting vs randomized one.
reviews.main_review:
Strong points:
1.	The paper provides a relative new framework for GNNs where the stochastic label trick is replaced by a deterministic alternative which focuses on exclusion of self propagation, the alternative seems to be novel
2.	Showing an upper bound in  Thm 2 on the deterministic proposal with respect to the cross entropy loss is a valuable result.

Weak points:
1.	Motivation: I still feel unconvinced on what are the reasons for replacing the non-deterministic label trick with a deterministic one, except of course under sampling. Is there a computation bottleneck as well? 
2.	The authors claim that prior use of the label trick has not been theoretically substantiated but what is the Deterministic advantage here in terms accuracy/ speed/ any other advantage?
3.	The experimental validation leaves me a bit puzzled: except for the graph in Fig 1 there no actual comparison between the deterministic and SotA random label tricks. The authors instead focus on comparisons that are not directly related to what they propose in the paper (e.g. trainable vs non-trainable, with label trick vs without). Why is that so?
4.	My main concern is that the experimental validation even for the experiments on the trainable version and use of the label trick is not convincing enough. I see that in some examples the difference between either the trainable label propagation vs the traditional one, as well as the use of the label trick do not show significant improvement (if at all) in particular when the STD is taken into account.
5.	There are some missing important references on the subject of label propagation in graph and the incorporation of label data:
a.	Regularization on Graphs with Function-adapted Diffusion Processes Arthur D. Szlam, Mauro Maggioni, Ronald R. Coifman; 9(55):1711−1739, 2008.
6.	Many of the algorithms mentioned are not fully understood and self-contained to be easily addressed by the reader, also what is the reference to C&S?

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper proposes a deterministic version for the label trick in GNNs (and other graph frameworks), that is based on a self-exclusion operator for the propagation process in order to avoid label leakage. The authors propose some applications in other label propagation settings. Experiments are shown to compare with regular label propagation, with and without label trick, effect of regularization term in the deterministic setting vs randomized one.

Main Review:
Strong points:
1.	The paper provides a relative new framework for GNNs where the stochastic label trick is replaced by a deterministic alternative which focuses on exclusion of self propagation, the alternative seems to be novel
2.	Showing an upper bound in  Thm 2 on the deterministic proposal with respect to the cross entropy loss is a valuable result.

Weak points:
1.	Motivation: I still feel unconvinced on what are the reasons for replacing the non-deterministic label trick with a deterministic one, except of course under sampling. Is there a computation bottleneck as well? 
2.	The authors claim that prior use of the label trick has not been theoretically substantiated but what is the Deterministic advantage here in terms accuracy/ speed/ any other advantage?
3.	The experimental validation leaves me a bit puzzled: except for the graph in Fig 1 there no actual comparison between the deterministic and SotA random label tricks. The authors instead focus on comparisons that are not directly related to what they propose in the paper (e.g. trainable vs non-trainable, with label trick vs without). Why is that so?
4.	My main concern is that the experimental validation even for the experiments on the trainable version and use of the label trick is not convincing enough. I see that in some examples the difference between either the trainable label propagation vs the traditional one, as well as the use of the label trick do not show significant improvement (if at all) in particular when the STD is taken into account.
5.	There are some missing important references on the subject of label propagation in graph and the incorporation of label data:
a.	Regularization on Graphs with Function-adapted Diffusion Processes Arthur D. Szlam, Mauro Maggioni, Ronald R. Coifman; 9(55):1711−1739, 2008.
6.	Many of the algorithms mentioned are not fully understood and self-contained to be easily addressed by the reader, also what is the reference to C&S?

Summary Of The Review:
Some fundamental issues in the paper are preventing me from accepting this paper: the experimental validation is insufficient and unconvincing. The motivation for proposing the deterministic alternative to the label trick is not discussed.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary
The paper proposes a deterministic, self-exclusion-based variant of the label trick to prevent label leakage in label propagation, and provides a theoretical upper bound (Theorem 2) under cross-entropy loss. While the idea is novel and the bound is a meaningful contribution, the motivation for replacing the established stochastic label trick is not sufficiently articulated, and the empirical validation does not convincingly demonstrate practical advantages. Overall, the work appears marginally below the acceptance threshold due to gaps in justification and evidence for the deterministic approach.

Strengths
- The deterministic self-exclusion framework to avoid label leakage is a seemingly novel alternative to the stochastic label trick.
- Theorem 2 provides an upper bound under cross-entropy loss, which is a valuable theoretical result and helps formalize the proposed approach.

Weaknesses
- The motivation for adopting a deterministic alternative remains unclear beyond sampling considerations. The manuscript does not convincingly argue why determinism is preferable in terms of accuracy, stability, theoretical guarantees beyond the provided bound, or computational efficiency.
- The practical benefits are not demonstrated. Claims of theoretical substantiation are not matched with evidence of improved accuracy, speed, or other concrete advantages.
- Direct, head-to-head comparisons with state-of-the-art stochastic label trick baselines are missing, with Figure 1 being the only such comparison. The experimental focus shifts to other axes (e.g., trainable vs. traditional propagation, with vs. without label trick), which does not answer the central question of whether the deterministic variant is better than stochastic alternatives.
- Reported empirical gains are small and often within standard deviations, making the conclusions about superiority or robustness unconvincing.
- Important related work is missing, notably Szlam, Maggioni, and Coifman (2008) on function-adapted diffusion, which is pertinent to label propagation and the use of label information.
- Some algorithmic descriptions are not sufficiently self-contained; references are unclear (e.g., “C&S”) and certain components lack adequate explanation for readers not already familiar with the area.

Suggestions for improvement
- Strengthen the motivation by explicitly articulating the benefits of determinism over the stochastic label trick: potential gains in accuracy, stability, theoretical guarantees beyond the current bound, or concrete computational efficiencies.
- Discuss computational trade-offs in depth, including potential bottlenecks or advantages of the deterministic self-exclusion operator relative to stochastic sampling.
- Provide comprehensive, head-to-head empirical comparisons between the deterministic and stochastic label tricks across multiple datasets and settings, beyond the limited comparison in Figure 1.
- Augment the experimental analysis with statistical testing and clearer reporting of variance to establish the significance (or lack thereof) of observed improvements.
- Add missing references, especially Szlam, Maggioni, and Coifman (2008), and ensure all referenced methods (e.g., C&S) are clearly defined and properly cited.
- Improve the clarity and self-containment of the algorithmic descriptions so that the method can be followed without extensive prior knowledge.

Overall assessment
The paper introduces a novel deterministic self-exclusion mechanism and contributes a useful upper bound result (Theorem 2). However, the central claim—that determinism is preferable to the stochastic label trick—is not sufficiently justified, and the experiments do not provide compelling, statistically supported evidence of practical benefits. Missing related work and unclear algorithmic exposition further weaken the case. I therefore recommend a rating of 5: marginally below the acceptance threshold.

extracted_ideas.summary: The reviewer finds the proposed deterministic, self-exclusion-based variant of the label trick novel and appreciates the theoretical upper bound (Theorem 2), but is unconvinced by the motivation and experimental validation, citing missing direct comparisons to the stochastic version and limited empirical gains. Overall, the paper is viewed as marginally below acceptance due to insufficient justification and evidence for the deterministic approach.
strengths: Introduces a seemingly novel deterministic alternative to the stochastic label trick based on self-exclusion to prevent label leakage.
Provides an upper bound (Theorem 2) for the cross-entropy loss, which the reviewer considers a valuable theoretical result.
weaknesses: Insufficient motivation for replacing the non-deterministic (stochastic) label trick with a deterministic one; unclear reasons beyond sampling considerations.
Unclear practical advantages of the deterministic approach (e.g., accuracy, speed, computational benefits) despite claims of theoretical substantiation.
Lack of direct, thorough comparisons between the deterministic method and state-of-the-art stochastic label trick baselines (only Figure 1 provides such a comparison).

Experimental evidence is not convincing; reported improvements (e.g., trainable label propagation vs. traditional, with vs. without label trick) are small and often not significant when standard deviations are considered.
Missing important related work on label propagation and incorporating label data (e.g., Szlam et al., 2008).
Some algorithmic descriptions are not sufficiently self-contained or clear, and references (e.g., to C&S) are unclear or missing.
suggestions: Strengthen the motivation for the deterministic alternative by explicitly articulating benefits (e.g., accuracy, stability, theoretical guarantees, computational efficiency) over the stochastic label trick.
Discuss potential computational bottlenecks or advantages of the deterministic approach relative to the stochastic method.
Include comprehensive, head-to-head empirical comparisons between the deterministic and stochastic label tricks across multiple datasets and settings (beyond Figure 1).
Augment experimental validation with statistical analyses to demonstrate significance of improvements and address variance across runs.
Add missing references, particularly Szlam, Maggioni, and Coifman (2008), and ensure all referenced methods (e.g., C&S) are properly cited and clearly explained.
Improve clarity and self-containment of algorithm descriptions so readers can follow without extensive prior knowledge.
main_points: The deterministic self-exclusion framework is novel but lacks clear justification for replacing the stochastic label trick.
Theorem 2’s upper bound under cross-entropy loss is a meaningful theoretical contribution.
Experimental design does not adequately compare deterministic vs. stochastic label tricks; focus is on other comparisons (e.g., trainable vs. non-trainable, with vs. without label trick).
Observed empirical gains are limited and often within standard deviation, weakening the conclusions.
Important related work is missing, and some method references/definitions (e.g., C&S) are not adequately provided.
technical_details: Deterministic method is based on a self-exclusion operator to avoid label leakage during propagation.
Theorem 2 establishes an upper bound for the deterministic proposal under the cross-entropy loss.
Figure 1 is cited as the only direct comparison between deterministic and stochastic label tricks.
Concerns about the statistical significance of reported improvements due to standard deviations.
Missing citation: Szlam, Maggioni, Coifman (2008) "Regularization on Graphs with Function-adapted Diffusion Processes."
Unclear or missing reference for "C&S" and insufficiently self-contained algorithm descriptions.

title: Imitation Learning by Reinforcement Learning

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper contributes a theoretical analysis of imitation learning (IL) under deterministic experts. The paper shows that IL in this setting can be reduced to RL with a stationary reward, and the stationary reward minimizes the total variation distance between the expert and the learner. Several empirical experiments were presented to validate the theoretical analysis.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
1. The paper shows that imitation learning (IL) may be reduced to RL with stationary reward for deterministic experts. The analysis provides the theoretical grounding for several existing IL methods. The paper also contributes the connection between the stationary reward to minimizing f-divergence typically seen in adversarial IL, proving that the stationary reward minimizes total variation distance.

2. The proof is clear, well motivated and easy to follow.

3. The experiment studies an important aspect of IL, which is the relationship between the amount of expert data and the IL's performance.

Weakness:
1. Similar to previous works, the practical algorithm uses soft reward (1 - min L_2 distance) to avoid reward sparsity. While a very reasonable choice, the soft reward violates the critical assumption of deterministic expert. Could the authors explain the implications of the soft reward on the theoretical results?

2. The empirical evaluation is limited, with several related questions unaddressed. For instance, the SAC expert appears to be rather weak (in terms of its performance) across different environments. It is unclear whether similar patterns would be observed if the expert matches the level of the performance observed in previous works (e.g. https://github.com/berkeleydeeprlcourse/homework/tree/master/hw1/experts). Another question is how the expert is trained has any impact on the IL performance. For instance, SAC implementation typically directly constraint the range of the action, while a Gaussian expert only clips the action as a post-processing step. In my experiences, these details have quite significant impacts on IL's performance and I hope the authors could expand on the experiments to further validate their theoretical results.

Questions:
1. Comparison between Wang et al. 2019 vs the 1-min L_2 distance reward. Could the authors motivate why the latter reward is used and how it compares to Wang et al. 2019?

2. Could the authors further elaborate on the assumption of the deterministic expert? E.g. How much does it matter in the practical algorithm? Is it even possible to differentiate between stochastic vs deterministic one from limited training data?

The paper contributes a theoretical analysis of imitation learning (IL) under deterministic experts. The paper also contributes the connection between the stationary reward to minimizing f-divergence typically seen in adversarial IL, proving that the stationary reward minimizes total variation distance.

3. The experiment studies an important aspect of IL, which is the relationship between the amount of expert data and the IL's performance.

It is unclear whether similar patterns would be observed if the expert matches the level of the performance observed in previous works (e.g. https://github.com/berkeleydeeprlcourse/homework/tree/master/hw1/experts). Another question is how the expert is trained has any impact on the IL performance. For instance, SAC implementation typically directly constraint the range of the action, while a Gaussian expert only clips the action as a post-processing step. In my experiences, these details have quite significant impacts on IL's performance and I hope the authors could expand on the experiments to further validate their theoretical results.

1. Comparison between Wang et al. 2019 vs the 1-min L_2 distance reward. Could the authors motivate why the latter reward is used and how it compares to Wang et al. 2019?

2. Could the authors further elaborate on the assumption of the deterministic expert? E.g. How much does it matter in the practical algorithm? Is it even possible to differentiate between stochastic vs deterministic one from limited training data?

6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary
This paper presents a clear and compelling theoretical result: for deterministic experts, imitation learning can be reduced to reinforcement learning with a stationary reward that minimizes total variation, thereby grounding several existing IL approaches and connecting them to adversarial IL through an f-divergence minimization lens. The proofs are well-structured and accessible. However, the practical instantiation deviates from the key deterministic-expert assumption via a soft reward, and the empirical validation is limited, relying on seemingly weak experts and leaving several important experimental questions unanswered. Overall, I find the theoretical contribution strong enough to merit publication, but I encourage the authors to address the practical gaps.

Strengths
- Establishes that imitation learning with deterministic experts reduces to RL with a stationary reward, providing a clean theoretical foundation for a class of IL methods.
- Connects the stationary reward to total variation minimization, situating the work within f-divergence frameworks and clarifying links to adversarial IL.
- Proofs are clear, well-motivated, and easy to follow.
- Empirical section includes a useful investigation of how IL performance scales with the amount of expert data.

Weaknesses
- The practical algorithm replaces the theoretically motivated reward with a soft reward, 1 − min L2 distance to the expert dataset, to mitigate sparsity. This setup violates the critical deterministic-expert assumption underpinning the theory, and the implications for guarantees are not discussed.
- The empirical evaluation is narrow in scope. The SAC experts used appear relatively weak across environments (e.g., compared to widely used Berkeley Deep RL course experts), raising concerns about the generality of the findings.
- It remains unclear whether the reported results would hold with stronger experts and against stronger baselines aligned with prior benchmarks.
- The study does not examine how expert training details—such as action range handling via direct constraints (typical in SAC) versus Gaussian policies with post-hoc clipping—affect IL performance, despite these choices plausibly having a significant impact.
- Several closely related experimental questions remain unaddressed, limiting the practical significance of the otherwise strong theory.

Suggestions for Improvement
- Clarify the theoretical implications of using the soft reward 1 − min L2 distance: how does this choice interact with, or violate, the deterministic-expert assumption, and what properties (if any) are preserved relative to the stationary reward derived in the theory?
- Expand the empirical evaluation to include stronger expert policies commensurate with prior benchmarks, and compare against stronger baselines to assess generality.
- Analyze sensitivity to expert training details, particularly action range handling (direct constraints vs post-hoc clipping), and quantify how these factors influence IL performance under the proposed approach.
- Provide a clear comparison and motivation between the chosen soft reward and the reward formulation in Wang et al. (2019), including when each is preferable and how they relate to the TV/f-divergence perspective.
- Elaborate on the practical relevance of the deterministic expert assumption: under what conditions is it reasonable, and how might one distinguish deterministic vs stochastic experts from limited offline data?

Overall Assessment
The paper’s main theoretical contribution—reducing IL with deterministic experts to RL with a stationary reward that minimizes total variation and links to adversarial IL—is novel, insightful, and clearly presented. However, the practical algorithm diverges from the assumptions required by the theory, and the empirical validation is limited and potentially influenced by weak experts and unexamined expert-training choices. I recommend acceptance on the strength and clarity of the theory, contingent on addressing the requested clarifications and expanding the empirical support in a revision.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
In this paper, the authors show that, for deterministic experts, imitation learning can be done by reduction
to reinforcement learning with a stationary reward. theoretical analysis both
certifies the recovery of expert reward and bounds the total variation distance
between the expert and the imitation learner, showing a link to adversarial imitation
learning. Experiments are given to confirm that the reduction works well in
practice for continuous control tasks.
reviews.main_review:
The paper includes potentially interesting results. Some comments that can improve the paper are given below. 
1) In this paper, average rewards are considered. Is there any reason to consider the average reward MDP instead of discounted MDP, which is more widely used?
2) It is not clear what the norm || ||_{TV} means.
3) It would be better to discuss what kinds of RLs can be used in Proposition 1. It is not clear in the current paper. 
4) It would be better if the definition of intrinsic reward and extrinsic reward is explained in this paper. 
5) It is not clear how the imitation learner can learn the expert policy from the intrinsic reward in section 3. 
It would be better if the process is explained in more details.
6) In the experiment, it is not clear how the comaprative analysis is done. Because the authors did not develop new algorithms, 
it would be better to explain details of the comparative analysis. 
 7) Overall, the organization of the paper can be improved further to more clearly deliver the ideas by adding more detailed backgrounds.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary
The paper presents a reduction from imitation learning to reinforcement learning via an intrinsic reward, with accompanying theoretical guarantees and empirical evidence. The theory certifies recovery of the expert reward and provides a bound in total variation distance, and the work draws a clear connection to adversarial imitation learning. Empirically, the reduction appears to work in practice on continuous control tasks without introducing new algorithms. While the contributions are potentially interesting, the paper currently suffers from clarity and presentation issues that make some of the core ideas difficult to follow.

Strengths
- Provides potentially interesting results with both theoretical and empirical components.
- Offers a theoretical analysis that certifies recovery of the expert reward and bounds the total variation distance.
- Establishes a link to adversarial imitation learning, helping to situate the work within existing literature.
- Demonstrates that the reduction can work in practice on continuous control tasks, using existing RL methods.

Weaknesses
- The choice to model the problem as an average-reward MDP rather than a discounted MDP is not sufficiently justified.
- The total variation norm is referenced but not explicitly defined, leaving ambiguity in the statements and proofs.
- Proposition 1 lacks clarity on which RL algorithms are admissible and under what conditions they satisfy the assumptions.
- The notions of intrinsic versus extrinsic reward are not clearly defined or distinguished in the text.
- Section 3 does not adequately explain how optimizing the intrinsic reward leads to imitation of the expert policy; the mechanism and learning process are underspecified.
- The experimental comparisons are under-described, especially important given that no new algorithms are proposed; readers need more detail on how comparisons were conducted.
- Overall organization and background exposition are insufficient, making the paper harder to parse than necessary.

Suggestions for Improvement
- Explain the modeling choice of average-reward MDPs: why this setting is used here, how it compares to the more common discounted formulation, and the implications of this choice for both theory and practice.
- Explicitly define the total variation norm and the exact objects over which it is computed in the analysis.
- Clarify the scope of Proposition 1 by specifying which classes of RL solvers apply (e.g., on-policy vs. off-policy, model-free vs. model-based) and the conditions under which they meet the proposition’s requirements.
- Provide clear and consistent definitions for intrinsic and extrinsic rewards, and use these terms uniformly throughout.
- Expand Section 3 with a step-by-step account of how learning from the intrinsic reward leads to imitation: what is optimized, how the policy is updated, what assumptions are needed, and how the result connects to the stated guarantees.
- Augment the experimental section with detailed descriptions of the comparative setup and methodology: what baselines are used, how the comparisons are conducted in the absence of a new algorithm, what metrics and protocols are followed, and how hyperparameters and training budgets are handled.
- Improve organization and add background to make the paper more self-contained; a clearer narrative thread from problem setup to theory to algorithmic instantiation to experiments would substantially improve readability.

Overall Assessment
This work has meaningful theoretical contributions, a useful connection to adversarial imitation learning, and promising empirical indications on continuous control. However, the clarity and presentation issues outlined above impede understanding and reproducibility. With improved justification of modeling choices, precise definitions, clearer statements of applicability, a more detailed explanation of the learning mechanism, and a better-documented experimental setup, the paper would be significantly strengthened. I view the contribution as marginally above the acceptance threshold and recommend a rating of 6, contingent on the authors addressing these presentation and clarity concerns.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper considers imitation learning problem which aims at obtaining a policy that imitate expert behavior. Authors considers using reinforcement learning with a stationary reward constructed by expert datasets. Through theoretical analysis, the corresponding imitation policy is proved to achieve high expected per-step intrinsic reward and extrinsic reward. The difference between the expert policy and the imitation policy is bounded with a high probability. Some empirical experiments are performed on continuous control tasks. The results show this method is comparable with other algorithms while the algorithm is simpler.
reviews.main_review:
The idea of using reinforcement learning to imitate expert behavior is simple to execute compared to other imitation learning methods. I have a concern regarding the sparse reward function used in this paper. When training a reinforcement learning model, the sparse reward becomes problematic when you apply RL algorithm to solve it. Although the better intrinsic reward leads to better extrinsic reward, it is still troublesome to get a good policy by any reinforcement learning algorithm with this sparse reward function. This makes the result less useful than just applying other comparison methods. Could you give more explanation on this?
In term of computation time, the sparse reward can cause problem of bad convergence rate. It is much clearer if you can provide comparison of computation time of different algorithms in the experiment section. 
Overall, this paper is well written and very clear to readers. The result is promising and interesting from a theoretical point of view.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This paper provides rigorous analysis for using reinforcement learning in imitation learning. The result is novel and shows the potential to accomplish imitation learning in a straightforward way. The intrinsic reward function reduced by the expert dataset is simple to construct. By setting a constant one reward for matching the demonstrated action in a demonstrated state, any reinforcement learning algorithm can provide policy for such a MDP problem. The result in this paper gives intuition of how this method performs in terms of expected return. And sufficient experimental results are shown to verify their theoretical results.

Authors considers using reinforcement learning with a stationary reward constructed by expert datasets. Through theoretical analysis, the corresponding imitation policy is proved to achieve high expected per-step intrinsic reward and extrinsic reward. The difference between the expert policy and the imitation policy is bounded with a high probability. Some empirical experiments are performed on continuous control tasks. The results show this method is comparable with other algorithms while the algorithm is simpler.

Main Review:
The idea of using reinforcement learning to imitate expert behavior is simple to execute compared to other imitation learning methods. I have a concern regarding the sparse reward function used in this paper. When training a reinforcement learning model, the sparse reward becomes problematic when you apply RL algorithm to solve it. This makes the result less useful than just applying other comparison methods. Could you give more explanation on this?

Summary Of The Review:
This paper provides rigorous analysis for using reinforcement learning in imitation learning. The result is novel and shows the potential to accomplish imitation learning in a straightforward way. The intrinsic reward function reduced by the expert dataset is simple to construct. By setting a constant one reward for matching the demonstrated action in a demonstrated state, any reinforcement learning algorithm can provide policy for such a MDP problem. The result in this paper gives intuition of how this method performs in terms of expected return. And sufficient experimental results are shown to verify their theoretical results.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary
This paper proposes a simple reduction of imitation learning (IL) to reinforcement learning (RL) by defining a stationary, intrinsic reward derived from expert demonstrations: the agent receives a reward of 1 only on demonstrated state–action pairs. The authors provide rigorous theoretical analysis, including high-probability bounds on the difference between the expert and the learned imitation policy, and argue that policies achieving higher intrinsic reward will also obtain higher extrinsic reward. Empirically, on continuous control tasks, the method achieves performance comparable to standard baselines, and the experiments are sufficient to substantiate the main theoretical claims.

Strengths
- The paper is clearly written and easy to follow.
- Theoretical contributions are rigorous, with meaningful high-probability performance bounds.
- The reduction of IL to RL via a stationary, demonstration-derived reward is novel and conceptually straightforward.
- The intrinsic reward construction (binary reward on demonstration matches) is simple and makes the approach easy to implement relative to many existing IL methods.
- Empirical results on continuous control are competitive with other algorithms.
- The experiments adequately verify the key theoretical assertions.

Weaknesses
- The binary, highly sparse intrinsic reward may make RL training difficult in practice, potentially leading to poor convergence rates and slow learning.
- Because of these sparsity-induced challenges, the approach may be less practical than comparison methods that provide denser learning signals.
- The paper does not report computation time, runtime comparisons, or convergence behavior across methods, leaving open questions about practical efficiency.

Suggestions for Improvement
- Provide a more detailed explanation of how the method mitigates the well-known challenges of training with sparse rewards, and why it remains effective in practice despite the limited signal. Clarifying mechanisms that enable stable or efficient learning under this reward design would strengthen the empirical narrative supporting the theory.
- Include runtime and convergence comparisons across the evaluated algorithms. Reporting training time, sample throughput, and convergence profiles would help assess practical efficiency and address concerns about slow learning under sparse rewards.

Overall Assessment
The contribution is novel, theoretically well-grounded, and refreshingly simple. The empirical results are promising and lend credibility to the theoretical claims. However, the reliance on a sparse, binary reward raises practical concerns about training difficulty and convergence speed, and the absence of runtime/convergence analyses limits the assessment of practical utility. With additional clarification and empirical evidence on computational efficiency and learning dynamics, the paper’s impact would be considerably strengthened. As it stands, I find the work marginally above the acceptance threshold due to its conceptual clarity, rigorous analysis, and competitive performance.

Rating: 6 (marginally above the acceptance threshold)

This paper provides rigorous analysis for using reinforcement learning in imitation learning. The result is novel and shows the potential to accomplish imitation learning in a straightforward way. The intrinsic reward function reduced by the expert dataset is simple to construct. By setting a constant one reward for matching the demonstrated action in a demonstrated state, any reinforcement learning algorithm can provide policy for such a MDP problem. The result in this paper gives intuition of how this method performs in terms of expected return. And sufficient experimental results are shown to verify their theoretical results.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

extracted_ideas.summary: The review finds the paper well written with rigorous, novel theoretical analysis and a simple IL-by-RL approach that achieves comparable performance to baselines. However, it raises concerns that the sparse, binary reward may make RL training difficult and slow, and requests runtime comparisons and more explanation on handling sparse rewards.
strengths: Clear and well-written presentation.
Rigorous theoretical analysis with high-probability bounds on the difference between expert and imitation policies.
Novel and straightforward reduction of imitation learning to RL with a stationary reward.
Simple intrinsic reward construction from expert data (reward 1 on demonstrated state-action pairs).
Method is simpler to execute than many other imitation learning approaches.
Empirical results on continuous control show performance comparable to other algorithms.
Experiments are sufficient to verify the key theoretical claims.
weaknesses: The sparse (binary) reward used can make RL training problematic, potentially leading to difficulty in learning a good policy.
The approach may be less practically useful than comparison methods due to challenges inherent to sparse rewards.
Sparse rewards may cause poor convergence rates during training.
Lack of reported computation time/runtime comparisons across methods in the experiments.
suggestions: Provide more explanation and justification for how the method mitigates the challenges of learning with a sparse reward and why it remains effective in practice.
Include a comparison of computation time/runtime and convergence behavior across the evaluated algorithms in the experimental section.
main_points: The proposed IL-by-RL approach is simple and theoretically grounded, but its sparse reward design could hinder practical RL training.
Better intrinsic reward leading to better extrinsic reward is shown theoretically, yet obtaining high intrinsic reward with sparse signals may be difficult in practice.
Empirical results are promising and comparable, but computational efficiency and convergence speed should be explicitly evaluated.
Overall contribution is novel and straightforward, making the approach attractive if practical concerns are addressed.
technical_details: Intrinsic reward is defined as a constant one for matching demonstrated actions in demonstrated states (binary, sparse reward).
Theoretical results include high-probability bounds on the difference between expert and imitation policies.
The paper argues that achieving higher intrinsic reward leads to higher extrinsic reward.
Sparse reward may cause bad convergence rates in RL training.
Experiments are conducted on continuous control tasks, but reported runtimes across methods are not provided.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The authors propose an algorithm for imitation learning which rewards the agent for observing state-action pairs that are part of the demonstration-set. The main contribution of the paper is the theoretical analysis which shows that the algorithm, given sufficient expert data, matches the expert’s occupancy distribution and expected reward. The proof comes in three parts: first, the authors relate expert rollouts to the limiting distribution of the expert’s policy. Second, the authors put a probabilistic bound on the expected agreement with expert-data based on the mixing time of the agent. Finally, the authors use this bound on expected agreement to bound the difference in expected extrinsic reward. The authors also provide an empirical evaluation in standard control benchmarks.
reviews.main_review:
The paper is generally well written and easy to follow; however, the structure is sometimes unusual. The proofs would be easier to follow if the authors moved Lemmas from Appendix C & D into the main body while moving some of the detailed proofs from the main body into the Appendix.

The derived bound is non-trivial, but it is unclear how significant it is. The main issue lies in the assumption on the amount of data that is required which is linear (with a factor > 1) in the squared size of the state-space (note that the bound remains large even if \delta is set to 1). Such a large amount of expert data is required since rewarding agreement is not a valid strategy when expert data is sparse: imagine a sub-sampled expert trajectory where the agent has the capability to return to previously seen states. In a more typical distribution-matching objective, the agent has to visit all the demonstration states and thus still has to complete the trajectory. In the objective proposed by the authors, the agent can maximize reward by returning to the same demonstration-state repeatedly. Naturally, if demonstrations are available in all states and the agent’s representational capacity is unlimited, then maximizing agreement is trivially optimal. The author’s bound does not require demonstrations to be available in all states and may thus be useful; however, the required amount of expert data is extremely large and no statement can be made if less data is available.

In the related works section, the authors claim that the method is minimizing the GAIL-objective. This is misleading; the GAIL objective is equivalent to minimizing the JS divergence to the expert histogram for all choices of N while the proposed method only minimizes TV distance and thus JS divergence if N is large enough. 

For the empirical evaluation, the authors use an L2-distance as a stand-in for the reward used in the analysis. This is necessary as the reward is undefined in continuous state-action spaces (similarly, it will be too sparse in large discrete state-action spaces), but the implications of that heuristic are not explored in the paper. It would be good if the authors could add motivation and discussion for this form of the reward. The results are very positive, but it is unclear whether they are due to the theoretical properties of the algorithm or due to the particular nature of the mujoco walkers. Since the reward is a part of the observation-space in these domains, using simple heuristics like squared distance can plausibly lead to high performance; however, this would not generalize to other domains. It is furthermore problematic that the hardest benchmark task, humanoid, is missing as the given baseline algorithms have no problem solving it. Finally, the reported numbers for behavioral cloning are significantly worse than what has previously been reported in the literature.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The proposed bound is non-trivial and potentially useful; however, it would benefit from additional context to show whether it is truly significant. The empirical results are interesting, but rely on a heuristic that is not well-motivated and may be domain specific.
reviews.correctness: 4: All of the claims and statements are well-supported and correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors propose an algorithm for imitation learning which rewards the agent for observing state-action pairs that are part of the demonstration-set. The main contribution of the paper is the theoretical analysis which shows that the algorithm, given sufficient expert data, matches the expert’s occupancy distribution and expected reward. The proof comes in three parts: first, the authors relate expert rollouts to the limiting distribution of the expert’s policy. Second, the authors put a probabilistic bound on the expected agreement with expert-data based on the mixing time of the agent. Finally, the authors use this bound on expected agreement to bound the difference in expected extrinsic reward. The authors also provide an empirical evaluation in standard control benchmarks.

Main Review:
The paper is generally well written and easy to follow; however, the structure is sometimes unusual. The proofs would be easier to follow if the authors moved Lemmas from Appendix C & D into the main body while moving some of the detailed proofs from the main body into the Appendix.

The derived bound is non-trivial, but it is unclear how significant it is. The main issue lies in the assumption on the amount of data that is required which is linear (with a factor > 1) in the squared size of the state-space (note that the bound remains large even if \delta is set to 1). Such a large amount of expert data is required since rewarding agreement is not a valid strategy when expert data is sparse: imagine a sub-sampled expert trajectory where the agent has the capability to return to previously seen states. In a more typical distribution-matching objective, the agent has to visit all the demonstration states and thus still has to complete the trajectory. In the objective proposed by the authors, the agent can maximize reward by returning to the same demonstration-state repeatedly. Naturally, if demonstrations are available in all states and the agent’s representational capacity is unlimited, then maximizing agreement is trivially optimal. The author’s bound does not require demonstrations to be available in all states and may thus be useful; however, the required amount of expert data is extremely large and no statement can be made if less data is available.

In the related works section, the authors claim that the method is minimizing the GAIL-objective. This is misleading; the GAIL objective is equivalent to minimizing the JS divergence to the expert histogram for all choices of N while the proposed method only minimizes TV distance and thus JS divergence if N is large enough. 

For the empirical evaluation, the authors use an L2-distance as a stand-in for the reward used in the analysis. This is necessary as the reward is undefined in continuous state-action spaces (similarly, it will be too sparse in large discrete state-action spaces), but the implications of that heuristic are not explored in the paper. It would be good if the authors could add motivation and discussion for this form of the reward. The results are very positive, but it is unclear whether they are due to the theoretical properties of the algorithm or due to the particular nature of the mujoco walkers. Since the reward is a part of the observation-space in these domains, using simple heuristics like squared distance can plausibly lead to high performance; however, this would not generalize to other domains. It is furthermore problematic that the hardest benchmark task, humanoid, is missing as the given baseline algorithms have no problem solving it. Finally, the reported numbers for behavioral cloning are significantly worse than what has previously been reported in the literature.

Summary Of The Review:
The proposed bound is non-trivial and potentially useful; however, it would benefit from additional context to show whether it is truly significant. The empirical results are interesting, but rely on a heuristic that is not well-motivated and may be domain specific.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
