Response:

title: Understanding approximate and unrolled dictionary learning for pattern recovery

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper studies dictionary learning where assumes that data can be represented as a linear combination of a few atoms of a matrix called dictionary. Traditionally, one way to approach the problem is to set up a min-min (bi-convex) optimization problem known as lasso or basis pursuit and solve it through alternating minimization (alternate between a sparse coding step and a dictionary update). The paper compares gradient based alternating minimization which uses an analytic gradient (given the code estimate, compute the gradient) to unrolled-based dictionary learning which uses backpropagation (automatic differentiation) through an iterative algorithm estimating the code (inner problem) to compute the gradient for update of the dictionary. This paper borrows results from (Ablin et al., 2020) that had studied min-min optimization problems when the objective functions are smooth, differentiable, and strongly convex. Specifically, solving lasso iteratively through ISTA or FISTA, after support selection, with some assumption on the dictionary, the problem of this paper is reduced to the strongly convex case of (Ablin et al., 2020). Hence, the results follow. Their contribution that makes their paper different from (Ablin et al., 2020) is the study of the Jacobian and instability of the convergence prior to support selection. This very same model (dictionary learning through unrolled algorithms) have been already studied theoretically by [1] (which is missing in the citations) and empirically in the context of convolutional dictionary learning by this paper (Tolooshams et al., 2020) that they cite.
reviews.main_review:
Pros: The paper is written clearly. They study an interesting problem. Given the summary above, the stability analysis of the Jacobian and insights of limited-depth backpropagation to improve stability is new and of interest (proposition 2.5). The authors have provided comprehensive numerical results along with real applications to support their theories and claims. The visualization of loss landscape is interesting and has not previously explored for dictionary learning.

Cons: Given (Ablin et al., 2020, Tolooshams et al., 2020, and [1]), the novelty of the paper is incremental. Specifically, proposition 2.2 is known results from Ablin et al., 2020. Given the support and uniqueness assumption of the code, the reconstruction loss is strongly convex, and the optimization is reduced to the case in Ablin et al., 2020. Hence, proposition 2.3 follows. Stochastic CDL using unrolled networks has been previously explored in (Tolooshams et al., 2020) in the context of spike sorting and image denoising. Theoretical analysis and comparison of g1 and g2 for dictionary learning is known from [1].

The authors must clearly indicate their contributions and how their work differs from (Ablin et al., 2020, Tolooshams et al., 2020, and [1]). An additional analysis to make this work different from prior works can be providing dictionary recovery type of results similar to [2]. Overall, a better organization of the paper is recommended. See my detailed comments below. 

- There are some statements that are not fully supported and are not completely accurate.
	- For example, In "Gradient estimation in dictionary learning" paragraph, the authors mention that " ... complete theoretical analysis of these problems are arduous". It is not clear what that means. Indeed, [2] is missing where it studies gradient-based alternating minimization for dictionary learning and provides convergence guarantees.
	- The statement on "... computing optimal sparse codes at each gradient step is unnecessary to recover the dictionary" is not precise. Indeed,  one factor that is crucial is sparsity of the code.
	- The statement before Section 3 is not generally true. For example, the instability issue does not exist for the case of strongly convex function or a another loss function.
	- The statement "we notice that there is no gain in the usage of DDL for the minimization of F_N without learning the steps sizes" seems to be contradicting the whole idea of "a better gradient estimation with DDL compared to AM". Elaborations needed.
	- The statement "... taking sub-windows of the full signal and performing SGD ..." is not precise. It depends whether you define a global loss function over the whole image or local for each patch.
	- The authors refer to the dictionary learning problem as non-convex. However, a more accurate statement is "bi-convex".

- How can you guarantee that invertibility of D^TD on the support stays along the whole iterations of alternating minimization?

- In dictionary learning, you usually learn the dictionary for a finite set of examples. However, (1) does not reflect this and aims to learn D for only one example y.

- The proof of proposition 2.2, the authors should clearly cite and highlight that Proposition 2.2 is a result from Ablin et al., 2020.

- How is lambda chosen to guarantee dictionary recovery in the numerical examples given the data generation of y = Dx? Given the overcomplete dictionary, very low lambda may result in false positive in support recovery which will affect the dictionary recovery result. The value of lambda is indeed crucial at the presence of noise to avoid noise overfitting.

- Experiments: 
	- The paper has skipped a lot of experimental details in the main text and included only in the supp. Here are some that is recommended to be included in the main. How many iterations is "full"? At what iteration of the dictionary learning, Figure 1, 2, 3, 4 are based on (i.e., at dictionary initialization, after convergence, etc.)? Please elaborate what DL-Oracle is? How do you compute the optimal loss?
	- Why the dictionary error in Figure 4 (right) does not go to zero, and if their theory and analysis can explain that.
	- How to you define D of interest used in S(C)?
	- In Figure 5, do the authors train with various N values or train with the max N and look into the denoising performance given the code estimate at iteration n (i.e., Dx_n)?
	- What is the PSNR of the noisy image?
	- Intuitively, you may expect to find a trend in performance as you move from 100 batch size (which is not very low) to full batch. However, Figure 5 does not show such trend. Is there any insight for this?


- Other comments:
	- In the introduction, it is missed to mentioned that n  >> m.
	- "we propose to study the correlation between the gradients ...". It is not clear how we can show dictionary recovery given such comparison.
	- In (6), does y entails the entire data? Is there a notion of # of examples?
	- The change of y-axis from Figure 4 to 5 is confusing. Recommend to use the same x-axis on Figure 5 (left) as in Figure 4.
	- What is the SNR for when the noise variance is 0.1?
	- What are the properties of the dictionary and code, in addition to lambda, that can make support selection faster?
	- Recommend to differentiate between L in proposition 2.2 and 2.3 which are different.

- There are various typos and change of notation:
	- What is matrix A? there are numerous places that we see AD.
	- D has size of m x n but also n x L.
	- S is used once for the support and another time as a matric for dictionary error.
	- In the proof of Proposition 2.1, argmin must be over D \in C not D in general. In addition, z must have dimension m not L.

[1] B. Tolooshams and D. Ba, "PUDLE: implicit acceleration of dictionary learning by backpropagations", 2021.

[2] N. Chatterji and P. Bartlett, "Alternating minimization for dictionary learning: Local convergence guarantees", 2017.


------------
after discussion

See discussion on "Revision of the paper" comment initiated by the author's for my updated review. I hope the authors find my overall comments and the discussion useful in improving their manuscript. My main concern had been in relation to prior work and its novelty. The authors have addressed this with proper citation and additional explanation in the intro. I have updated my rating accordingly, increased the technical novelty to 3 (given the focus of the paper on the regime before support identification), however still kept the empirical novelty to 2.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
The paper contains some new results compared to prior works (stability analysis of the Jacobian and limited-depth backprop). However, the papers lacks novelty given the works from Ablin et al., 2020, Tolooshams et al., 2020, and [1] (see above for details). Given the current formulation, results, and version of the paper, the paper's contributions do not distinguish itself from prior works. Hence, I do not recommend an acceptance of this paper. I hope that authors find my comments helpful and can address my concerns detailed in the review.

------------
after discussion

The authors explained how their work with focus on instability of the Jacobian and analysis prior to support identification distinguishes itself from prior studies. Given this and the additional citations, I have increased my score.

Cons: Given (Ablin et al., 2020, Tolooshams et al., 2020, and [1]), the novelty of the paper is incremental. Specifically, proposition 2.2 is known results from Ablin et al., 2020. Given the support and uniqueness assumption of the code, the reconstruction loss is strongly convex, and the optimization is reduced to the case in Ablin et al., 2020. Hence, proposition 2.3 follows. Stochastic CDL using unrolled networks has been previously explored in (Tolooshams et al., 2020) in the context of spike sorting and image denoising. Theoretical analysis and comparison of g1 and g2 for dictionary learning is known from [1].

The authors must clearly indicate their contributions and how their work differs from (Ablin et al., 2020, Tolooshams et al., 2020, and [1]). An additional analysis to make this work different from prior works can be providing dictionary recovery type of results similar to [2]. Overall, a better organization of the paper is recommended. See my detailed comments below. 

- There are some statements that are not fully supported and are not completely accurate.
	- For example, In "Gradient estimation in dictionary learning" paragraph, the authors mention that " ... complete theoretical analysis of these problems are arduous". It is not clear what that means. Indeed, [2] is missing where it studies gradient-based alternating minimization for dictionary learning and provides convergence guarantees.
	- The statement on "... computing optimal sparse codes at each gradient step is unnecessary to recover the dictionary" is not precise. Indeed,  one factor that is crucial is sparsity of the code.
	- The statement before Section 3 is not generally true. For example, the instability issue does not exist for the case of strongly convex function or a another loss function.
	- The statement "we notice that there is no gain in the usage of DDL for the minimization of F_N without learning the steps sizes" seems to be contradicting the whole idea of "a better gradient estimation with DDL compared to AM". Elaborations needed.
	- The statement "... taking sub-windows of the full signal and performing SGD ..." is not precise. It depends whether you define a global loss function over the whole image or local for each patch.
	- The authors refer to the dictionary learning problem as non-convex. However, a more accurate statement is "bi-convex".

- How can you guarantee that invertibility of D^TD on the support stays along the whole iterations of alternating minimization?

- In dictionary learning, you usually learn the dictionary for a finite set of examples. However, (1) does not reflect this and aims to learn D for only one example y.

- The proof of proposition 2.2, the authors should clearly cite and highlight that Proposition 2.2 is a result from Ablin et al., 2020.

- How is lambda chosen to guarantee dictionary recovery in the numerical examples given the data generation of y = Dx? Given the overcomplete dictionary, very low lambda may result in false positive in support recovery which will affect the dictionary recovery result. The value of lambda is indeed crucial at the presence of noise to avoid noise overfitting.

- Experiments: 
	- The paper has skipped a lot of experimental details in the main text and included only in the supp. Here are some that is recommended to be included in the main. How many iterations is "full"? At what iteration of the dictionary learning, Figure 1, 2, 3, 4 are based on (i.e., at dictionary initialization, after convergence, etc.)? Please elaborate what DL-Oracle is? How do you compute the optimal loss?
	- Why the dictionary error in Figure 4 (right) does not go to zero, and if their theory and analysis can explain that.
	- How to you define D of interest used in S(C)?
	- In Figure 5, do the authors train with various N values or train with the max N and look into the denoising performance given the code estimate at iteration n (i.e., Dx_n)?
	- What is the PSNR of the noisy image?
	- Intuitively, you may expect to find a trend in performance as you move from 100 batch size (which is not very low) to full batch. However, Figure 5 does not show such trend. Is there any insight for this?


- Other comments:
	- In the introduction, it is missed to mentioned that n  >> m.
	- "we propose to study the correlation between the gradients ...". It is not clear how we can show dictionary recovery given such comparison.
	- In (6), does y entails the entire data? Is there a notion of # of examples?
	- The change of y-axis from Figure 4 to 5 is confusing. Recommend to use the same x-axis on Figure 5 (left) as in Figure 4.
	- What is the SNR for when the noise variance is 0.1?
	- What are the properties of the dictionary and code, in addition to lambda, that can make support selection faster?
	- Recommend to differentiate between L in proposition 2.2 and 2.3 which are different.

- There are various typos and change of notation:
	- What is matrix A? there are numerous places that we see AD.
	- D has size of m x n but also n x L.
	- S is used once for the support and another time as a matric for dictionary error.
	- In the proof of Proposition 2.1, argmin must be over D \in C not D in general. In addition, z must have dimension m not L.

[1] B. Tolooshams and D. Ba, "PUDLE: implicit acceleration of dictionary learning by backpropagations", 2021.

[2] N. Chatterji and P. Bartlett, "Alternating minimization for dictionary learning: Local convergence guarantees", 2017.


------------
after discussion

See discussion on "Revision of the paper" comment initiated by the author's for my updated review. I hope the authors find my overall comments and the discussion useful in improving their manuscript. My main concern had been in relation to prior work and its novelty. The authors have addressed this with proper citation and additional explanation in the intro. I have updated my rating accordingly, increased the technical novelty to 3 (given the focus of the paper on the regime before support identification), however still kept the empirical novelty to 2.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper contains some new results compared to prior works (stability analysis of the Jacobian and limited-depth backprop). However, the papers lacks novelty given the works from Ablin et al., 2020, Tolooshams et al., 2020, and [1] (see above for details). Given the current formulation, results, and version of the paper, the paper's contributions do not distinguish itself from prior works. Hence, I do not recommend an acceptance of this paper. I hope that authors find my comments helpful and can address my concerns detailed in the review.

------------
after discussion

The authors explained how their work with focus on instability of the Jacobian and analysis prior to support identification distinguishes itself from prior studies. Given this and the additional citations, I have increased my score.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

Summary Of The Paper:
The authors theoretically study the performance of dictionary learning using "unrolling" based methods. As opposed to alternating minimization (AM) which switches back and forth between dictionary estimation and sparse recovery, the paper writes down the target dictionary as the solution to a bi-level optimization, where the "inner" optimization is approximated by unrolling with N steps. The main contribution is an approach (along with careful analysis) of computing the subgradient for the outer optimization, and experiments to show that this method works on synthetic and real datasets.

Main Review:
Pros:
- The topic is important and timely.

- The significance of the proposed method (and analysis), and overall relative contributions to the state of the art, is somewhat unclear.
- Theoretical analysis of dictionary learning is a well-studied problem (with all kinds of running time and sample complexity guarantees).
It will be helpful to precisely position the quantitative guarantees (Theorem 2.4, Prop 2.6) in the literature whether there is any particular benefit or drawback of computing the (outer) sub-gradient over, say, AM or other techniques.
- Backpropagation through the solution of sparse recovery has been already done before (by Bertrand et al, ICML 2020), so a clear comparison with this previous approach (and discussions of pros vs cons of backpropagation through unrolling) will be beneficial.
- The experimental results in Figs 3/4 don't really show significant improvement over AM. It may be helpful to illustrate what lessons can be learned here. 
- Since the MEG application addresses convolutional DL, it may be helpful to address whether variations of the analysis approach is applicable to the convolutional case.

reviews.text:
Summary
This paper presents a careful theoretical and empirical study of dictionary learning trained via unrolling the inner sparse recovery steps and backpropagating to compute an outer sub-gradient in a bi-level formulation. The manuscript is clearly written, timely, and supported by solid experiments on both synthetic and real datasets. While I found the analysis and execution strong, the paper would benefit from clearer positioning relative to existing work and a more explicit articulation of when unrolling/backpropagation provides advantages over classical alternating minimization (AM). Overall, I assess the paper as marginally above the acceptance threshold.

Strengths
- The topic is important and timely, as unrolling-based training continues to be widely adopted for inverse problems.
- The theoretical analysis of computing the outer sub-gradient by backpropagating through unrolled sparse recovery is careful and technically competent.
- The paper is well organized and well written, making the main ideas and results accessible.
- The experimental section is thorough and well executed, covering both synthetic and real datasets and demonstrating a thoughtful evaluation protocol.

Weaknesses and points needing clarification
- The novelty and significance relative to the state of the art are not positioned as clearly as they could be. Existing dictionary learning theory is extensive; it remains unclear where the presented quantitative guarantees provide a distinct advance.
- The placement of Theorem 2.4 and Proposition 2.6 within the literature is insufficiently precise. Their quantitative guarantees should be explicitly contrasted with prior work in dictionary learning theory.
- Prior art on differentiating through sparse recovery (e.g., Bertrand et al., ICML 2020) is highly relevant. A direct comparison—conceptual and technical—is missing, leaving it unclear what is new or strictly stronger here.
- The experimental results in Figures 3 and 4 show limited improvements over AM. Without a clearer interpretation, the practical benefits of unrolling-based training remain ambiguous.
- It is not clear whether the theoretical analysis extends to the convolutional setting used in the MEG application. This gap makes it difficult to assess the implications of the theory for the showcased real-data use case.
- The pros and cons of computing the outer sub-gradient via unrolling compared to AM (or other techniques) are not articulated clearly enough.

Suggestions for improvement
- Precisely situate the quantitative guarantees of Theorem 2.4 and Proposition 2.6 within the existing dictionary learning literature. Spell out what is improved, what is comparable, and any trade-offs relative to established results. In the same spirit, discuss the benefits and drawbacks of using unrolled backpropagation to obtain the outer sub-gradient versus AM or related methods.
- Provide a clear, direct comparison to Bertrand et al. (ICML 2020). Discuss what aspects of backpropagation through sparse recovery are shared, what differs (assumptions, stability, convergence guarantees, exactness of differentiation, required regularity), and the practical implications of these differences.
- Clarify the main takeaways from Figures 3 and 4. Given the limited gains over AM, explain what practitioners should learn: are there regimes (e.g., data/model mismatch, limited iterations N, initialization sensitivity) where unrolling is preferable, or are the methods roughly interchangeable in practice?
- Explicitly address whether and how the theoretical analysis applies to convolutional dictionary learning as used in the MEG application. If the extension is straightforward, sketch the argument and assumptions; if not, delineate the obstacles and the extent to which the empirical findings can be justified theoretically.
- More generally, articulate where unrolling-style training is expected to be better or worse than current state of the art approaches. A concise discussion highlighting conditions favoring each method would substantially strengthen the paper’s impact.

Overall assessment
The paper is strong in clarity, rigor, and experimental design, and it addresses a timely problem. However, the contribution would be significantly clearer with better positioning relative to prior theory and a more explicit articulation of practical advantages over AM, especially in light of the results in Figures 3/4 and the convolutional MEG setting. With these clarifications, the work would make a solid contribution. I recommend a rating of 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The authors investigate the asymptotic behavior of unrolling applied to dictionary learning. The applicability of unrolling to dictionary learning results from the circumstance that dictionary learning can be reformulated in terms of bilevel optimization, where the lower-level (or inner) problem is a sparse coding problem (in the LASSO case considered here). Unrolling means to replace the argmin in the lower-level problem with the $N$-th iterate of a suitable optimization algorithm. Gradients of the upper-level loss can then be computed by means of backpropagation through algorithmic iterates. The authors study the behavior of resulting gradients depending on $N$ and draw a comparison with alternating minimization. They find that unrolling constitutes a scalable alternative to alternating minimization, where unrolling a relatively small number of iterations or using truncated backpropagation is favorable to ensure stable approximate gradients.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors investigate the asymptotic behavior of unrolling applied to dictionary learning. The applicability of unrolling to dictionary learning results from the circumstance that dictionary learning can be reformulated in terms of bilevel optimization, where the lower-level (or inner) problem is a sparse coding problem (in the LASSO case considered here). Unrolling means to replace the argmin in the lower-level problem with the $N$-th iterate of a suitable optimization algorithm. Gradients of the upper-level loss can then be computed by means of backpropagation through algorithmic iterates. The authors study the behavior of resulting gradients depending on $N$ and draw a comparison with alternating minimization. They find that unrolling constitutes a scalable alternative to alternating minimization, where unrolling a relatively small number of iterations or using truncated backpropagation is favorable to ensure stable approximate gradients.

Main Review:
From my point of view, this paper is well-written and the presented analysis is interesting in the context of a recently growing body of work on unrolling. It provides a rigorous theoretical investigation and justification of practically observable phenomena.

I only have one minor point which can hopefully be clarified. Maybe the authors can comment on the role of $L$ in Proposition 2.2. Is it correct that $L$ is at the same time the row dimension of $D$ and a constant to estimate convergence speed?

Summary Of The Review:
I think that this paper should be accepted. As far as I can see, the presented results are relevant and correct. However, I did not check the proofs in the last detail.

Rating:
8: accept, good paper
reviews.text:
Summary
This paper presents a rigorous theoretical study of unrolling for dictionary learning, cast as a bilevel optimization problem with a LASSO inner objective. The authors replace the inner argmin with the N-th iterate of an optimization algorithm and compute gradients of the upper-level loss by backpropagating through these iterates. The analysis characterizes how the resulting gradients behave as a function of N and systematically compares this approach to alternating minimization. A key conclusion is that unrolling offers a scalable alternative to alternating minimization, and that using a small number of unrolled iterations—or truncated backpropagation—produces stable approximate gradients. The results appear relevant to current practice and, to the best of my reading, correct.

Strengths
- The manuscript is well written and easy to follow.
- The topic is timely and interesting within the unrolling literature, addressing a widely used but not fully understood methodology.
- The theoretical investigation is rigorous and provides justification for phenomena frequently observed in practice.
- The work convincingly identifies unrolling as a scalable alternative to alternating minimization in dictionary learning.
- The findings highlight that small N or truncated backpropagation yields stable approximate gradients, aligning with practitioners’ heuristics.
- Overall, the contributions seem both relevant and technically sound.

Weaknesses
- There is a minor ambiguity regarding the role of L in Proposition 2.2: L appears to denote both the row dimension of the dictionary D and a constant related to convergence speed, which could lead to confusion.
- I did not verify every proof in complete detail, though the arguments appear coherent.

Suggestions for Revision
- Please clarify the notation L in Proposition 2.2. If L denotes both the row dimension of D and a convergence-related constant, consider disambiguating with distinct symbols.
- Explicitly define all uses of L at first mention and in Proposition 2.2 to avoid confusion, especially in the discussion of convergence speed.

Decision
8: accept, good paper. The paper makes a solid, rigorous contribution to the theory of unrolling for dictionary learning and provides insights that are both practically meaningful and theoretically grounded. The minor notational clarification suggested above can be addressed in the final version.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper present an efficient unrolling scheme for finding an approximate solution to the optimization problem arising from sparsity-based dictionary learning. Some asymptotically analysis on its gradient is presented and the method is also tested on analyzing MEG signal.
reviews.main_review:
*Strong points*

1. The technical writing of the paper is fairly well. The derivation can be followed without too much effort.

2. The paper presented an quite detailed discussion on unrolling scheme related to deep dictionary learning (DDL) which can be interested to the people working on DDL.

*Weak points*

1.  The applicability of the presented unrolling scheme is rather limited. It is only applicable to the problem motivated from a convex formulation of sparse coding, while a majority of the dictionary learning is using non-convex sparsity-regularization term such as -norm.

2. Its advantage over existing optimization methods e.g. K-SVD, proximal alternating method, and proximal alternating linearized method (PALM) for dictionary learning, is neither justified in theoretical analysis nor in experimental evaluation.

3. Experimental evaluation is limited with important missing information.

*Comments and Questions*


1. The hard part in dictionary learning is about the efficient treatment of non-convex constraints such as orthogonality or normalization. Are these treated using Lagrangian multiplier? if one want to call the proposed scheme for optimization model of dictionary learning

2. In Proposition 2.2 and 2.3. The results says there exist  such that the conclusion holds true. It seems that  is the cardinality of dictionary. Is this a typo, or they are the same.

3. While the proposed scheme is applicable to a specific learning scheme for MEG signal, it is not convincing enough to claim it is computationally efficient. The scale of such a problem is well handled by the traditional methods such as K-SVD, PAML.

4. In general, dictionary learning is not well-defined without additional constraints on the dictionary atoms, there is no mention of such constraints in the experiments.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Numerical scheme for solving the non-convex problem related to dictionary learning has been extensively studied. For example, proximal alternating method and proximal alternating linearized method. The hard part indeed lies in efficient and effective estimation of dictionary with non-convex constraints. The proposed unrolling scheme does not address the true bottleneck of the problem. The asymptotic analysis presented indeed are rather the application of existing available techniques. Numerical experiments on MEG signals are also too limited to show its practical advantage
reviews.correctness: 2: Several of the paper’s claims are incorrect or not well-supported.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 1: The contributions are neither significant nor novel.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper present an efficient unrolling scheme for finding an approximate solution to the optimization problem arising from sparsity-based dictionary learning. Some asymptotically analysis on its gradient is presented and the method is also tested on analyzing MEG signal.

Main Review:
*Strong points*

1. The technical writing of the paper is fairly well. The derivation can be followed without too much effort.

2. The paper presented an quite detailed discussion on unrolling scheme related to deep dictionary learning (DDL) which can be interested to the people working on DDL.

*Weak points*

1.  The applicability of the presented unrolling scheme is rather limited. It is only applicable to the problem motivated from a convex formulation of sparse coding, while a majority of the dictionary learning is using non-convex sparsity-regularization term such as -norm.

2. Its advantage over existing optimization methods e.g. K-SVD, proximal alternating method, and proximal alternating linearized method (PALM) for dictionary learning, is neither justified in theoretical analysis nor in experimental evaluation.

3. Experimental evaluation is limited with important missing information.

*Comments and Questions*


1. The hard part in dictionary learning is about the efficient treatment of non-convex constraints such as orthogonality or normalization. Are these treated using Lagrangian multiplier? if one want to call the proposed scheme for optimization model of dictionary learning

2. In Proposition 2.2 and 2.3. The results says there exist  such that the conclusion holds true. It seems that  is the cardinality of dictionary. Is this a typo, or they are the same.

3. While the proposed scheme is applicable to a specific learning scheme for MEG signal, it is not convincing enough to claim it is computationally efficient. The scale of such a problem is well handled by the traditional methods such as K-SVD, PAML.

4. In general, dictionary learning is not well-defined without additional constraints on the dictionary atoms, there is no mention of such constraints in the experiments.

Summary Of The Review:
Numerical scheme for solving the non-convex problem related to dictionary learning has been extensively studied. For example, proximal alternating method and proximal alternating linearized method. The hard part indeed lies in efficient and effective estimation of dictionary with non-convex constraints. The proposed unrolling scheme does not address the true bottleneck of the problem. The asymptotic analysis presented indeed are rather the application of existing available techniques. Numerical experiments on MEG signals are also too limited to show its practical advantage

Rating:
3: reject, not good enough
reviews.text:
Summary
The paper presents an unrolling-based approach for deep dictionary learning focused on convex sparse-coding formulations. The manuscript is clearly written, and the derivations are easy to follow. The discussion around unrolling schemes is detailed and will likely be of interest to researchers working on deep dictionary learning.

Strengths
- The technical writing is clear, and the mathematical developments are straightforward to follow.
- The paper offers a thorough discussion of unrolling strategies in the context of deep dictionary learning that could be useful for the community.

Weaknesses
- The proposed approach is restricted to convex sparse-coding setups. Many practical dictionary learning applications rely on non-convex sparsity regularizers (e.g., l0-like penalties), which are not covered here.
- The work does not provide a compelling theoretical or empirical case for advantages over established baselines such as K-SVD, proximal alternating methods, or PALM. As a result, the contribution remains incremental and insufficiently justified.
- The experimental evaluation is limited and omits important details. It does not substantiate claims of practical efficiency.
- The core practical challenge of handling non-convex constraints on dictionary atoms (e.g., orthogonality or normalization) is not adequately addressed. It is unclear how such constraints are enforced in practice or whether any mechanism such as Lagrangian multipliers is employed.
- The claim of computational efficiency, including on MEG-scale data, is unconvincing. The reported problem sizes appear to be already manageable by traditional methods like K-SVD or PALM.
- The experiments do not clearly state or enforce necessary constraints on the dictionary atoms.
- There are notation issues in Propositions 2.2 and 2.3: a symbol appears to be used both for an existential quantity and for the dictionary cardinality, leading to ambiguity.

Suggestions for Improvement
- Explicitly address non-convex constraints on dictionary atoms (e.g., orthogonality, normalization). Clarify whether and how such constraints are enforced, for example via Lagrangian multipliers or other mechanisms.
- Provide rigorous theoretical and empirical comparisons against strong baselines (K-SVD, proximal alternating methods, PALM) to substantiate any claimed advantages.
- Include the missing experimental details and expand the evaluation to demonstrate practical efficiency at relevant scales.
- Clearly state and enforce dictionary constraints in the experimental setup, and report how these constraints affect performance and convergence.
- Clarify the notation in Propositions 2.2 and 2.3 to resolve the ambiguity regarding the symbol that also denotes the dictionary cardinality.
- Demonstrate computational scalability and runtime benefits on problem sizes where traditional methods are known to struggle, rather than on scales already well handled.

Overall Assessment
While the paper is well written and the unrolling discussion is informative, the current scope is limited to convex formulations and does not tackle key non-convex aspects central to modern dictionary learning. The lack of strong theoretical or empirical evidence against established methods, together with limited experiments and unresolved notation issues, prevents me from being convinced of the practical utility and efficiency of the approach.

Decision: 3 — reject, not good enough.

Summary Of The Paper:
This paper present an efficient unrolling scheme for finding an approximate solution to the optimization problem arising from sparsity-based dictionary learning. Some asymptotically analysis on its gradient is presented and the method is also tested on analyzing MEG signal.

Main Review:
*Strong points*

1. The technical writing of the paper is fairly well. The derivation can be followed without too much effort.

2. The paper presented an quite detailed discussion on unrolling scheme related to deep dictionary learning (DDL) which can be interested to the people working on DDL.

*Weak points*

1.  The applicability of the presented unrolling scheme is rather limited. It is only applicable to the problem motivated from a convex formulation of sparse coding, while a majority of the dictionary learning is using non-convex sparsity-regularization term such as -norm.

2. Its advantage over existing optimization methods e.g. K-SVD, proximal alternating method, and proximal alternating linearized method (PALM) for dictionary learning, is neither justified in theoretical analysis nor in experimental evaluation.

3. Experimental evaluation is limited with important missing information.

4. In general, dictionary learning is not well-defined without additional constraints on the dictionary atoms, there is no mention of such constraints in the experiments.

The asymptotic analysis presented indeed are rather the application of existing available techniques. Numerical experiments on MEG signals are also too limited to show its practical advantage

Rating:
3: reject, not good enough

title: HyperDQN: A Randomized Exploration Method for Deep Reinforcement Learning

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper introduces HyperDQN, a novel exploration technique for deep reinforcement learning, which extends the ideas from an existing method, namely RLSVI, to the deep RL settings. HyperDQN optimizes a linear hypermodel, which is used to compute state-action values as a function of extracted features, to work as a module that converges to a sample from the posterior distribution of the Q-value function. The authors further present a specific cost function to jointly optimize the hypermodel and the feature extractor that can efficiently address the limitation of the RLSVI approach in deep RL scenarios. Empirical study demonstrates significant improvement compared to the competitors on several benchmarks.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths: 
+ The outcome of the paper which adapts the ideas from RLSVI to deep reinforcement learning settings is significant and practical. 
+ The paper is very well written, clear, and easy to understand. The supplementary materials provide most of the information needed for acquiring a better understanding of the paper. 
+ The authors provide theoretical analysis and proofs to support their main ideas and design choices. 
+ The experimental results show superior performance of HyperDQN compared to state-of-the-art algorithms. 
+ The results are reproducible as the implementation and experimental details are provided in the paper.

Weaknesses: 
- The proposed approach provides incremental contributions w.r.t. the existing ideas from the previous work.
- The contributions are very specific to a class of problems and algorithms.

Comments: 
- The main ideas of the paper has been previously introduced in the literature and is further extended to the deep RL scenarios which make the presented contributions only somewhat novel. 
- The paper can be improved by providing some information and\or intuition about the generalizability of the proposed approach to the other domains. Additionally, discussions about the challenges in extending the proposed idea to the areas such as continuous control, offline RL, etc. would be highly appreciated. 
- Could you elaborate about the challenges and potential problems that could arise when jointly optimizing the hypermodel and the feature extractor? One of the main contributions of the paper is addressing the major problems with RLSVI which are restricting its applications in deep RL. While addressing these limitations regarding computational costs of RLSVI is discussed extensively, providing a specific cost function to jointly optimize the feature extractor and the hypermodel might lead to stability and convergence problems. Hence, it would be beneficial if the authors provide further insights into this matter.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Overall, the paper introduces a novel approach for randomized exploration in deep RL, is well written, and shows strong promise in terms of performance compared to other state-of-the-art methods. Additionally, it includes valuable theoretical and experimental discussions that provide further support for their contributions. I thus vote for an accept.
reviews.correctness: 4: All of the claims and statements are well-supported and correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper introduces HyperDQN, a novel exploration technique for deep reinforcement learning, which extends the ideas from an existing method, namely RLSVI, to the deep RL settings. HyperDQN optimizes a linear hypermodel, which is used to compute state-action values as a function of extracted features, to work as a module that converges to a sample from the posterior distribution of the Q-value function. The authors further present a specific cost function to jointly optimize the hypermodel and the feature extractor that can efficiently address the limitation of the RLSVI approach in deep RL scenarios. Empirical study demonstrates significant improvement compared to the competitors on several benchmarks.

Main Review:
Strengths: 
+ The outcome of the paper which adapts the ideas from RLSVI to deep reinforcement learning settings is significant and practical. 
+ The paper is very well written, clear, and easy to understand. The supplementary materials provide most of the information needed for acquiring a better understanding of the paper. 
+ The authors provide theoretical analysis and proofs to support their main ideas and design choices. 
+ The experimental results show superior performance of HyperDQN compared to state-of-the-art algorithms. 
+ The results are reproducible as the implementation and experimental details are provided in the paper.

Weaknesses: 
- The proposed approach provides incremental contributions w.r.t. the existing ideas from the previous work.
- The contributions are very specific to a class of problems and algorithms.

Comments: 
- The main ideas of the paper has been previously introduced in the literature and is further extended to the deep RL scenarios which make the presented contributions only somewhat novel. 
- The paper can be improved by providing some information and\or intuition about the generalizability of the proposed approach to the other domains. Additionally, discussions about the challenges in extending the proposed idea to the areas such as continuous control, offline RL, etc. would be highly appreciated. 
- Could you elaborate about the challenges and potential problems that could arise when jointly optimizing the hypermodel and the feature extractor? One of the main contributions of the paper is addressing the major problems with RLSVI which are restricting its applications in deep RL. While addressing these limitations regarding computational costs of RLSVI is discussed extensively, providing a specific cost function to jointly optimize the feature extractor and the hypermodel might lead to stability and convergence problems. Hence, it would be beneficial if the authors provide further insights into this matter.

Summary Of The Review:
Overall, the paper introduces a novel approach for randomized exploration in deep RL, is well written, and shows strong promise in terms of performance compared to other state-of-the-art methods. Additionally, it includes valuable theoretical and experimental discussions that provide further support for their contributions. I thus vote for an accept.

Rating:
8: accept, good paper
reviews.text:
Summary
The paper presents HyperDQN, an extension of randomized least-squares value iteration (RLSVI) to deep reinforcement learning. The method uses a linear hypermodel to approximate posterior samples of the Q-value function and introduces a specific cost function to jointly optimize the hypermodel and a feature extractor. The approach is supported by theoretical analysis and proofs, and the empirical results show clear gains over strong baselines. The paper is clearly written, with comprehensive supplementary materials and sufficient implementation details to enable reproducibility. While effective and practically relevant, the contribution is somewhat incremental and targeted to a specific class of problems.

Strengths
- Adapts core RLSVI ideas to deep RL in a way that is both meaningful and practically useful.
- Clear, well-structured presentation with thorough supplementary materials.
- Solid theoretical support for the main ideas and design choices.
- Strong empirical performance relative to state-of-the-art methods across benchmarks.
- Reproducible results, supported by released code and detailed experimental settings.

Weaknesses
- The novelty is incremental relative to prior work, extending known ideas rather than introducing a fundamentally new paradigm.
- The contribution is tailored to a particular class of problems and algorithms, limiting its immediate breadth.
- The conceptual advance beyond previously introduced ideas is modest.

Suggestions for improvement and clarification
- Generalizability: Provide more intuition and discussion about how the hypermodel and joint objective might transfer to other domains. In particular, outline the expected behavior and required modifications for:
  - Continuous control settings
  - Offline RL
- Joint optimization: Elaborate on potential challenges that arise from jointly optimizing the hypermodel and the feature extractor. Specifically:
  - Discuss possible stability and convergence issues associated with the joint objective.
  - Describe any observed failure modes, mitigations, or diagnostics that practitioners should consider.

Overall assessment
This is a well-executed, clearly written paper with strong theoretical grounding and compelling empirical results. Despite the incremental nature and domain specificity of the contribution, its practical significance and demonstrated performance justify acceptance.

Recommendation: 8 — accept, good paper

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposes a new posterior sampling-based exploration method, called *HyperDQN*, for deep reinforcement learning. Conceptually, *HyperDQN* is modified based on randomized least-square value iteration (RLSVI) and it resolves the limitations of RLSVI on feature engineering and high computational complexity. Technically, *HyperDQN* extends the Hypermodel technique, which is only used for bandit learning in its original paper. The performance of *HyperDQN* is evaluated on Atari 2600 games, SuperMarioBros and deep sea environment.
reviews.main_review:
### Strengths
Overall, I think it is a good trial to extend the idea of Hypermodel to the realm of deep reinforcement learning. Compared with BootDQN, *HyperDQN* will require much less computational power. Meanwhile, the paper provides a large amount of experiment results to show the superior performance of *HyperDQN*. Meanwhile, the paper is also written in a clear logical flow.

### Weaknesses
I apologize if I understand incorrectly. One of my major concern is on the novelty. Given the existence of Hypermodel, the *HyperDQN* seems to be a very straightforward application of it on deep reinforcement learning without more insights. Meanwhile, from my perspective, the Theorem 1 simply re-writes the motivation of Hypermodel in a mathematical way, using linear hypermodel to approximate the posterior distribution, which is already explained in [1].

Another concern I have is about the performance of *HyperDQN*. Specifically, although the experiments compare *HyperDQN* with BootDQN, it seems that this paper uses the version of BootDQN based on [2]. However, the version of BootDQN in [3] has much better performance than that in [2]. Further, it seems the BootDQN in [3] did not use $\epsilon$-greedy. Have you compred *HyperDQN* with the BootDQN in [3]?

**Honestly, I'm not very experienced in running experiments, so I apologize if the above comments on experiments contain some flaws.**

### Questions
- Does *HyperDQN* contain other insights that are not presented in [1]?
- Did the comparison experiments use BootDQN in [2] or [3]?
- Did you compare *HyperDQN* and BootDQN on deep sea?
- When you ran BootDQN, did you use number of ensembles significantly less than the one used in [3]?

### Suggestions on Writing
- In appendix C, it seems $b$ should be replaced by $\nu_b$ in equation C.6.
- At the bottom of page 4, it says $\theta_{\mathrm{predict}}\in\mathbb{R}^{d\times N_a}$, but in the previous paragraph, it also says $\theta_{\mathrm{predict}}\in\mathbb{R}^d$. This can potentially be confusing.
- In Theorem 1, it is may be better to replace "then $\theta:=\dots$ with" by "then $\theta:=\dots$ satisfies"

```
[1] Vikranth Dwaracherla, Xiuyuan Lu, Morteza Ibrahimi, Ian Osband, Zheng Wen, and Benjamin Van Roy. Hypermodels for exploration. In Proceedings of the 8th International Conference on Learning Representations, 2020.

[2] Ian Osband, Charles Blundell, Alexander Pritzel, and Benjamin Van Roy. Deep exploration via bootstrapped DQN. In Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 29, pp. 4026–4034, 2016a.

[3] Ian Osband, John Aslanides, and Albin Cassirer. Randomized prior functions for deep reinforcement learning. In Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 31, pp. 8626–8638, 2018.
```

However, the formulation of *HyperDQN* is conceptually not novel and its performance compared to other methods is also questionable.

This paper proposes a new posterior sampling-based exploration method, called *HyperDQN*, for deep reinforcement learning. ### Strengths

Meanwhile, the paper provides a large amount of experiment results to show the superior performance of *HyperDQN*. Meanwhile, the paper is also written in a clear logical flow.

### Weaknesses
I apologize if I understand incorrectly. One of my major concern is on the novelty. Given the existence of Hypermodel, the *HyperDQN* seems to be a very straightforward application of it on deep reinforcement learning without more insights. Meanwhile, from my perspective, the Theorem 1 simply re-writes the motivation of Hypermodel in a mathematical way, using linear hypermodel to approximate the posterior distribution, which is already explained in [1].

Another concern I have is about the performance of *HyperDQN*. Have you compred *HyperDQN* with the BootDQN in [3]?

**Honestly, I'm not very experienced in running experiments, so I apologize if the above comments on experiments contain some flaws.**

### Suggestions on Writing

- At the bottom of page 4, it says $\theta_{\mathrm{predict}}\in\mathbb{R}^{d\times N_a}$, but in the previous paragraph, it also says $\theta_{\mathrm{predict}}\in\mathbb{R}^d$. This can potentially be confusing.

```
[1] Vikranth Dwaracherla, Xiuyuan Lu, Morteza Ibrahimi, Ian Osband, Zheng Wen, and Benjamin Van Roy. Hypermodels for exploration. In Proceedings of the 8th International Conference on Learning Representations, 2020.

[2] Ian Osband, Charles Blundell, Alexander Pritzel, and Benjamin Van Roy. Deep exploration via bootstrapped DQN. In Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 29, pp. 4026–4034, 2016a.
```

Summary Of The Review:
The paper proposes a computationally lighter method *HperDQN* for exploration in deep reinforcement learning and shows its superior performance in experiments. However, the formulation of *HyperDQN* is conceptually not novel and its performance compared to other methods is also questionable.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary
The paper proposes HyperDQN, a practical extension of hypermodels to deep reinforcement learning. It is computationally lighter than Bootstrapped DQN (BootDQN) and is presented clearly, with extensive experiments that often show strong performance. While I am generally positive, my enthusiasm is tempered by concerns about the marginal novelty and the fairness/completeness of the experimental comparisons, particularly with respect to stronger BootDQN variants. I recommend acceptance at the margin, contingent on clarifications and strengthened baselines.

Strengths
- Practical extension of hypermodels to deep RL that appears easy to deploy.
- Substantial computational savings relative to BootDQN.
- Broad experimental evaluation with many cases where HyperDQN performs well.
- The paper is well written with a clear logical flow.

Weaknesses
- Limited novelty: the method largely applies the existing hypermodel idea to deep RL without clear new conceptual advances.
- Theorem 1 seems to mainly restate the hypermodel posterior-approximation motivation in mathematical form, adding little beyond what appears already explained in prior work [1].
- Experimental comparisons raise fairness concerns:
  - The paper appears to compare primarily to the BootDQN version from [2], whereas the stronger variant with randomized prior functions in [3] is the more appropriate baseline.
  - BootDQN in [3] reportedly does not use epsilon-greedy. If your BootDQN baseline does use epsilon-greedy, this could disadvantage BootDQN and complicate performance claims.
  - Without clarity on the ensemble size used for BootDQN (and whether it differs substantially from [3]), it is difficult to assess the fairness of comparisons.
- Due to these issues, the strength of the performance claims is uncertain.

Suggestions for Improvement
Conceptual and clarity:
- Clearly articulate what insights or methodological contributions HyperDQN adds beyond [1]. If the contribution is primarily practical, state this explicitly and highlight the specific practical design choices.
- Clarify the role and interpretation of Theorem 1 relative to [1].

Experimental setup and baselines:
- Explicitly state which BootDQN implementation you used (from [2] or [3]).
- Include comparisons against the stronger BootDQN with randomized prior functions [3], which reportedly performs better and does not rely on epsilon-greedy.
- State whether your BootDQN baseline used epsilon-greedy; if so, justify this choice in light of [3].
- Report the number of ensembles used for BootDQN and whether it differs from [3].
- Provide a direct comparison on the deep sea environment between HyperDQN and BootDQN to substantiate exploration efficiency claims.

Presentation and minor issues:
- Resolve the inconsistency in the dimensionality of θ_predict: it is described as both in R^{d×N_a} and in R^d on page 4.
- In Appendix C, equation C.6 should use ν_b instead of b.
- Improve the wording in Theorem 1 by replacing “then θ := … with” with “then θ := … satisfies.”

Overall Assessment
HyperDQN is a well-executed and computationally efficient application of hypermodels to deep RL with solid empirical results and clear exposition. However, the novelty is limited, and the experimental comparisons need to be aligned with the stronger BootDQN variant to fully substantiate the performance claims. With the requested clarifications and additional baselines, the paper would be considerably stronger. As it stands, I lean slightly in favor of acceptance.

Rating: 6 (marginally above the acceptance threshold)
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes a new posterior sampling-based exploration method, called *HyperDQN*, for deep reinforcement learning. Conceptually, *HyperDQN* is modified based on randomized least-square value iteration (RLSVI) and it resolves the limitations of RLSVI on feature engineering and high computational complexity. Technically, *HyperDQN* extends the Hypermodel technique, which is only used for bandit learning in its original paper. The performance of *HyperDQN* is evaluated on Atari 2600 games, SuperMarioBros and deep sea environment.

Main Review:
### Strengths
Overall, I think it is a good trial to extend the idea of Hypermodel to the realm of deep reinforcement learning. Compared with BootDQN, *HyperDQN* will require much less computational power. Meanwhile, the paper provides a large amount of experiment results to show the superior performance of *HyperDQN*. Meanwhile, the paper is also written in a clear logical flow.

### Weaknesses
I apologize if I understand incorrectly. One of my major concern is on the novelty. Given the existence of Hypermodel, the *HyperDQN* seems to be a very straightforward application of it on deep reinforcement learning without more insights. Meanwhile, from my perspective, the Theorem 1 simply re-writes the motivation of Hypermodel in a mathematical way, using linear hypermodel to approximate the posterior distribution, which is already explained in [1].

Another concern I have is about the performance of *HyperDQN*. Specifically, although the experiments compare *HyperDQN* with BootDQN, it seems that this paper uses the version of BootDQN based on [2]. However, the version of BootDQN in [3] has much better performance than that in [2]. Further, it seems the BootDQN in [3] did not use $\epsilon$-greedy. Have you compred *HyperDQN* with the BootDQN in [3]?

**Honestly, I'm not very experienced in running experiments, so I apologize if the above comments on experiments contain some flaws.**

### Questions
- Does *HyperDQN* contain other insights that are not presented in [1]?
- Did the comparison experiments use BootDQN in [2] or [3]?
- Did you compare *HyperDQN* and BootDQN on deep sea?
- When you ran BootDQN, did you use number of ensembles significantly less than the one used in [3]?

### Suggestions on Writing
- In appendix C, it seems $b$ should be replaced by $\nu_b$ in equation C.6.
- At the bottom of page 4, it says $\theta_{\mathrm{predict}}\in\mathbb{R}^{d\times N_a}$, but in the previous paragraph, it also says $\theta_{\mathrm{predict}}\in\mathbb{R}^d$. This can potentially be confusing.
- In Theorem 1, it is may be better to replace "then $\theta:=\dots$ with" by "then $\theta:=\dots$ satisfies"

```
[1] Vikranth Dwaracherla, Xiuyuan Lu, Morteza Ibrahimi, Ian Osband, Zheng Wen, and Benjamin Van Roy. Hypermodels for exploration. In Proceedings of the 8th International Conference on Learning Representations, 2020.

[2] Ian Osband, Charles Blundell, Alexander Pritzel, and Benjamin Van Roy. Deep exploration via bootstrapped DQN. In Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 29, pp. 4026–4034, 2016a.

[3] Ian Osband, John Aslanides, and Albin Cassirer. Randomized prior functions for deep reinforcement learning. In Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 31, pp. 8626–8638, 2018.
```

Summary Of The Review:
The paper proposes a computationally lighter method *HperDQN* for exploration in deep reinforcement learning and shows its superior performance in experiments. However, the formulation of *HyperDQN* is conceptually not novel and its performance compared to other methods is also questionable.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

extracted_ideas.summary: The reviewer finds HyperDQN a computationally lighter and well-presented extension of hypermodels to deep RL with strong experimental results, but raises concerns about novelty and the fairness/completeness of comparisons to stronger BootDQN variants. Overall sentiment is marginally positive with requests for clarifications and stronger baselines.
strengths: Extends hypermodel ideas to deep reinforcement learning in a practical way
Requires much less computational power than BootDQN
Provides a large amount of experimental results showing superior performance
Paper is written with a clear logical flow
weaknesses: Questionable novelty: appears to be a straightforward application of hypermodels to deep RL without substantial new insights
Theorem 1 contribution seems to restate prior hypermodel motivation in mathematical form and may already be explained in prior work [1]
Experimental comparison may be unfair or incomplete: compared against BootDQN version from [2] rather than the stronger variant in [3]
Potential mismatch in exploration strategy: BootDQN in [3] reportedly does not use epsilon-greedy, raising concerns about the setup used
Overall performance claims are questioned due to the above comparison issues
suggestions: Clarify and articulate insights in HyperDQN that go beyond those presented in [1]
Explicitly state which BootDQN implementation was used in comparisons (from [2] or [3])
Include comparisons against the BootDQN variant in [3], which reportedly has better performance and does not use epsilon-greedy
Report results on the deep sea environment comparing HyperDQN and BootDQN
Disclose the number of ensembles used for BootDQN and whether it differs significantly from [3]
Fix notation in Appendix C: replace b with ν_b in equation C.6
Resolve inconsistency in the dimensionality of θ_predict (R^{d×N_a} vs R^d) described on page 4
Improve wording in Theorem 1: replace 'then θ := … with' by 'then θ := … satisfies'
main_points: HyperDQN’s novelty is limited; it largely applies the hypermodel approach to deep RL without substantial new conceptual contributions
Theorem 1 may not add new theoretical insight beyond [1], being mainly a mathematical restatement of hypermodel posterior approximation
Experimental evaluation should compare against the stronger BootDQN with randomized prior functions [3], not only the earlier version [2]
Clarifications are needed on epsilon-greedy usage and ensemble counts for BootDQN, as these affect fairness and outcomes
A direct comparison on the deep sea benchmark would better establish exploration efficiency claims
technical_details: References to BootDQN versions: [2] (2016 NIPS) vs [3] (2018 NIPS, randomized prior functions)
Reviewer notes that BootDQN in [3] did not use epsilon-greedy; asks whether the paper’s BootDQN used epsilon-greedy
Requests confirmation whether HyperDQN vs BootDQN was compared on the deep sea environment
Asks whether the number of BootDQN ensembles used was significantly less than in [3]
Notation issue: In Appendix C, equation C.6 should use ν_b instead of b
Dimensionality inconsistency: θ_predict stated as in R^{d×N_a} and also as in R^d on page 4
Theorem 1 wording suggestion: change 'then θ := … with' to 'then θ := … satisfies'

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper presents HyperDQN as a practical algorithm of Randomized least-square value iteration (RLSVI).  HyperDQN consists of two parametric models: first is a base model that is similar to that of a DQN agent and the second a meta model that parameterizes the last layer of the Q-network as a function of a latent variable. The purpose of this architecture is to generate posterior samples using the diverse Q-value functions represented through this architecture. The paper demonstrates this approach empirically across a variety of Atari games, SuperMarioBros and DeepSea.
reviews.main_review:
The paper begins with a strong motivation towards computationally implementing RLSVI in Deep RL using a base and meta models, and also points out reasons why this is non-trivial. Also, the main differences between BootDQN and HyperDQN are also discussed, which is important because BootDQN is a highly relevant prior work in this domain. Overall the paper is well-written and has strong motivations that are relevant to the RL community at large. 

The following are some of the questions based on my understanding of the paper and would be great if the authors could clarify some of them: 

1. The meta-model in HyperDQN is considered as a linear parameterized model and there is a theoretical justification for this. What would happen to the empirical/theoretical results if the meta-model is a non-linear function of the latent variable z? I am inclined to think that the representation power for the posterior distribution would be better and thus should produce improvements overall.

2. How are the sigma_w and sigma_p parameters chosen in the objective function? Have the authors considered a baseline where an agent uses an identical artificial noise term for modifying the Q_{target} (i.e., sigma_w z^{\top} \epsilon_i ) to the one used in HyperDQN? 

3. The Atari performance curves summarized in Fig 3 is not sufficient to demonstrate the utility of the presented approach. This curve is computed over all the Atari games (some of them require no exploration and some require a complex strategy for exploration). It would be more appropriate to show how the agent performs on “hard-exploration” and “easy-exploration” games (this classification is presented in Bellemare et al. 2016) rather than present a summary across all games. 

Also, why are the agents only trained for 20M frames? It is conventional in Atari to run the agents for 200M frames (Machado et al. 2018). This makes it easier to compare the performance of HyperDQN wrto the prior methods in Atari. 

A minor comment: why do some curves in Fig 3 start at 0 and another starts at a value lower than 0? 

Bellemare, M., Srinivasan, S., Ostrovski, G., Schaul, T., Saxton, D., and Munos, R. Unifying count-based exploration and intrinsic motivation. In NeurIPS, 2016.

Machado, M. C., Bellemare, M. G., Talvitie, E., Veness, J., Hausknecht, M., & Bowling, M. (2018). Revisiting the arcade learning environment: Evaluation protocols and open problems for general agents. Journal of Artificial Intelligence Research, 61, 523-562.

4. How many random seeds were used to produce the learning curves in all the experiments (Atari, Mario and DeepSea)? It would be useful to add this information in the main text. Also, are the error bars reported in each of the presented learning curves?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Overall, I think the contribution would be important to the RL community. My main concerns with the paper is that the presented approach claims to be helpful for exploration but the empirical results are not demonstrating this: One specific way to demonstrate this would be to show and emphasize that HyperDQN is able to achieve better returns on Atari games that are classified to be hard-exploration games. This would be a more powerful argument for HyperDQN.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper presents HyperDQN as a practical algorithm of Randomized least-square value iteration (RLSVI).  HyperDQN consists of two parametric models: first is a base model that is similar to that of a DQN agent and the second a meta model that parameterizes the last layer of the Q-network as a function of a latent variable. The purpose of this architecture is to generate posterior samples using the diverse Q-value functions represented through this architecture. The paper demonstrates this approach empirically across a variety of Atari games, SuperMarioBros and DeepSea.

Main Review:
The paper begins with a strong motivation towards computationally implementing RLSVI in Deep RL using a base and meta models, and also points out reasons why this is non-trivial. Also, the main differences between BootDQN and HyperDQN are also discussed, which is important because BootDQN is a highly relevant prior work in this domain. Overall the paper is well-written and has strong motivations that are relevant to the RL community at large. 

The following are some of the questions based on my understanding of the paper and would be great if the authors could clarify some of them: 

1. The meta-model in HyperDQN is considered as a linear parameterized model and there is a theoretical justification for this. What would happen to the empirical/theoretical results if the meta-model is a non-linear function of the latent variable z? I am inclined to think that the representation power for the posterior distribution would be better and thus should produce improvements overall.

2. How are the sigma_w and sigma_p parameters chosen in the objective function? Have the authors considered a baseline where an agent uses an identical artificial noise term for modifying the Q_{target} (i.e., sigma_w z^{\top} \epsilon_i ) to the one used in HyperDQN? 

3. The Atari performance curves summarized in Fig 3 is not sufficient to demonstrate the utility of the presented approach. This curve is computed over all the Atari games (some of them require no exploration and some require a complex strategy for exploration). It would be more appropriate to show how the agent performs on “hard-exploration” and “easy-exploration” games (this classification is presented in Bellemare et al. 2016) rather than present a summary across all games. 

Also, why are the agents only trained for 20M frames? It is conventional in Atari to run the agents for 200M frames (Machado et al. 2018). This makes it easier to compare the performance of HyperDQN wrto the prior methods in Atari. 

A minor comment: why do some curves in Fig 3 start at 0 and another starts at a value lower than 0? 

Bellemare, M., Srinivasan, S., Ostrovski, G., Schaul, T., Saxton, D., and Munos, R. Unifying count-based exploration and intrinsic motivation. In NeurIPS, 2016.

Machado, M. C., Bellemare, M. G., Talvitie, E., Veness, J., Hausknecht, M., & Bowling, M. (2018). Revisiting the arcade learning environment: Evaluation protocols and open problems for general agents. Journal of Artificial Intelligence Research, 61, 523-562.

4. How many random seeds were used to produce the learning curves in all the experiments (Atari, Mario and DeepSea)? It would be useful to add this information in the main text. Also, are the error bars reported in each of the presented learning curves?

Summary Of The Review:
Overall, I think the contribution would be important to the RL community. My main concerns with the paper is that the presented approach claims to be helpful for exploration but the empirical results are not demonstrating this: One specific way to demonstrate this would be to show and emphasize that HyperDQN is able to achieve better returns on Atari games that are classified to be hard-exploration games. This would be a more powerful argument for HyperDQN.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Overall assessment
This paper presents a well-written and clearly motivated deep RL instantiation of RLSVI that combines base and meta models. The exposition is careful, the differences between HyperDQN and BootDQN are well articulated, and the contribution is relevant and potentially impactful for the RL community. However, the empirical section does not yet convincingly establish improved exploration, especially on hard-exploration Atari games. With additional analyses and clarifications, the work would be strengthened. I recommend a score of 6 (marginally above the acceptance threshold).

Strengths
- Strong motivation for a practical deep RL implementation of RLSVI with a base/meta model decomposition.
- Clear explanation of how HyperDQN differs from BootDQN and why those design choices may matter.
- Generally well written and relevant to ongoing discussions on exploration in deep RL.

Weaknesses and points needing clarification
- The current evidence for exploration gains is not compelling, particularly on hard-exploration Atari titles. Aggregated results over all games may mask differences between easy- and hard-exploration regimes.
- The use of 20M frames (rather than the conventional 200M) complicates comparison with prior work and may understate or overstate the method’s advantages.
- The choices of key objective parameters sigma_w (noise scale) and sigma_p (regularization scale) are not clearly justified or explained.
- The number of random seeds is not reported, and learning curves lack error bars, limiting confidence in the reported trends.
- Some curves in Figure 3 begin below zero without explanation, which raises questions about evaluation or normalization.
- The meta-model is linear in z; potential benefits of a non-linear meta-model are not explored.
- An informative baseline—modifying the Q_target with an identical artificial noise term (e.g., sigma_w z^T ε_i)—is absent, making it harder to attribute gains to the proposed mechanism.

Suggestions for improvement
- Report results separately on hard- and easy-exploration Atari games, for example following the taxonomy of Bellemare et al. (2016), to directly assess exploration benefits where they matter most.
- Either train for 200M frames in Atari (as is standard per Machado et al., 2018) or provide a strong justification for the 20M-frame budget, and align comparisons with prior work using conventional evaluation protocols.
- Investigate a non-linear meta-model for z and report both theoretical implications (if any) and empirical performance differences relative to the current linear choice.
- Clarify and justify how sigma_w and sigma_p are selected; ideally include an ablation or sensitivity study to show robustness and the role each parameter plays.
- Include a baseline that injects the same artificial noise into the target (sigma_w z^T ε_i) to help disentangle the benefits of HyperDQN from simpler noise-injection strategies.
- State the number of random seeds used across all experiments and include appropriate error bars in learning curves.
- Explain why some learning curves in Figure 3 start below zero (e.g., scoring conventions, preprocessing, or evaluation protocol), and ensure consistency across plots.

Conclusion
The paper’s motivation, clarity, and relevance are strong, and the core idea is promising. The current empirical setup and reporting, however, do not yet make a definitive case for improved exploration, particularly on hard-exploration games. Addressing the evaluation protocol, hyperparameter justification, baselines, and reporting issues would substantially strengthen the contribution. I recommend acceptance at 6 (marginally above the threshold), contingent on the authors’ ability to incorporate the suggested analyses and clarifications.
