reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This paper provides a practical guide on how to train an NN-based controller for locomotion tasks with robustness and efficiency. 

The tasks are categorized into motion primitives including running, jumping, and crawling, each with a manually designed objective function. Considering the periodic nature of locomotion, the author proposed to use sine activation layers and periodic signals in different phases as input. The experiments show that a larger batch size can lead to faster convergence. Compared to SGD, this paper points out that Adam is better for the tasks. The supplementary video presents that the trained networks enable real-time control of complex skills.
reviews.main_review:
Based on existing differentiable physics engines, this paper further gives empirical recipes on how to use the physics gradients to train powerful controllers, which can help the community to develop techniques related to differentiable physics. 

This method divides long-horizon tasks into smaller periods with different objectives. To avoid sparse and non-smooth rewards, objective functions are manually designed for subtasks. Periodic control input is also added to the input to actuate the controller. When training the network, the authors use batching, regularization terms, and sine activation layer to accelerate and stabilize the convergence.

When reading this paper, I have several concerns,
1. In Equation 5, how is $g_v(n)$ subtracted from $A(t)$? Do they have the same dimension? $g_v(n)$ as a velocity should have a dimension of 3 (or 6 if rotation is included). The dim of  $A(t)$ should be the DOF of actuation. Maybe you mean $||g_v(n)||$. Why only target velocity is considered, how about the height $g_h(n)$? Why “periodic control signal may induce unwanted high-frequency noises”？
2. The paper mentions "a modified Adam optimizer". I did not find how Adam is modified.
3. In Figure 4, what is the definition of one iteration? Is it one step of network update or one step of simulation?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This paper gives us a good example of how to train a network using differentiable physics. Their video of interactively controlling 2D and 3D robots looks interesting. Although some of the techniques like staging, batching, since activation, and periodic signals are intuitive and existing tricks, it is good to see that a combination of them can successfully train a controller.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper provides a practical guide on how to train an NN-based controller for locomotion tasks with robustness and efficiency. 

The tasks are categorized into motion primitives including running, jumping, and crawling, each with a manually designed objective function. Considering the periodic nature of locomotion, the author proposed to use sine activation layers and periodic signals in different phases as input. The experiments show that a larger batch size can lead to faster convergence. Compared to SGD, this paper points out that Adam is better for the tasks. The supplementary video presents that the trained networks enable real-time control of complex skills.

Main Review:
Based on existing differentiable physics engines, this paper further gives empirical recipes on how to use the physics gradients to train powerful controllers, which can help the community to develop techniques related to differentiable physics. 

This method divides long-horizon tasks into smaller periods with different objectives. To avoid sparse and non-smooth rewards, objective functions are manually designed for subtasks. Periodic control input is also added to the input to actuate the controller. When training the network, the authors use batching, regularization terms, and sine activation layer to accelerate and stabilize the convergence.

When reading this paper, I have several concerns,
1. In Equation 5, how is $g_v(n)$ subtracted from $A(t)$? Do they have the same dimension? $g_v(n)$ as a velocity should have a dimension of 3 (or 6 if rotation is included). The dim of  $A(t)$ should be the DOF of actuation. Maybe you mean $||g_v(n)||$. Why only target velocity is considered, how about the height $g_h(n)$? Why “periodic control signal may induce unwanted high-frequency noises”？
2. The paper mentions "a modified Adam optimizer". I did not find how Adam is modified.
3. In Figure 4, what is the definition of one iteration? Is it one step of network update or one step of simulation?

Summary Of The Review:
This paper gives us a good example of how to train a network using differentiable physics. Their video of interactively controlling 2D and 3D robots looks interesting. Although some of the techniques like staging, batching, since activation, and periodic signals are intuitive and existing tricks, it is good to see that a combination of them can successfully train a controller.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary
This paper presents a practical recipe for training neural network locomotion controllers using gradients from differentiable physics. The core contribution is not a single novel algorithmic component but a well-curated combination of established techniques—task staging into periods with tailored objectives, batching, sine activations and periodic inputs, and regularization—that together yield robust and sample-efficient training. The empirical findings are useful to practitioners: larger batch sizes accelerate convergence, and Adam consistently outperforms SGD on the reported tasks. The supplementary video compellingly demonstrates real-time, interactive control on 2D and 3D robots. While I find the paper valuable as a practitioner’s guide, several clarity issues in the methodology and notation need to be addressed.

Strengths
- Provides concrete, empirical guidance for leveraging differentiable physics to train robust controllers.
- Effective synthesis of intuitive, existing techniques (staging/periods, batching, sine activations, periodic inputs, and regularization) to stabilize and speed up training.
- The manual decomposition of long-horizon tasks into shorter periods with specific objectives mitigates sparse and non-smooth rewards.
- Clear empirical takeaways: larger batch sizes lead to faster convergence, and Adam outperforms SGD on these tasks.
- Strong qualitative evidence via the supplementary video showing real-time control in both 2D and 3D settings.

Weaknesses and points needing clarification
- Actuation regularization (Eq. 5) is unclear. There appears to be a dimensional mismatch between A(t), which is in actuation DOF space, and gv(n), which is a velocity vector; this suggests a norm such as ||gv(n)|| may have been intended.
- It is unclear why the actuation regularization considers only the target velocity gv(n) and not the height target gh(n) (or other task targets) for scenarios like jumping.
- The statement that periodic control signals can induce high-frequency noise is asserted but not explained.
- The paper refers to a “modified Adam optimizer” without specifying what modifications were made (e.g., changed hyperparameters versus algorithmic changes).
- The notion of an “iteration” in Figure 4 is ambiguous—whether it denotes one simulation step or one optimization update is not clear.

Suggestions for the authors
- Clarify Equation 5: explicitly state the dimensions of A(t) and gv(n), and whether a norm ||gv(n)|| is intended in the regularization term.
- Explain the choice to regularize only toward target velocity. If height gh(n) (or other objectives) are intentionally excluded, provide the rationale; alternatively, discuss whether and when they should be included.
- Elaborate on the mechanism by which periodic control signals may induce unwanted high-frequency noise.
- Specify what constitutes the “modified Adam optimizer.” If the changes are purely hyperparameters, enumerate them; if algorithmic, describe the adjustments.
- Define precisely what one “iteration” means in the training loop (simulation step vs. network update), especially as it relates to the timing in Figure 4.

Overall assessment
This work is a useful and pragmatic contribution that will help practitioners train differentiable-physics-based locomotion controllers effectively. The main ideas are well-motivated, the empirical guidance is actionable, and the demonstrations are compelling. The outstanding concerns are largely about clarity and should be straightforward to address. I therefore recommend acceptance at the marginal level.

Rating: 6 (marginally above the acceptance threshold)

extracted_ideas.summary: The reviewer finds the paper a practical and useful guide for training NN controllers with differentiable physics, showing that a combination of known techniques can effectively learn complex locomotion skills. However, they note several clarity issues regarding the actuation regularization term, the claimed modified Adam optimizer, and the definition of an iteration.
strengths: Provides empirical recipes for using differentiable physics gradients to train robust controllers.
Effectively combines intuitive existing techniques (staging/periods, batching, sine activations, periodic input signals, regularization) to accelerate and stabilize training.
Manually designed objective functions avoid sparse and non-smooth rewards for subtasks.
Demonstrates that larger batch sizes can lead to faster convergence.
Shows Adam performs better than SGD for these tasks.
Compelling supplementary video demonstrating real-time interactive control for 2D and 3D robots.
Divides long-horizon tasks into shorter periods with different objectives to improve learning.
weaknesses: Equation 5 is unclear: dimensional mismatch between A(t) (actuation DOF) and gv(n) (velocity vector), suggesting a possible need for ||gv(n)||.
Actuation regularization seems to consider only target velocity; unclear why height gh(n) is omitted.
The claim that periodic control signals may induce unwanted high-frequency noise is not explained.
The paper mentions a "modified Adam optimizer" but does not specify what modifications were made.
The definition of an iteration (in Figure 4) is ambiguous—unclear if it is one simulation step or one optimization update.
suggestions: Clarify Equation 5, including the dimensions of A(t) and gv(n), and whether a norm ||gv(n)|| is intended.
Explain why only target velocity is used in the actuation regularization and whether height gh(n) (or other targets) should be included.
Justify or elaborate on the statement that periodic control signals can cause high-frequency noise.
Explicitly describe how the Adam optimizer is modified (e.g., parameter changes or algorithmic adjustments).
Define clearly what constitutes one iteration in the training loop shown in Figure 4 (simulation steps vs. network updates).
main_points: The paper offers practical guidance and an effective combination of existing techniques to train locomotion controllers via differentiable physics.
Task decomposition into periodic segments with manually designed objectives mitigates sparse, non-smooth rewards.
Training stability and speed benefit from batching, regularization, and sine activation layers, with Adam outperforming SGD.
Key methodological details are unclear: the actuation regularization term, the nature of the modified Adam optimizer, and the training iteration definition.
technical_details: Question on Equation 5: how is gv(n) subtracted from A(t) given their likely different dimensions (actuation DOF vs. 3D/6D velocity), suggesting ||gv(n)|| may be intended.
Why the actuation loss considers only target velocity gv(n) and not jumping height gh(n).
Unclear rationale for the statement that periodic control signals may induce high-frequency noise.
"Modified Adam optimizer" is mentioned but the specific modifications are not described.
Ambiguity about the meaning of one iteration in Figure 4 (network update vs. simulation step).
Empirical observations: larger batch sizes speed up convergence; Adam superior to SGD for these tasks; use of sine activation and periodic signal inputs.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposes to use differentiable simulators for learning locomotion skills for deformable characters. The key contribution is the experiments and analysis of a large number of design choices, including activation function, reward design, optimizer, batch size, etc. The paper finds that by combining differentiable simulator with the right design choices, the character can learn general and complex skills with one policy. The paper also shows that this combination can significantly outperform PPO.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:
1) This paper combines differentiable simulation with many carefully chosen design decisions, which enables effective learning of soft body locomotion.
2) The analysis and ablation study provides useful and practical guidance to practitioners in the field of reinforcement learning and locomotion control.

Weakness:
1) Technical contribution is lean. The Taichi differentiable simulator is an existing work. This paper seems to use it without lots of changes. The second claimed contribution "end-to-end differentiable simulation environment, including mass-spring and material point" is not well supported. The description of simulation is high level or in Section 2. It is hard to understand the scope of this contribution.

2) I hope that the paper can demonstrate more impressive and diverse results. For example, a soft character navigating uneven terrains, or a soft robot manipulating objects. A soft body character running on flat ground, as shown in this paper, has been demonstrated in several prior works, such as DiffTaichi. Even though this paper demonstrates multiple locomotion skills (turning, different speed and jumping) in one policy, the improvement over the prior work is incremental.

Additional questions and comments:
1) The paper claims in Section 2.1 that "Any physically-based deformable object simulator can be applied to our learning framework". I thought that the simulator needs to be differentiable. So only a small subset of simulation will work. Did I miss anything?

2) It is unclear to me how applicable the results of the ablation study are to other tasks. For example, the sine actuation function and the periodic signal may not be as important for manipulation tasks. If the application of this paper is only locomotion, its contributions are quite limited.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper demonstrates good results for learning soft body locomotion. Although the technical novelty is lean, I find that the analysis and ablation of various design decisions are helpful, and has practical values to guide further development in this direction. For this reason, I am slightly leaning towards accepting the paper.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes to use differentiable simulators for learning locomotion skills for deformable characters. The key contribution is the experiments and analysis of a large number of design choices, including activation function, reward design, optimizer, batch size, etc. The paper finds that by combining differentiable simulator with the right design choices, the character can learn general and complex skills with one policy. The paper also shows that this combination can significantly outperform PPO.

Main Review:
Strengths:
1) This paper combines differentiable simulation with many carefully chosen design decisions, which enables effective learning of soft body locomotion.
2) The analysis and ablation study provides useful and practical guidance to practitioners in the field of reinforcement learning and locomotion control.

Weakness:
1) Technical contribution is lean. The Taichi differentiable simulator is an existing work. This paper seems to use it without lots of changes. The second claimed contribution "end-to-end differentiable simulation environment, including mass-spring and material point" is not well supported. The description of simulation is high level or in Section 2. It is hard to understand the scope of this contribution.

2) I hope that the paper can demonstrate more impressive and diverse results. For example, a soft character navigating uneven terrains, or a soft robot manipulating objects. A soft body character running on flat ground, as shown in this paper, has been demonstrated in several prior works, such as DiffTaichi. Even though this paper demonstrates multiple locomotion skills (turning, different speed and jumping) in one policy, the improvement over the prior work is incremental.

Additional questions and comments:
1) The paper claims in Section 2.1 that "Any physically-based deformable object simulator can be applied to our learning framework". I thought that the simulator needs to be differentiable. So only a small subset of simulation will work. Did I miss anything?

2) It is unclear to me how applicable the results of the ablation study are to other tasks. For example, the sine actuation function and the periodic signal may not be as important for manipulation tasks. If the application of this paper is only locomotion, its contributions are quite limited.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper demonstrates good results for learning soft body locomotion. Although the technical novelty is lean, I find that the analysis and ablation of various design decisions are helpful, and has practical values to guide further development in this direction. For this reason, I am slightly leaning towards accepting the paper.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary
This paper investigates learning soft-body locomotion by leveraging a differentiable simulation stack combined with a set of carefully chosen design elements. The central contribution lies in empirically grounded design choices and comprehensive ablation analyses that guide effective control, including multi-skill locomotion within a single policy. While the approach shows clear empirical advantages over PPO, the technical novelty is limited and several claims about the simulation environment are insufficiently substantiated. Overall, I lean slightly toward acceptance because the practical insights are valuable to practitioners, despite incremental methodological contributions.

Strengths
- Effective combination of differentiable simulation with well-motivated design decisions yields strong soft-body locomotion behaviors.
- Comprehensive ablation studies offer concrete guidance for reinforcement learning and locomotion control (e.g., activation functions, periodic control signals).
- Demonstrates clear performance gains over PPO and achieves multiple locomotion skills with a single policy.

Weaknesses
- The technical contribution is thin: the work largely relies on the existing Taichi/DiffTaichi differentiable simulator with minimal evident changes.
- The claimed “end-to-end differentiable simulation environment,” including both mass-spring and MPM backends, is described only at a high level; the scope and novelty of this component remain unclear.
- Experimental demonstrations are not sufficiently broad or challenging; results focus mainly on running on flat ground, which has been shown by prior work, making the contribution appear incremental.

Suggestions for Improvement
- Provide more diverse and demanding demonstrations (e.g., uneven terrain traversal, object interaction/manipulation) to better establish the approach’s capabilities beyond flat-ground locomotion.
- Clarify the claim that “any physically based deformable simulator can be used.” Please explicitly acknowledge the requirement of differentiability and delineate the practical subset of applicable simulators.
- Substantiate the contribution of the end-to-end differentiable environment with more technical detail on the mass-spring and MPM components, their interfaces, and what is novel relative to Taichi/DiffTaichi.
- Discuss the applicability and generality of the ablation findings—particularly the role of sine activations and periodic signals—beyond locomotion. Are these choices likely to transfer to manipulation or other deformable-body tasks, or are they task-specific?

Overall Assessment
The paper’s primary value is the empirical synthesis of differentiable simulation with targeted design choices, supported by informative ablations. These insights are likely to help practitioners build effective controllers for soft-body systems and appear to enable multi-skill locomotion within a single policy that outperforms PPO. However, the technical novelty is limited, and key claims about the simulation environment are not sufficiently detailed. The demonstrations, while functional, are not broad enough to convincingly push the frontier relative to prior work.

Decision
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

Rationale
Despite incremental methodological contributions and limited breadth of demonstrations, the empirical analyses and practical guidance are useful and likely to impact the community. Strengthening the technical exposition and expanding the experimental scope would significantly elevate the paper.
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes to use differentiable simulators for learning locomotion skills for deformable characters. The key contribution is the experiments and analysis of a large number of design choices, including activation function, reward design, optimizer, batch size, etc. The paper finds that by combining differentiable simulator with the right design choices, the character can learn general and complex skills with one policy. The paper also shows that this combination can significantly outperform PPO.

Main Review:
Strengths:
1) This paper combines differentiable simulation with many carefully chosen design decisions, which enables effective learning of soft body locomotion.
2) The analysis and ablation study provides useful and practical guidance to practitioners in the field of reinforcement learning and locomotion control.

Weakness:
1) Technical contribution is lean. The Taichi differentiable simulator is an existing work. This paper seems to use it without lots of changes. The second claimed contribution "end-to-end differentiable simulation environment, including mass-spring and material point" is not well supported. The description of simulation is high level or in Section 2. It is hard to understand the scope of this contribution.

2) I hope that the paper can demonstrate more impressive and diverse results. For example, a soft character navigating uneven terrains, or a soft robot manipulating objects. A soft body character running on flat ground, as shown in this paper, has been demonstrated in several prior works, such as DiffTaichi. Even though this paper demonstrates multiple locomotion skills (turning, different speed and jumping) in one policy, the improvement over the prior work is incremental.

Additional questions and comments:
1) The paper claims in Section 2.1 that "Any physically-based deformable object simulator can be applied to our learning framework". I thought that the simulator needs to be differentiable. So only a small subset of simulation will work. Did I miss anything?

2) It is unclear to me how applicable the results of the ablation study are to other tasks. For example, the sine actuation function and the periodic signal may not be as important for manipulation tasks. If the application of this paper is only locomotion, its contributions are quite limited.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper demonstrates good results for learning soft body locomotion. Although the technical novelty is lean, I find that the analysis and ablation of various design decisions are helpful, and has practical values to guide further development in this direction. For this reason, I am slightly leaning towards accepting the paper.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper proposes to learn neural network controllers in a differentiable simulator for locomotion tasks. A set of technical improvements are made and make the controllers more robust and require fewer samplers to learn. These include: using periodic activation functions, design of loss functions, and using large batch size. The paper shows learned locomotion skills of running, jumping, and crawling for different soft robots, which can be interactively controlled.
reviews.main_review:
### Strength
* +++ The paper shows great results of interactively controlling soft robot locomotion and switching among different goals.

### Weakness
* --- The overall technical contribution is limited. The proposed contribution on loss designs is limited to the locomotion tasks, and it is not clear whether these losses would generalize to other robots and the real world [1, 2].
* -- The use of the sine activation function is not well supported by the experiments. In table 1, sine function shows marginal improvement over tanh, and in table 5 of the appendix, using tanh shows much better performance over sine. Furthermore, tanh tends to become saturated during backpropagation of a long sequence. I would suggest the author also compares with ReLU.
* -- Presentation in the paper can be improved:
     1. The modified Adam optimizer is listed as one of the technical contributions. However, it is not stated clearly what is the modification and how does it compare to the original Adam optimizer. 
     2. The name of the robots are presented in Fig. 7 of Sec. 4.5 but are referenced multiple times in previous sections and table 1. It would be helpful to clarify the name of the agents early on. 
     3. Reference of Fig. 5 at the end of Sec. 4.2 seems to be incorrect, as no comparison of SGD and Adam is shown in Fig. 5.

[1] Lee, Joonho, et al. "Learning quadrupedal locomotion over challenging terrain." Science robotics 5.47 (2020).
[2] Zhao, Allan, et al. "RoboGrammar: graph grammar for terrain-optimized robot design." ACM Transactions on Graphics (TOG) 39.6 (2020): 1-16.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper shows interesting results of locomotion tasks, but the overall technical contribution is limited as presented. The writing of the paper could also be further improved. I am willing to raise my score if the listed concerns can be addressed.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper proposes to learn neural network controllers in a differentiable simulator for locomotion tasks. A set of technical improvements are made and make the controllers more robust and require fewer samplers to learn. These include: using periodic activation functions, design of loss functions, and using large batch size. The paper shows learned locomotion skills of running, jumping, and crawling for different soft robots, which can be interactively controlled.

Main Review:
### Strength
* +++ The paper shows great results of interactively controlling soft robot locomotion and switching among different goals.

### Weakness
* --- The overall technical contribution is limited. The proposed contribution on loss designs is limited to the locomotion tasks, and it is not clear whether these losses would generalize to other robots and the real world [1, 2].
* -- The use of the sine activation function is not well supported by the experiments. In table 1, sine function shows marginal improvement over tanh, and in table 5 of the appendix, using tanh shows much better performance over sine. Furthermore, tanh tends to become saturated during backpropagation of a long sequence. I would suggest the author also compares with ReLU.
* -- Presentation in the paper can be improved:
     1. The modified Adam optimizer is listed as one of the technical contributions. However, it is not stated clearly what is the modification and how does it compare to the original Adam optimizer. 
     2. The name of the robots are presented in Fig. 7 of Sec. 4.5 but are referenced multiple times in previous sections and table 1. It would be helpful to clarify the name of the agents early on. 
     3. Reference of Fig. 5 at the end of Sec. 4.2 seems to be incorrect, as no comparison of SGD and Adam is shown in Fig. 5.

[1] Lee, Joonho, et al. "Learning quadrupedal locomotion over challenging terrain." Science robotics 5.47 (2020).
[2] Zhao, Allan, et al. "RoboGrammar: graph grammar for terrain-optimized robot design." ACM Transactions on Graphics (TOG) 39.6 (2020): 1-16.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper shows interesting results of locomotion tasks, but the overall technical contribution is limited as presented. The writing of the paper could also be further improved. I am willing to raise my score if the listed concerns can be addressed.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: On the Unreasonable Effectiveness of Feature Propagation in Learning on Graphs with Missing Node Features

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The authors consider the case of training GNNs with missing features. The proposed framework consists of a diffusion based step prior to training the GNN. A discretization of the approach leads to what they term "feature propagation", a scalable method to impute features on the graph. The assumption is that the energy function that determines how related the features are to each other can be learnt from the data. The authors consider the gradient flow and it's solution to minimize the dirichlet energy, which is far more scalable than computing the closed form solution. Experiments on multiple datasets show that the proposed method outperforms several baselines.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:

The paper is well written, and easy to follow. The problem the authors address is interesting, and the solution provided is simple, yet elegant. The experimental results are thorough, both in terms of comparing performance and speed of the method. 

Weaknesses:

It's not completely clear how the proposed method extends to vector valued features. Is the process repeated independently for each feature? or is there some "interdependencies" between feature dimensions that can be made use of? How does that affect the computational complexity?
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
Initial assessment : accept

The paper is well written, and easy to follow. The problem the authors address is interesting, and the solution provided is simple, yet elegant. The experimental results are thorough, both in terms of comparing performance and speed of the method. Missing value imputation is a problem that is often ill-addressed when it comes to ML research. In this paper, the authors specifically look at imputation in the context of training GNNs, and use the graph to perform the imputation. 

I feel the authors need to address the difference between the proposed method and label propagation a bit more. They claim it's different because of labels being discrete and the diffusion operator being different, but I'm not clear why that's a deal breaker. Won't the algorithm be the same but with a new operator? 

Another point to address is the extension to vector valued features. They claim the extension is straightforward, but a note to this effect will be nice. 

other comments:
P1 last paragraph : I’m not sure if being unaware to the task is a bad thing necessarily.  isn't it better to do imputation in a task agnostic fashion?

Fig 3: Please make the text larger. The figures are hard to read. 

Fig 3: Also any intuition why Random is so much worse than Zero in all these datasets?

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors consider the case of training GNNs with missing features. The proposed framework consists of a diffusion based step prior to training the GNN. A discretization of the approach leads to what they term "feature propagation", a scalable method to impute features on the graph. The assumption is that the energy function that determines how related the features are to each other can be learnt from the data. The authors consider the gradient flow and it's solution to minimize the dirichlet energy, which is far more scalable than computing the closed form solution. Experiments on multiple datasets show that the proposed method outperforms several baselines.

Main Review:
Strengths:

The paper is well written, and easy to follow. The problem the authors address is interesting, and the solution provided is simple, yet elegant. The experimental results are thorough, both in terms of comparing performance and speed of the method. 

Weaknesses:

It's not completely clear how the proposed method extends to vector valued features. Is the process repeated independently for each feature? or is there some "interdependencies" between feature dimensions that can be made use of? How does that affect the computational complexity?

Initial assessment : accept

In this paper, the authors specifically look at imputation in the context of training GNNs, and use the graph to perform the imputation. 

I feel the authors need to address the difference between the proposed method and label propagation a bit more. They claim it's different because of labels being discrete and the diffusion operator being different, but I'm not clear why that's a deal breaker. Won't the algorithm be the same but with a new operator? 

Another point to address is the extension to vector valued features. They claim the extension is straightforward, but a note to this effect will be nice.

Fig 3: Please make the text larger. The figures are hard to read.

8: accept, good paper
reviews.text:
Summary and overall assessment
This is a clear, well-written paper tackling an important and timely problem: imputing missing node features for GNNs. The proposed diffusion-based feature propagation—framed as gradient flow minimizing a Dirichlet energy—offers a simple, elegant, and scalable alternative to closed-form solutions. The experimental study is thorough, with convincing performance and speed comparisons. I recommend acceptance (rating: 8).

Strengths
- Clarity and readability: The paper is easy to follow, with a clean problem setup and method description.
- Importance: Missing features in graph learning is a relevant and impactful setting.
- Method: The diffusion-based approach is simple yet principled, and the use of gradient flow is appealing for scalability relative to the closed-form solution.
- Empirics: Experiments are comprehensive, covering both accuracy and efficiency; the results are strong and consistent across datasets.

Weaknesses and requests for clarification
- Extension to vector-valued features: The paper does not sufficiently explain how the method handles multi-channel inputs. Are feature dimensions treated independently, or are interdependencies across channels modeled? If processed independently, please discuss whether this is a design choice or a limitation, and what the computational implications are. If interdependencies are leveraged, clarify how this is implemented and how it affects complexity.
- Relation to label propagation: The current distinction—emphasizing discrete labels and a different diffusion operator—does not fully establish whether the algorithm is fundamentally different or essentially a variant of label propagation with a different operator. A sharper conceptual and algorithmic comparison would be helpful.
- Position on task-agnostic imputation: The critique of task-agnostic approaches (e.g., in the last paragraph of page 1) seems under-argued. It would be useful to articulate when task-agnostic imputation is appropriate or even preferable, and when task-aware objectives offer clear benefits.
- Figure readability: Figure 3 is hard to read; the text is too small.
- Baseline behavior: Across datasets, the Random baseline underperforms the Zero baseline. Some intuition or analysis for this pattern would improve the empirical narrative.

Suggestions for improvement
- Provide a detailed treatment of vector-valued features: specify whether channels are imputed independently, whether any cross-channel coupling is used, and the resulting impact on computational complexity.
- Strengthen the positioning relative to label propagation: go beyond operator-level differences to clarify whether the algorithmic mechanism and objectives are meaningfully distinct, or whether the method can be seen as an instance within a broader label/feature propagation family.
- Discuss task-agnostic versus task-aware imputation: outline trade-offs, assumptions, and practical considerations to justify the chosen stance.
- Improve figure readability by enlarging the text in Figure 3.
- Offer intuition for the Random versus Zero baseline behavior, potentially tying it to graph structure, feature distributions, or the diffusion dynamics.

Conclusion
The paper offers a clear and elegant solution with strong empirical support and good scalability, and it addresses a problem of substantial interest. While several clarifications and presentation improvements are needed—particularly regarding vector-valued features, the relation to label propagation, the task-agnostic stance, figure readability, and the Random versus Zero observation—these are largely addressable. I recommend acceptance (8: accept, good paper).

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: A graph neural network (GNN) pre-processing step for completing missing node features is presented: feature propagation (FP). The method is based on minimisation of Dirichlet energy. A more efficient iterative update aproach is derived avoiding matrix inversion (cubic operation) to a series of sparse-to-dense matrix multiplications. The method acts similar to a low-pass filter for missing features, which complements the GNN learning algorithm. Relevant work is covered and similarities and differences mentioned in many cases. Experiments on seven publicly available datasets show that the method is effective and performs especially well when a large proportion of the feature values are missing (above 90%). A run-time analysis of wall-clock performance compares their method to two state-of-the-art methods for handling missing node features in GNNs and their method is 3 x faster in these comparison. A weakness of their method, FP, is analysed; FP assumes the graphs are homophlic, that nearby nodes have similar node features but if this is not the case then the method does not perform well. This is some interesting future work.
reviews.main_review:
This paper addresses a very interesting problem that is relevant to real-world applications; dealing with missing node features for graph neural networks (GNNs). The approach is based on minimising Dirichlet energy, which is a emthod previously used for graph regularisation and has relations with graph Laplcians, so is well suited to working with data on graph topology. The author(s) derive an efficient iterative algorithm which makes the approach scalable to large graphs which makes it more practical for real-world applications. Many public datasets were used for benchmarking that show very promising results.

In the experiments, it's surprising how well the nearest-neigbour averaging worked. In the comparison method's own papers SAT paper has a 'NeighAggre' baseline that seems to do something similar but gets worse scores than their method and GCNMF compares with kNN with k=5, and this also performs worse than their method. Do you have some insights into why neighbourhood averaging performs so well?

This method is a pre-processing step, not integrated into the GNN, as such many other methods for estimating the missing values could be used. One justification of this choice of approach is that is acts as a low-pass filter, and the GNN mainly learns from the lower frequency components.  This work is very close to matrix completion with graph side information.In this domain there are other methods that are highly scalable and are able to accurately estimate missing values when large amounts of the data (>99%) are missing [1, 2, 4]. Furthermore, one method uses a similar optimisation approach of iterative dense-sparse matrix mulitplication [1]. Worth note is that an extension to [1] will also remove edges in the graph that do not have high-homophily that could improve results for low-himopholy results. Also [3] is a kernel method for matrix completion that allows for a choice of covariance functions, including a diffusion kernel that is therefore similar to this approach. I think it is worth at mentioning this in comparison to this approach and even adding emprical evaluation against these methods, especially [1].

Other small comments:

You report using V100 GPUs (16 or 32Gb?), how much memory did GCNMF take before ran out of memory.

It wasn't crystal clear how the approach works for vector node attributes, do you simply repeat the algorithm for each dimension separately?

"Proposition 1: inverse of Laplacian depends on a connected graph, as eigen values are 0 if graph has clusters." How true is this assumption in real graphs, and what can be done in the case where a graph is not connected?

How do you know 40 iterations is enough, is there a easy guide to this and what happens if you keep running the algorithm, will it eventually make all missing featues almost the same if run for a large number of iterations?

"For a fair comparison, we use the same standard hyperparameters for all methods across all experiments." Does this mean that the downstream 2-layer GCN model has the same hyperparameters? 

Edits:
    A_u should be A_uu ?
    
    Zhu and Ghahramani reference is incomplete

    donwstream task -> downstream task


1. Rao, Nikhil, et al. "Collaborative Filtering with Graph Information: Consistency and Scalable Methods." NIPS. Vol. 2. No. 4. 2015.

2. Strahl, Jonathan, et al. "Scalable probabilistic matrix factorization with graph-based priors." Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence. Vol. 34. No. 04. 2020.

3. Zhou, Tinghui, et al. "Kernelized probabilistic matrix factorization: Exploiting graphs and side information." Proceedings of the 2012 SIAM international Conference on Data mining. Society for Industrial and Applied Mathematics, 2012.

4. Monti, Federico, Michael M. Bronstein, and Xavier Bresson. "Geometric matrix completion with recurrent multi-graph neural networks." arXiv preprint arXiv:1704.06803 (2017).
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
As this is a preprocessing step that essentially is similar to matrix completion with graph side information, then I believe that comparison with this literature is missing. Otherwise this is a very good paper. The approach they have for the problem, which could be seen as matrix completion with graph side information, is I believe novel in that domain, as well as in this framework for graph neural networks. Therefore, comparison with this domain is important here, as other methods in this domain could also be well justified as pre-processing steps. One such method that both scalable (using similar final equation, but dervied from a different approach, conjugate gradients), must be compared both in the paper and experiments in my opinion. Otherwise, it feels like the work is trying to avoid comparison with similar methods by claiming this work is related to GNN, while really it feels like this is a matrix completion with graph side information method, that is then used for GNNs.

After feedback:

I still feel that the motivation for using this particular method of estimating the missing values is not clearly better than other approaches. Also, the link to GNN seems weak as essentially this is a method for completing missing data and with this seems better suited to the domain for estimating missing values, where a stronger comparison with other missing values estimating methods can be compared - for example in comparison to recommender system approaches like collaborative filtering with graph side information. The link to GNN could be a small subsection in that style of paper. I don't see why running GNN is such a large part of this work when it's a stand alone preprocessing step. Therefore I've lowered my score from 6 to 5.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
A graph neural network (GNN) pre-processing step for completing missing node features is presented: feature propagation (FP). The method is based on minimisation of Dirichlet energy. A more efficient iterative update aproach is derived avoiding matrix inversion (cubic operation) to a series of sparse-to-dense matrix multiplications. The method acts similar to a low-pass filter for missing features, which complements the GNN learning algorithm. Relevant work is covered and similarities and differences mentioned in many cases. Experiments on seven publicly available datasets show that the method is effective and performs especially well when a large proportion of the feature values are missing (above 90%). A run-time analysis of wall-clock performance compares their method to two state-of-the-art methods for handling missing node features in GNNs and their method is 3 x faster in these comparison. A weakness of their method, FP, is analysed; FP assumes the graphs are homophlic, that nearby nodes have similar node features but if this is not the case then the method does not perform well. This is some interesting future work.

Main Review:
This paper addresses a very interesting problem that is relevant to real-world applications; dealing with missing node features for graph neural networks (GNNs). The approach is based on minimising Dirichlet energy, which is a emthod previously used for graph regularisation and has relations with graph Laplcians, so is well suited to working with data on graph topology. The author(s) derive an efficient iterative algorithm which makes the approach scalable to large graphs which makes it more practical for real-world applications. Many public datasets were used for benchmarking that show very promising results.

In the experiments, it's surprising how well the nearest-neigbour averaging worked. In the comparison method's own papers SAT paper has a 'NeighAggre' baseline that seems to do something similar but gets worse scores than their method and GCNMF compares with kNN with k=5, and this also performs worse than their method. Do you have some insights into why neighbourhood averaging performs so well?

This method is a pre-processing step, not integrated into the GNN, as such many other methods for estimating the missing values could be used. One justification of this choice of approach is that is acts as a low-pass filter, and the GNN mainly learns from the lower frequency components.  This work is very close to matrix completion with graph side information.In this domain there are other methods that are highly scalable and are able to accurately estimate missing values when large amounts of the data (>99%) are missing [1, 2, 4]. Furthermore, one method uses a similar optimisation approach of iterative dense-sparse matrix mulitplication [1]. Worth note is that an extension to [1] will also remove edges in the graph that do not have high-homophily that could improve results for low-himopholy results. Also [3] is a kernel method for matrix completion that allows for a choice of covariance functions, including a diffusion kernel that is therefore similar to this approach. I think it is worth at mentioning this in comparison to this approach and even adding emprical evaluation against these methods, especially [1].

Other small comments:

You report using V100 GPUs (16 or 32Gb?), how much memory did GCNMF take before ran out of memory.

It wasn't crystal clear how the approach works for vector node attributes, do you simply repeat the algorithm for each dimension separately?

"Proposition 1: inverse of Laplacian depends on a connected graph, as eigen values are 0 if graph has clusters." How true is this assumption in real graphs, and what can be done in the case where a graph is not connected?

How do you know 40 iterations is enough, is there a easy guide to this and what happens if you keep running the algorithm, will it eventually make all missing featues almost the same if run for a large number of iterations?

"For a fair comparison, we use the same standard hyperparameters for all methods across all experiments." Does this mean that the downstream 2-layer GCN model has the same hyperparameters? 

Edits:
    A_u should be A_uu ?
    
    Zhu and Ghahramani reference is incomplete

    donwstream task -> downstream task


1. Rao, Nikhil, et al. "Collaborative Filtering with Graph Information: Consistency and Scalable Methods." NIPS. Vol. 2. No. 4. 2015.

2. Strahl, Jonathan, et al. "Scalable probabilistic matrix factorization with graph-based priors." Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence. Vol. 34. No. 04. 2020.

3. Zhou, Tinghui, et al. "Kernelized probabilistic matrix factorization: Exploiting graphs and side information." Proceedings of the 2012 SIAM international Conference on Data mining. Society for Industrial and Applied Mathematics, 2012.

4. Monti, Federico, Michael M. Bronstein, and Xavier Bresson. "Geometric matrix completion with recurrent multi-graph neural networks." arXiv preprint arXiv:1704.06803 (2017).

Otherwise this is a very good paper. Therefore, comparison with this domain is important here, as other methods in this domain could also be well justified as pre-processing steps. One such method that both scalable (using similar final equation, but dervied from a different approach, conjugate gradients), must be compared both in the paper and experiments in my opinion. Otherwise, it feels like the work is trying to avoid comparison with similar methods by claiming this work is related to GNN, while really it feels like this is a matrix completion with graph side information method, that is then used for GNNs.

After feedback:

I still feel that the motivation for using this particular method of estimating the missing values is not clearly better than other approaches. Also, the link to GNN seems weak as essentially this is a method for completing missing data and with this seems better suited to the domain for estimating missing values, where a stronger comparison with other missing values estimating methods can be compared - for example in comparison to recommender system approaches like collaborative filtering with graph side information. The link to GNN could be a small subsection in that style of paper. I don't see why running GNN is such a large part of this work when it's a stand alone preprocessing step. Therefore I've lowered my score from 6 to 5.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposes a fast way to do node feature imputation in graph learning settings. In a nutshell, the idea is to consider the graph topology in the imputation by doing a feature by feature minimization of the Laplacian quadratic form. However, since the exact solution to this requires a matrix inversion, this quantity is minimized by a gradient descent procedure that relies on sparse matrix multiplications.
reviews.main_review:
Through numerical experiments the authors illustrate the benefit of this simple approach. Nonetheless, my main concern has to do with the novelty of this. Using the quadratic form of the graph Laplacian (Dirichlet energy) for label propagation or graph signal interpolation has been used several times over the last decade (as referenced in this paper). Moreover, using a gradient descent approach instead of inverting the Laplacian has also been used quite often. E.g., see the tutorial "Signal processing on higher-order networks: Livin’on the edge... and beyond" where in Section 2.4.2 the authors review some typical formulations of signals on graphs before going into higher-order networks. Notice that Equation (4) contains (I-L)^k, which corresponds to several applications of the (normalized) adjacency matrix. In fact, it is mentioned that "This may be interpreted in terms of k gradient descent steps of the cost function (Dirichlet energy)".

From the perspective of graph signal processing, the true solution with the inverse of the Laplacian can be seen as an IIR filter and this is an FIR approximation of the filter. This idea of graph filter design has also been studied quite a bit over the last 7 years.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Under this perspective, I regard that the novelty in this paper is limited.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes a fast way to do node feature imputation in graph learning settings. In a nutshell, the idea is to consider the graph topology in the imputation by doing a feature by feature minimization of the Laplacian quadratic form. However, since the exact solution to this requires a matrix inversion, this quantity is minimized by a gradient descent procedure that relies on sparse matrix multiplications.

Main Review:
Through numerical experiments the authors illustrate the benefit of this simple approach. Nonetheless, my main concern has to do with the novelty of this. Using the quadratic form of the graph Laplacian (Dirichlet energy) for label propagation or graph signal interpolation has been used several times over the last decade (as referenced in this paper). Moreover, using a gradient descent approach instead of inverting the Laplacian has also been used quite often. E.g., see the tutorial "Signal processing on higher-order networks: Livin’on the edge... and beyond" where in Section 2.4.2 the authors review some typical formulations of signals on graphs before going into higher-order networks. Notice that Equation (4) contains (I-L)^k, which corresponds to several applications of the (normalized) adjacency matrix. In fact, it is mentioned that "This may be interpreted in terms of k gradient descent steps of the cost function (Dirichlet energy)".

From the perspective of graph signal processing, the true solution with the inverse of the Laplacian can be seen as an IIR filter and this is an FIR approximation of the filter. This idea of graph filter design has also been studied quite a bit over the last 7 years.

Summary Of The Review:
Under this perspective, I regard that the novelty in this paper is limited.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary
The paper proposes a feature propagation/imputation method that minimizes the Dirichlet energy (Laplacian quadratic form) via gradient descent. The approach is simple, computationally efficient—relying on sparse matrix multiplications—and is supported by numerical experiments that demonstrate practical benefits. While I find the empirical results and simplicity appealing, the core formulation and its iterative realization substantially overlap with well-established ideas in graph signal processing (GSP), which limits the novelty.

Strengths
- Conceptually and computationally simple: the use of gradient descent leads to an efficient algorithm implementable with sparse matrix multiplications.
- The numerical experiments clearly illustrate that the proposed procedure is beneficial in practice.

Weaknesses
- Limited novelty: minimizing the Laplacian quadratic form for interpolation/label or feature propagation is a well-known strategy in the literature.
- Using gradient descent as a substitute for explicit Laplacian inversion has appeared frequently, and repeated applications of (I − L) are well-documented as gradient steps on the Dirichlet energy.
- From a GSP perspective, the exact solution corresponds to an IIR filter given by the inverse Laplacian, whereas the proposed iterative scheme is an FIR approximation—an angle that has been studied extensively over the past several years.

Technical perspective
- The exact minimizer involves inverting the Laplacian; the paper replaces this with iterative gradient descent using sparse mat-vec operations.
- Repeated application of (I − L)^k can be interpreted as k steps of gradient descent on the Dirichlet energy (equivalently, repeated application of a normalized adjacency operator).
- This aligns with prior formulations (e.g., the tutorial “Signal processing on higher-order networks: Livin’on the edge... and beyond,” Section 2.4.2, Eq. (4)), and with the broader GSP view of inverse-Laplacian filtering (IIR) versus its FIR approximations.

Suggestions for improvement
- Clarify and strengthen the novelty claims relative to the GSP literature, explicitly acknowledging prior work that interprets repeated (I − L) applications as gradient descent on the Dirichlet energy.
- Position the method squarely within the graph filter design framework, discussing the IIR (inverse Laplacian) versus FIR approximation perspective and articulating how this work departs from or advances beyond prior studies.
- Provide a more detailed comparison to established formulations (including the referenced tutorial) to highlight any substantive contributions beyond known techniques.

Overall assessment
The paper presents a clean and efficient method with demonstrable empirical benefits. However, given the close alignment with well-established formulations in GSP—both in objective (Dirichlet energy minimization) and in algorithmic realization (gradient descent and its interpretation as FIR approximation to the inverse-Laplacian filter)—the contribution appears incremental. I therefore assess it as marginally below the acceptance threshold.

Rating: 5 (marginally below the acceptance threshold)

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper address the problem of  missing node features in graph by conducting feature propagation (FP). An iterative updated algorithm is presented. FP is a preprocessing step and can be used in multiple downstream tasks. Experiments on node classification shows the effectiveness of FP. The authors also provided comparison on running time and showed how homophily affects the performance of FP.
reviews.main_review:
The strengths:

1. The paper is clearly written and easy to follow
2. The performance on missing 99% of the features is impressive
3. The speed improvement is promising

The weaknesses:

1. FP is not a new idea/algorithm, it is essentially same as label propagation (LP) by Zhu et al. [1]. Although the authors argued that "Differently from our setting of diffusion of continuous node features, they deal with discrete label classes directly, resulting in a different diffusion operator.", from my understanding, there is not substantial difference. LP also propagate the label probability, which is continuous. The derivation, the update formula of FP are the same as LP, the only difference is to substitute the weight matrix in LP with Laplacian matrix. Interpreting label propagation via Dirchlet energy is also proposed in [2], where instead of using $x_i$, $x_j$, Solomon et al. were using $f_i$, $f_j$, which are label function of node $i$ and $j$. Consider $x_i$'s as the soft label with label function $f_i$, then they are essentially the same thing.
2. The experiment part shows that simple averaging of the neighbors gives good result when the missing portion is $\leq$ 50%. In that case, I think it is also important to show experimental results of algorithms that do not use node features, to see if there is really a gap on the performance. (Note that under strong assumption of homophily, it is not surprising that simple averaging of neighbors works well. FP or LP takes account the topology of the graph and changes simple averaging of neighbors to weighted average of the whole network. The global information of the graph is the key to a better performance in this case, and I doubt other algorithms using the global information can also get similar good performance.) For example, one can use simple label propagation on the node classes, or using LINE, 
DeepWalk or node2vec to get a embedding of each node and then classifying with the embeddings. If the performance is similar as what FP gives when there are 99% features missing, I would argue that in that case we can simply go with classical algorithms using only the network topology. We will only use GNN when we have enough features available, while in which case it seems simple averaging of neighbors is good enough. The paper is not so convincing at the current stage.

[1] Xiaojin, Zhu, and Ghahramani Zoubin. "Learning from labeled and unlabeled data with label propagation." Tech. Rep., Technical Report CMU-CALD-02–107, Carnegie Mellon University (2002).

[2] Solomon, Justin, Raif Rustamov, Leonidas Guibas, and Adrian Butscher. "Wasserstein propagation for semi-supervised learning." In International Conference on Machine Learning, pp. 306-314. PMLR, 2014.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: In general I like the idea of using FP as a preprocessing step. However, I am not convinced that the idea is novel enough to be accepted in ICLR, details are referred to my main review.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper address the problem of  missing node features in graph by conducting feature propagation (FP). An iterative updated algorithm is presented. FP is a preprocessing step and can be used in multiple downstream tasks. Experiments on node classification shows the effectiveness of FP. The authors also provided comparison on running time and showed how homophily affects the performance of FP.

Main Review:
The strengths:

1. The paper is clearly written and easy to follow
2. The performance on missing 99% of the features is impressive
3. The speed improvement is promising

The weaknesses:

1. FP is not a new idea/algorithm, it is essentially same as label propagation (LP) by Zhu et al. [1]. Although the authors argued that "Differently from our setting of diffusion of continuous node features, they deal with discrete label classes directly, resulting in a different diffusion operator.", from my understanding, there is not substantial difference. LP also propagate the label probability, which is continuous. The derivation, the update formula of FP are the same as LP, the only difference is to substitute the weight matrix in LP with Laplacian matrix. Interpreting label propagation via Dirchlet energy is also proposed in [2], where instead of using $x_i$, $x_j$, Solomon et al. were using $f_i$, $f_j$, which are label function of node $i$ and $j$. Consider $x_i$'s as the soft label with label function $f_i$, then they are essentially the same thing.
2. The experiment part shows that simple averaging of the neighbors gives good result when the missing portion is $\leq$ 50%. In that case, I think it is also important to show experimental results of algorithms that do not use node features, to see if there is really a gap on the performance. (Note that under strong assumption of homophily, it is not surprising that simple averaging of neighbors works well. FP or LP takes account the topology of the graph and changes simple averaging of neighbors to weighted average of the whole network. The global information of the graph is the key to a better performance in this case, and I doubt other algorithms using the global information can also get similar good performance.) For example, one can use simple label propagation on the node classes, or using LINE, 
DeepWalk or node2vec to get a embedding of each node and then classifying with the embeddings. If the performance is similar as what FP gives when there are 99% features missing, I would argue that in that case we can simply go with classical algorithms using only the network topology. We will only use GNN when we have enough features available, while in which case it seems simple averaging of neighbors is good enough. The paper is not so convincing at the current stage.

[1] Xiaojin, Zhu, and Ghahramani Zoubin. "Learning from labeled and unlabeled data with label propagation." Tech. Rep., Technical Report CMU-CALD-02–107, Carnegie Mellon University (2002).

[2] Solomon, Justin, Raif Rustamov, Leonidas Guibas, and Adrian Butscher. "Wasserstein propagation for semi-supervised learning." In International Conference on Machine Learning, pp. 306-314. PMLR, 2014.

Summary Of The Review:
In general I like the idea of using FP as a preprocessing step. However, I am not convinced that the idea is novel enough to be accepted in ICLR, details are referred to my main review.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary
The paper is clearly written and easy to follow. It reports strong empirical performance, including under extreme feature sparsity (e.g., 99% missing features), and highlights promising speed improvements. However, I am not convinced by the novelty claims. The proposed Feature Propagation (FP) appears essentially equivalent to classical Label Propagation (LP), with the main difference being the diffusion operator choice. The empirical evaluation would also benefit from stronger comparisons to topology-only baselines that are likely to perform similarly when features are largely missing.

Strengths
- Clear, accessible presentation.
- Strong results, notably with 99% missing features.
- Compelling speed improvements.

Main Concerns
- Novelty: FP seems to mirror LP both in derivation and update rule, with FP effectively substituting LP’s weight matrix by the Laplacian. This does not establish a substantive methodological distinction.
- Scope of LP: LP already propagates continuous label probabilities (soft labels), so the argument that FP’s handling of continuous features differentiates it is not convincing.
- Prior theory: The interpretation of propagation via Dirichlet (or related) energy minimization has appeared in prior work (e.g., Solomon et al., 2014), which weakens the novelty of the theoretical framing.
- Empirical evidence: The results do not fully isolate the contribution of FP relative to simple, topology-driven effects. In particular:
  - Simple neighbor averaging is known to work well when ≤50% of features are missing.
  - Under homophily, global graph-driven averaging can largely explain performance; FP/LP and related methods all exploit this.
  - The current experiments do not verify whether topology-only methods can match FP in the heavy-missingness regime.

Suggestions for Improvement
- Add topology-only baselines:
  - Label propagation on node classes.
  - Network embeddings such as LINE, DeepWalk, or node2vec, followed by a downstream classifier.
- Demonstrate a clear and consistent performance gap over these topology-only methods, especially at 99% missing features.
- More clearly articulate and justify the novelty and differences between FP and LP beyond the choice of diffusion operator.

Decision
Rating: 5 (marginally below the acceptance threshold)

Rationale: While the paper is well written and the empirical results and speed are promising, the core methodological contribution appears too close to established LP, and the theoretical framing builds on known Dirichlet energy interpretations. Without stronger evidence against topology-only baselines and a clearer articulation of what is genuinely new in FP, I remain marginally below acceptance.
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper address the problem of  missing node features in graph by conducting feature propagation (FP). An iterative updated algorithm is presented. FP is a preprocessing step and can be used in multiple downstream tasks. Experiments on node classification shows the effectiveness of FP. The authors also provided comparison on running time and showed how homophily affects the performance of FP.

Main Review:
The strengths:

1. The paper is clearly written and easy to follow
2. The performance on missing 99% of the features is impressive
3. The speed improvement is promising

The weaknesses:

1. FP is not a new idea/algorithm, it is essentially same as label propagation (LP) by Zhu et al. [1]. Although the authors argued that "Differently from our setting of diffusion of continuous node features, they deal with discrete label classes directly, resulting in a different diffusion operator.", from my understanding, there is not substantial difference. LP also propagate the label probability, which is continuous. The derivation, the update formula of FP are the same as LP, the only difference is to substitute the weight matrix in LP with Laplacian matrix. Interpreting label propagation via Dirchlet energy is also proposed in [2], where instead of using $x_i$, $x_j$, Solomon et al. were using $f_i$, $f_j$, which are label function of node $i$ and $j$. Consider $x_i$'s as the soft label with label function $f_i$, then they are essentially the same thing.
2. The experiment part shows that simple averaging of the neighbors gives good result when the missing portion is $\leq$ 50%. In that case, I think it is also important to show experimental results of algorithms that do not use node features, to see if there is really a gap on the performance. (Note that under strong assumption of homophily, it is not surprising that simple averaging of neighbors works well. FP or LP takes account the topology of the graph and changes simple averaging of neighbors to weighted average of the whole network. The global information of the graph is the key to a better performance in this case, and I doubt other algorithms using the global information can also get similar good performance.) For example, one can use simple label propagation on the node classes, or using LINE, 
DeepWalk or node2vec to get a embedding of each node and then classifying with the embeddings. If the performance is similar as what FP gives when there are 99% features missing, I would argue that in that case we can simply go with classical algorithms using only the network topology. We will only use GNN when we have enough features available, while in which case it seems simple averaging of neighbors is good enough. The paper is not so convincing at the current stage.

[1] Xiaojin, Zhu, and Ghahramani Zoubin. "Learning from labeled and unlabeled data with label propagation." Tech. Rep., Technical Report CMU-CALD-02–107, Carnegie Mellon University (2002).

[2] Solomon, Justin, Raif Rustamov, Leonidas Guibas, and Adrian Butscher. "Wasserstein propagation for semi-supervised learning." In International Conference on Machine Learning, pp. 306-314. PMLR, 2014.

Summary Of The Review:
In general I like the idea of using FP as a preprocessing step. However, I am not convinced that the idea is novel enough to be accepted in ICLR, details are referred to my main review.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

extracted_ideas.summary: The review finds the paper clearly written with impressive performance and speed, but questions the novelty, arguing the proposed Feature Propagation (FP) is essentially equivalent to Label Propagation (LP). The reviewer requests stronger comparisons with topology-only baselines and remains unconvinced, rating it marginally below acceptance.
strengths: Clear and easy-to-follow writing
Impressive performance with 99% missing features
Promising speed improvements
weaknesses: Novelty is questionable; FP is viewed as essentially the same as Label Propagation (LP)
Differences from LP are not substantively established; LP also handles continuous label probabilities
Dirichlet energy interpretation for propagation has prior work, undermining novelty claims
Experimental evidence is not fully convincing since simple neighbor averaging works well when missing features ≤ 50%
Lack of comparisons to topology-only methods that might perform similarly when features are heavily missing
suggestions: Include experiments with topology-only baselines, such as simple label propagation on node classes
Compare against network embedding methods like LINE, DeepWalk, or node2vec followed by classification
Demonstrate a clear performance gap over topology-only methods, especially when 99% of features are missing
More clearly articulate and justify the novelty and differences between FP and LP beyond diffusion operator choice
main_points: FP is argued to be essentially equivalent to LP, with similar derivation and update rules, differing mainly by substituting the weight matrix with the Laplacian
LP propagates continuous label probabilities, so the distinction of FP handling continuous features is not convincing
Interpretation via Dirichlet (or related) energy minimization has prior art, weakening novelty claims
Experiments should verify whether topology-only methods can match FP when features are largely missing
Under homophily, global graph information (weighted averaging over the network) likely drives performance, which other methods may also exploit
technical_details: Reviewer asserts FP’s update is the same as LP’s, except FP substitutes LP’s weight matrix with the Laplacian
LP handles continuous label probabilities (soft labels), not only discrete labels
Prior work interprets label propagation through Dirichlet/Wasserstein energy perspectives (e.g., Solomon et al., 2014)

Homophily is a key assumption; FP/LP effectively perform weighted averaging across the whole network under this assumption
