title: Learning Audio-Visual Dereverberation

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper examines an audio-visual approach for dereverberation, where dereverberation of speech is conditioned on RGB and depth images (either field-of-view or panoramic). It proposes a dataset for this task based on real-world 3D scans of homes, using Librispeech data. Real data is also used The model is based on a U-Net conditioned on embeddings extracted by a "visual acoustics" network doing direct spectrogram prediction. The method is evaluated using PESQ, WER for speech recognition, and EER for speaker verification on synthetic and real data. The audio-visual method is found to perform marginally better than an audio-only version of the model. The audio-only version of the model outperforms several baselines from the literature on synthetic data, with mixed results on real data.
reviews.main_review:
### Strengths:

S1) To my knowledge, this is the first audio-visual approach to dereverberation. I think it is interesting to see how much visual information can help.

S2) The proposed dataset seems quite useful. A lot of existing synthetic reverb for such audio tasks is generated using shoebox room simulators, so having more realistic environments seems quite useful. The accompanying data recorded in real environments using loudspeaker playback (such that the clean reference is available) is also quite useful, and I hope the authors plan to release a recipe for this data.

S3) The paper includes a lot of ablations, which I really appreciate. Though the focus of the paper is on audio-visual approaches to the task, I'm really glad the paper evaluates audio-only versions of the model, as well as other components such as the matching loss.

S4) The paper is clear and well-written.

### Weaknesses:

W1) I listened to the demos in the supplementary material, and I feel like I heard a fair amount of artifacts and distortion in some examples: for moderate reverb, the model predictions have a certain "buzziness" to them, and more extreme levels of reverb have a "twanginess" to them. Something that would make this paper stronger would be a subjective human listening evaluation, e.g. MUSHRA, which would measure these kinds of artifacts better than an objective measure like PESQ. Also, alternative loss functions may help reduce artifacts. E.g. L1 loss instead of L2 on spectrogram magnitude, or MSE on power-compressed spectrograms can provide more perceptually-relevant loss functions.

W2) The processing method of using 2.56s frames, then concatenating the non-overlapping parts together seems suboptimal to me. Why not use e.g. a Hann window to stitch the predicted patches with overlap-add, or invert back to the time-domain and do overlap-add and train through this?

W3) Where does the "random speech embedding sampled from the data batch." come from? How is it computed? Is this a speaker ID embedding?

W4) The improvements by adding visual information are marginal compared to the audio-only model. The audio-only model does perform better than the baselines, but degrades PESQ compared to other baselines on real data. To benchmark the audio-only baseline against the literature, it would be interesting to compare to competitive systems in e.g. the REVERB challenge. I'm also not very convinced that visual input really helps that much.

W5) Table 1 would be improved by adding trainable parameter counts. Is the proposed model only doing better because it has more trainable parameters? WPE itself has no trainable parameters, since it's just a model-based EM algorithm.

W6) The lack of noise in the training and eval data limits real-world applicability, but I understand the focus of the paper in only on dereverberation. I'm glad that the authors tested noise robustness with WHAM! data, but 20 dB is a pretty high SNR for noise robustness. It would be interesting to see how performance degrades with decreasing SNR.

### Minor comments

M1) I feel that this statement isn't quite right: "R is a function of...the relative positioning of the speaker and the listener." I would argue that an RIR can be a function of the absolute positioning of the speaker and the listener. E.g. what if a source is close to a wall, versus in the the middle of a room?

M2) For the real data, would it be possible to be more specific about the source-to-microphone distances used in the real recordings, beyond "near-field to mid-field to far-field."?

M3) Also concerning real data: would it be possible to more more specific for this statement? "For each location, we play around 10 utterances.". How many utterances? Gender balanced? Different speakers across rooms?

M4) Nit: U-Net instead of UNet?

M5) "While our model is agnostic to the audio source type,": depends on the training data.

M6) In Tables 1 and 2, I would say "anechoic speech (upper bound)" instead of "clean speech (upper bound)", because to me "clean" suggests there's additive noise.

M7) About the blind RT60/DRR/distance predictions: "After converging, the average errors for them are 3e-3s, 160 dB and 17.92m respectively." Are these absolute or squared errors?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Overall, I vote for marginal acceptance. This is an interesting novel task (audio-visual dereverberation), and the proposed synthetic and real data are quite interesting and useful for the community. But the paper's results show that visual input provides marginal performance gain over the audio-only baseline. The audio-only baseline outperforms some benchmarks in terms of PESQ, WER for speech recognition, and EER for speaker verification on synthetic data, and WER and EER for real data, but I have concerns about the subjective listening quality of the demos, compared to other dereverberation algorithms I have listened to. Thus, I am concerned that this isn't a significant improvement over audio-only prior work, at least in terms of subjective listening quality.
reviews.correctness: 4: All of the claims and statements are well-supported and correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 4: The contributions are significant, and do not exist in prior works.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.confidence: 5: You are absolutely certain about your assessment. You are very familiar with the related work and checked the math/other details carefully.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper examines an audio-visual approach for dereverberation, where dereverberation of speech is conditioned on RGB and depth images (either field-of-view or panoramic). It proposes a dataset for this task based on real-world 3D scans of homes, using Librispeech data. Real data is also used The model is based on a U-Net conditioned on embeddings extracted by a "visual acoustics" network doing direct spectrogram prediction. The method is evaluated using PESQ, WER for speech recognition, and EER for speaker verification on synthetic and real data. The audio-visual method is found to perform marginally better than an audio-only version of the model. The audio-only version of the model outperforms several baselines from the literature on synthetic data, with mixed results on real data.

Main Review:
### Strengths:

S1) To my knowledge, this is the first audio-visual approach to dereverberation. I think it is interesting to see how much visual information can help.

S2) The proposed dataset seems quite useful. A lot of existing synthetic reverb for such audio tasks is generated using shoebox room simulators, so having more realistic environments seems quite useful. The accompanying data recorded in real environments using loudspeaker playback (such that the clean reference is available) is also quite useful, and I hope the authors plan to release a recipe for this data.

S3) The paper includes a lot of ablations, which I really appreciate. Though the focus of the paper is on audio-visual approaches to the task, I'm really glad the paper evaluates audio-only versions of the model, as well as other components such as the matching loss.

S4) The paper is clear and well-written.

### Weaknesses:

W1) I listened to the demos in the supplementary material, and I feel like I heard a fair amount of artifacts and distortion in some examples: for moderate reverb, the model predictions have a certain "buzziness" to them, and more extreme levels of reverb have a "twanginess" to them. Something that would make this paper stronger would be a subjective human listening evaluation, e.g. MUSHRA, which would measure these kinds of artifacts better than an objective measure like PESQ. Also, alternative loss functions may help reduce artifacts. E.g. L1 loss instead of L2 on spectrogram magnitude, or MSE on power-compressed spectrograms can provide more perceptually-relevant loss functions.

W2) The processing method of using 2.56s frames, then concatenating the non-overlapping parts together seems suboptimal to me. Why not use e.g. a Hann window to stitch the predicted patches with overlap-add, or invert back to the time-domain and do overlap-add and train through this?

W3) Where does the "random speech embedding sampled from the data batch." come from? How is it computed? Is this a speaker ID embedding?

W4) The improvements by adding visual information are marginal compared to the audio-only model. The audio-only model does perform better than the baselines, but degrades PESQ compared to other baselines on real data. To benchmark the audio-only baseline against the literature, it would be interesting to compare to competitive systems in e.g. the REVERB challenge. I'm also not very convinced that visual input really helps that much.

W5) Table 1 would be improved by adding trainable parameter counts. Is the proposed model only doing better because it has more trainable parameters? WPE itself has no trainable parameters, since it's just a model-based EM algorithm.

W6) The lack of noise in the training and eval data limits real-world applicability, but I understand the focus of the paper in only on dereverberation. I'm glad that the authors tested noise robustness with WHAM! data, but 20 dB is a pretty high SNR for noise robustness. It would be interesting to see how performance degrades with decreasing SNR.

### Minor comments

M1) I feel that this statement isn't quite right: "R is a function of...the relative positioning of the speaker and the listener." I would argue that an RIR can be a function of the absolute positioning of the speaker and the listener. E.g. what if a source is close to a wall, versus in the the middle of a room?

M2) For the real data, would it be possible to be more specific about the source-to-microphone distances used in the real recordings, beyond "near-field to mid-field to far-field."?

M3) Also concerning real data: would it be possible to more more specific for this statement? "For each location, we play around 10 utterances.". How many utterances? Gender balanced? Different speakers across rooms?

M4) Nit: U-Net instead of UNet?

M5) "While our model is agnostic to the audio source type,": depends on the training data.

M6) In Tables 1 and 2, I would say "anechoic speech (upper bound)" instead of "clean speech (upper bound)", because to me "clean" suggests there's additive noise.

M7) About the blind RT60/DRR/distance predictions: "After converging, the average errors for them are 3e-3s, 160 dB and 17.92m respectively." Are these absolute or squared errors?

Summary Of The Review:
Overall, I vote for marginal acceptance. This is an interesting novel task (audio-visual dereverberation), and the proposed synthetic and real data are quite interesting and useful for the community. But the paper's results show that visual input provides marginal performance gain over the audio-only baseline. The audio-only baseline outperforms some benchmarks in terms of PESQ, WER for speech recognition, and EER for speaker verification on synthetic data, and WER and EER for real data, but I have concerns about the subjective listening quality of the demos, compared to other dereverberation algorithms I have listened to. Thus, I am concerned that this isn't a significant improvement over audio-only prior work, at least in terms of subjective listening quality.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

Response:

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper presents a deep neural net-based dereverberation algorithm that uses both audio and video modalities. Based on the observation that a visual scene captured by a camera conveys information that is related to room characteristics, the authors propose a visually informed audio dereverberation method that aims to extract clean, anechoic speech from reverberant speech. In doing so, they first construct a large audio-visual dataset synthesized using a 3D simulator for real-world scanned environments and LibriSpeech data. They then train deep neural networks that take as inputs both visual data (RGB and depth images) and audio data (reverberant speech), and output clean speech. When training, two types of losses - one for clean speech spectrogram estimation and the other for reverb-visual matching - are used. Through the experiments with several downstream tasks for speech, they showed that the proposed audio-visual dererberation method outperforms the baseline models, both for synthetic and real-world test data.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:
- first multi-modal (audio-visual) approach applied to dereverberation
- paper is well organized and straightforward to follow
- solid experiments with ablation studies and in-depth analyses
- supplementary materials provide useful information not explained/described in the main text

Weaknesses:
- some of the key arguments are not sufficiently validated
- contributions are marginal and limited
- experiments and evaluation are not thorough

The key idea in this work is that visual input captured by a camera conveys information that characterizes room acoustics. With all the experiments and ablation studies, however, I don't believe that the authors provide sufficient information that support the aforementioned key hypothesis. The authors did take into account this matter in two ways: first, they included a reverb-visual matching loss term to penalize a matching between random speech and visual input, but little experiment or analysis was performed on this loss term - varying the weight or using different loss functions, for example. With or without using a reverb-visual matching loss was included in the ablation studies, but the effect was not significant compared with other methods. The performance was better with the audio-only model even in speech quality metric (PESQ). The second way is to use a random image instead of using the image that matches the room impulse response. In such scenario, the images does not carry any information relevant to the room acoustics, and thus the performance should be worse than or comparable to that of the audio-only method. However, the performance is better in general and is even best for some metric. Further experiments and deeper analysis are warranted to investigate this issue.

Another point that I hope to be added is the comparison of the model efficiency. Adding a visual embedding network increases the number of model parameters and computational costs. The proposed method is likely to work in real-time scenarios, and possibly on small devices, the efficiency should also be considered.

Lastly, I'd like to suggest to include R-vectors approaches as another baseline for comparison.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This paper proposes a multi-modal learning framework that is applied to speech dereverberation. The idea is interesting and somewhat novel, but the experimental validation is not thorough enough to support the key idea of reverb-visual matching nor to provide sufficient evidences that visual stream does convey information about room acoustics.

Summary Of The Paper:
This paper presents a deep neural net-based dereverberation algorithm that uses both audio and video modalities. Based on the observation that a visual scene captured by a camera conveys information that is related to room characteristics, the authors propose a visually informed audio dereverberation method that aims to extract clean, anechoic speech from reverberant speech. They then train deep neural networks that take as inputs both visual data (RGB and depth images) and audio data (reverberant speech), and output clean speech. When training, two types of losses - one for clean speech spectrogram estimation and the other for reverb-visual matching - are used. Through the experiments with several downstream tasks for speech, they showed that the proposed audio-visual dererberation method outperforms the baseline models, both for synthetic and real-world test data.

Main Review:
Strengths:
- first multi-modal (audio-visual) approach applied to dereverberation
- paper is well organized and straightforward to follow
- solid experiments with ablation studies and in-depth analyses
- supplementary materials provide useful information not explained/described in the main text

Weaknesses:
- some of the key arguments are not sufficiently validated
- contributions are marginal and limited
- experiments and evaluation are not thorough

The key idea in this work is that visual input captured by a camera conveys information that characterizes room acoustics. The authors did take into account this matter in two ways: first, they included a reverb-visual matching loss term to penalize a matching between random speech and visual input, but little experiment or analysis was performed on this loss term - varying the weight or using different loss functions, for example. With or without using a reverb-visual matching loss was included in the ablation studies, but the effect was not significant compared with other methods. The performance was better with the audio-only model even in speech quality metric (PESQ). The second way is to use a random image instead of using the image that matches the room impulse response. However, the performance is better in general and is even best for some metric. Further experiments and deeper analysis are warranted to investigate this issue.

Another point that I hope to be added is the comparison of the model efficiency. Adding a visual embedding network increases the number of model parameters and computational costs. The proposed method is likely to work in real-time scenarios, and possibly on small devices, the efficiency should also be considered.

Lastly, I'd like to suggest to include R-vectors approaches as another baseline for comparison.

This paper proposes a multi-modal learning framework that is applied to speech dereverberation. The idea is interesting and somewhat novel, but the experimental validation is not thorough enough to support the key idea of reverb-visual matching nor to provide sufficient evidences that visual stream does convey information about room acoustics.

reviews.text:
Recommendation: 6 (marginally above the acceptance threshold)

Summary
This paper presents, to my knowledge, the first audio-visual approach to speech dereverberation. The idea of leveraging visual context to inform room acoustics is intriguing and somewhat novel. The paper is well organized, easy to follow, and supported by solid experiments, ablations, and helpful supplementary materials. However, the central claim—that visual input conveys informative cues about room acoustics—is not convincingly substantiated by the current experimental evidence. In particular, the reverb-visual matching component requires deeper validation, and several results (e.g., strong performance with random images and audio-only models sometimes winning on PESQ) raise questions about the actual contribution of the visual modality. I therefore lean toward a marginal acceptance, contingent on clarifying and strengthening the empirical support for the main hypothesis.

Strengths
- Novelty and scope: The first multi-modal (audio-visual) approach applied to dereverberation.
- Clarity: The paper is well structured and straightforward to follow.
- Experimental effort: Includes ablation studies and in-depth analyses; the supplementary materials are informative and useful.

Weaknesses
- Insufficient validation of the central hypothesis that visuals characterize room acoustics. The presented results do not conclusively demonstrate that the visual stream provides reliable acoustic cues.
- The contributions feel marginal and limited given the current evidence.
- Experiments and evaluation are not thorough enough to support the strongest claims.
- The reverb-visual matching loss is under-analyzed. It is only toggled on/off in ablations; there is no exploration of different weights or alternative loss formulations to test robustness.
- The ablations do not convincingly establish a visual benefit. The audio-only model sometimes performs better (e.g., on PESQ).
- The random-image ablation, surprisingly, outperforms audio-only and is even best on some metrics, which undermines the claim that visuals encode room acoustics.
- Efficiency and deployability are not addressed: no comparisons of parameter counts, computational cost, or real-time feasibility—important for practical use on small devices.
- Missing relevant baselines, notably R-vectors.

Detailed Comments
- Training objectives: The method combines clean speech spectrogram estimation with a reverb-visual matching loss. While this is a reasonable design, the analysis around the latter is limited. Without a study of loss weighting or alternative formulations, it is hard to assess whether performance gains arise from meaningful cross-modal alignment or incidental regularization.
- Ablations: The observation that audio-only models sometimes outperform the audio-visual variant (e.g., PESQ) weakens the claim that visuals add consistent value. More concerning is that using random images (not matched to the room impulse response) can outperform audio-only and even achieve the best results on some metrics. This suggests that the pipeline may benefit from non-semantic effects of the visual branch, casting doubt on the asserted acoustic information conveyed by visuals.
- Evaluation scope: The absence of R-vectors as a baseline limits the interpretability of the gains. Likewise, without any discussion of model complexity and runtime/compute, it is difficult to gauge practicality and deployment potential.

Suggestions for Improvement
- Provide a deeper analysis of the reverb-visual matching loss:
  - Vary the loss weight to assess sensitivity and contribution.
  - Explore alternative loss functions to test whether the specific formulation is essential.
- Investigate why random images can outperform audio-only and sometimes yield the best metrics. Offer diagnostic analyses to clarify whether the visual stream contributes true acoustic information or acts as a regularizer or proxy signal.
- Include model efficiency comparisons: parameter counts, computational cost, and real-time feasibility, particularly for small-device scenarios.
- Add R-vectors approaches as a baseline to contextualize the performance.
- Expand the experimental validation to more directly substantiate the claim that visual input conveys room acoustic properties.

Overall Assessment
The paper tackles an interesting and novel angle on dereverberation and is thoughtfully executed in many respects. At the same time, the current evidence does not fully validate the central hypothesis about the role of visuals in characterizing room acoustics, and some ablation results actively call it into question. With stronger analysis of the reverb-visual matching loss, investigation into the random-image phenomenon, inclusion of relevant baselines, and attention to efficiency, the paper’s case would be significantly more compelling. As it stands, I recommend a marginal acceptance given the novelty, clarity, and potential impact, while encouraging the authors to address the identified gaps.
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper presents a deep neural net-based dereverberation algorithm that uses both audio and video modalities. Based on the observation that a visual scene captured by a camera conveys information that is related to room characteristics, the authors propose a visually informed audio dereverberation method that aims to extract clean, anechoic speech from reverberant speech. In doing so, they first construct a large audio-visual dataset synthesized using a 3D simulator for real-world scanned environments and LibriSpeech data. They then train deep neural networks that take as inputs both visual data (RGB and depth images) and audio data (reverberant speech), and output clean speech. When training, two types of losses - one for clean speech spectrogram estimation and the other for reverb-visual matching - are used. Through the experiments with several downstream tasks for speech, they showed that the proposed audio-visual dererberation method outperforms the baseline models, both for synthetic and real-world test data.

Main Review:
Strengths:
- first multi-modal (audio-visual) approach applied to dereverberation
- paper is well organized and straightforward to follow
- solid experiments with ablation studies and in-depth analyses
- supplementary materials provide useful information not explained/described in the main text

Weaknesses:
- some of the key arguments are not sufficiently validated
- contributions are marginal and limited
- experiments and evaluation are not thorough

The key idea in this work is that visual input captured by a camera conveys information that characterizes room acoustics. With all the experiments and ablation studies, however, I don't believe that the authors provide sufficient information that support the aforementioned key hypothesis. The authors did take into account this matter in two ways: first, they included a reverb-visual matching loss term to penalize a matching between random speech and visual input, but little experiment or analysis was performed on this loss term - varying the weight or using different loss functions, for example. With or without using a reverb-visual matching loss was included in the ablation studies, but the effect was not significant compared with other methods. The performance was better with the audio-only model even in speech quality metric (PESQ). The second way is to use a random image instead of using the image that matches the room impulse response. In such scenario, the images does not carry any information relevant to the room acoustics, and thus the performance should be worse than or comparable to that of the audio-only method. However, the performance is better in general and is even best for some metric. Further experiments and deeper analysis are warranted to investigate this issue.

Another point that I hope to be added is the comparison of the model efficiency. Adding a visual embedding network increases the number of model parameters and computational costs. The proposed method is likely to work in real-time scenarios, and possibly on small devices, the efficiency should also be considered.

Lastly, I'd like to suggest to include R-vectors approaches as another baseline for comparison.

Summary Of The Review:
This paper proposes a multi-modal learning framework that is applied to speech dereverberation. The idea is interesting and somewhat novel, but the experimental validation is not thorough enough to support the key idea of reverb-visual matching nor to provide sufficient evidences that visual stream does convey information about room acoustics.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This paper describes an audio de-reverberation method which integrates (panoramic, rgb+depth map) visual input with a spectrogram U-Net to estimate a de-reverberated spectrogram and recover the original clean signal.
The proposed method is compared to several baseline (audio-only) de-reverberation methods on speech enhancement, recognition, and speaker verification problems using both synthetic and real data.
Several ablations of the proposed method are compared, along with some quantitative error analysis investigating the effects of distance and environment.
The proposed method appears to consistently improve on speech recognition and speaker verification, and perform about on par with prior work on speech enhancement (as measured by PESQ).
reviews.main_review:
This paper takes an interesting and novel approach to de-reverberation.
The paper is well written, and each component of the method is well justified.
The qualitative analysis (e.g. figure 4) is a nice touch, and helps shed some light on the behavior of the proposed method (and baselines).
That said, I do have a few questions and suggestions.

My primary criticism is in the reporting of the evaluations (tables 1, 2, 3).
Each setting is summarized by a single average score, and the "best" number is written in bold with no discussion given to the distribution of scores on the test data or the practical meaning of score differences.
This may give readers an inflated sense of performance here: is PESQ=2.38 really any different from PESQ=2.37? 2.33?  (Likewise for WER, EER, etc.)
There is a brief mention of astronomically low p-values (1.56e-60?) which I expect are primarily driven by the size of the test set, and not the size of the effect.
I would strongly encourage the authors to do a more responsible job of reporting their results: include some error bars, or some other indication of the spread of the data!

The proposed method takes an interesting approach to optimizing both magnitude and phase (eqs 1 and 2) for the output spectrogram.
The Griffin-Lim algorithm is then used to refine the phase estimate, with a vague statement of this working better than directly using predicted phase or GL with random initialization.
While I won't ask for more experiments on this, I think the point deserves a bit more explanation than what is given here, especially since there is no ablation study on the phase reconstruction loss.  (Nor is there any discussion of hyper-parameter tuning for equation 4 beyond the settings reported in the appendix.)


I found Figure 5 (qualitative examples) to be pretty difficult to parse, and I say this as a reader with (probably) above-average experience reading spectrograms.
With a bit of squinting I can see what the authors are getting at, but it would be easier to read if the spectrograms were A) larger, and B) plotted on a logarithmic frequency axis so that the low frequencies (where most of the action is) occupy more of the visual real estate.


Finally, it was nice to see the results in table 3, which show quite a strong effect on the performance of the system depending on differences in distance and room geometry.
Not much is said about the geometric differences in the environments though, either for real or simulated data.
In particular, it looks from figure 2 as though there may be configurations of speaker/listener that have no direct path, in which one might say that everything observed is "reverb".
If this is the case, it would be interesting to see how performance (table 1) differs (if at all) in the presence or absence of a direct path.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Overall, I enjoyed this paper!  I think some of the reporting could be done better, but this is a minor complaint.
reviews.correctness: 4: All of the claims and statements are well-supported and correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 4: The contributions are significant, and do not exist in prior works.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 4: The contributions are significant, and do not exist in prior works.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

reviews.text:
Overall recommendation: 8 — accept (good paper)

Summary
This paper presents a novel audio-visual dereverberation method that leverages panoramic RGB and depth input with a spectrogram U-Net, jointly optimizing magnitude and phase and using Griffin–Lim for phase refinement. The approach is clearly described, components are well motivated, and qualitative analysis (e.g., Fig. 4) helps illuminate behavior. Empirically, the method consistently improves downstream ASR (WER) and speaker verification (EER), while speech enhancement (PESQ) is comparable to prior work. The experimental coverage is broad, including baselines, ablations, and analysis across distances and environments.

Strengths
- Interesting and novel use of panoramic RGB + depth for dereverberation in tandem with a spectrogram U-Net.
- Clear, well-written presentation with justified architectural choices and training objectives.
- Helpful qualitative insights (Fig. 4) into what the model attends to.
- Consistent, meaningful gains on downstream tasks (WER and EER).
- PESQ on par with prior work for speech enhancement.
- Thorough empirical study with baselines, ablations, and evaluations across distance and multiple environments.

Weaknesses
- Result reporting largely relies on single average scores with bolded best numbers in Tables 1–3, without showing variability or distributions. This makes it hard to judge robustness and practical significance.
- The practical importance of very small PESQ differences (e.g., 2.38 vs. 2.37 vs. 2.33) is unclear. Reported extremely low p-values (e.g., 1.56e-60) likely reflect large test set sizes rather than effect sizes.
- The phase reconstruction strategy needs deeper treatment: the rationale for combining magnitude/phase losses (Eqs. 1–2) with Griffin–Lim is under-explained, and there is no ablation isolating the contribution of the phase loss and the GL refinement (beyond claims that GL with the predicted phase outperforms random initialization).
- Limited discussion of how the combined loss in Eq. 4 was tuned beyond listing defaults in the appendix.
- Figure 5 is difficult to read; the spectrograms are small and plotted on a linear frequency axis, limiting interpretability.
- The analysis of environmental geometry is underdeveloped. In particular, there is no breakdown of performance in cases with and without a direct acoustic path between source and listener, and geometric differences across environments are not sufficiently discussed.

Suggestions for improvement
- Report variability (e.g., error bars, confidence intervals, or score distributions) in Tables 1–3 and discuss the practical significance of differences, especially for PESQ. Where p-values are reported, contextualize them with effect sizes.
- Provide a more detailed explanation of the phase refinement choice (why Griffin–Lim is preferred in this setting) and include an ablation that isolates the effect of the phase loss and the GL step.
- Elaborate on how the weights in the combined loss (Eq. 4) were selected and tuned in practice, beyond listing appendix defaults.
- Improve Figure 5 by enlarging spectrograms and adopting a logarithmic frequency axis to better highlight low-frequency content.
- Expand the analysis of environmental geometry, including performance stratified by the presence/absence of a direct path and a clearer discussion of geometric differences across environments.

Conclusion
This is a strong paper with a novel audio-visual dereverberation framework that delivers consistent gains on ASR and speaker verification and competitive speech enhancement quality. The writing is clear, the method is well justified, and the empirical study is generally thorough. Addressing the reporting, phase reconstruction analysis, loss tuning, figure clarity, and geometry-conditioned evaluation would further strengthen the contribution. I recommend acceptance.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: In this paper, the authors introduce a novel audio-visual dereverberation approach. They propose a Visually-Informed Dereverberation of Audio (VIDA) model for dereverberation. The authors also create synthetic/simulated datasets and real-world data for experimentation. Finally, they show the impact of the proposed VIDA model on several speech tasks including, recognition, enhancement, and speaker verification. The results are encouraging. The main contribution of this work is the use of visual information as an auxiliary input for dereverberation.
reviews.main_review:
While authors suggest visual information is helpful, it isn't reflected in the results. In Table 1, if we compare the "Audio-only dereverb." and "VIDA" results, the gains are very modest. The PESQ 2.32 to 2.37, WER-FT 3.76 to 3.66, and SID EER 2.61 to 2.40. This raises the question about the effectiveness of the proposed approach. I feel the majority of the dereverberation is still learned by the audio model. This should be investigated. 

Overall, the technical contribution is limited to a new dataset and the addition of ResNet for visual information processing. Similar approaches have been used in the audio-visual enhancement with lip information.

Summary Of The Paper:
In this paper, the authors introduce a novel audio-visual dereverberation approach. They propose a Visually-Informed Dereverberation of Audio (VIDA) model for dereverberation. The authors also create synthetic/simulated datasets and real-world data for experimentation. Finally, they show the impact of the proposed VIDA model on several speech tasks including, recognition, enhancement, and speaker verification. The results are encouraging. The main contribution of this work is the use of visual information as an auxiliary input for dereverberation.

Main Review:
While authors suggest visual information is helpful, it isn't reflected in the results. In Table 1, if we compare the "Audio-only dereverb." and "VIDA" results, the gains are very modest. The PESQ 2.32 to 2.37, WER-FT 3.76 to 3.66, and SID EER 2.61 to 2.40. This raises the question about the effectiveness of the proposed approach. I feel the majority of the dereverberation is still learned by the audio model. This should be investigated. 

Overall, the technical contribution is limited to a new dataset and the addition of ResNet for visual information processing. Similar approaches have been used in the audio-visual enhancement with lip information.

Summary Of The Review:
I believe the manuscript in its current form offers a very limited contribution to the research community, and the contribution of visual information in dereverberation is minimal.

Rating:
3: reject, not good enough
reviews.text:
Summary
The paper proposes VIDA, an audio-visual dereverberation framework that integrates a ResNet-based visual branch with an audio model. The authors assemble both synthetic/simulated datasets and real-world recordings, and evaluate VIDA across multiple downstream tasks, including speech recognition, speech enhancement, and speaker verification. While the overall results are encouraging, the evidence presented does not convincingly demonstrate that visual information substantively contributes to dereverberation beyond what the audio-only model achieves.

Strengths
- Presents an audio-visual dereverberation approach (VIDA).
- Curates useful datasets spanning synthetic/simulated conditions and real-world data.
- Evaluates broadly across tasks—speech enhancement, ASR, and speaker verification—providing a multifaceted view of performance.
- Empirical results are generally positive across tasks.

Weaknesses
- The contribution of the visual modality appears minimal. The improvements over the audio-only baseline in Table 1 are very modest: PESQ 2.32→2.37, WER-FT 3.76→3.66, and SID EER 2.61→2.40. These small gains do not substantiate the paper’s claim that visual cues materially aid dereverberation.
- The majority of dereverberation seems to be learned by the audio model, with the visual branch adding little.
- Technical novelty is limited. Beyond constructing datasets and adding a ResNet-based visual pathway, the approach is closely aligned with prior audio-visual enhancement paradigms that leverage lip information.
- Given the small improvements and overlap with existing methods, the overall contribution is limited.

Suggestions for improvement
- More thoroughly investigate and quantify the extent to which visual information contributes to dereverberation relative to the audio model, to substantiate the claim that the visual modality is beneficial.

Recommendation
3: reject, not good enough. Despite careful experimentation and generally encouraging results, the visual modality’s added value is not convincingly demonstrated, and the technical novelty is limited.

Summary Of The Review:
I believe the manuscript in its current form offers a very limited contribution to the research community, and the contribution of visual information in dereverberation is minimal.

Rating:
3: reject, not good enough

title: Layer-wise Adaptive Model Aggregation for Scalable Federated Learning

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposes a novel adaptive model aggregating schema under a federated learning setting, where it effective reduces the communication overhead without significantly sacrificing the generalization performance. Both empirical study and theoretical analysis are included to justify the effectiveness and efficiency of the proposed method.
reviews.main_review:
Effectively reducing the communication overhead is an important problem for federated learning, with the recent advance of layer-wise model freezing training, this paper manages to extend this idea for network traffic reduction in federated learning. Concretely, I list the main strengths and minor weakness of this paper as below:

Advantages:

+ To leverage layer-wise model freezing for federated learning is a simple but novel idea. According to the empirical study, this method seems to be effective and efficient. 
+ The paper also includes tight convergence analysis for the proposed method. 

Disadvantages:
+ The communication cost calculated in Section 6 is a little sloppy, in practices, there are plenty of system advances managing to hide the communication overhead within the computation slots, e.g. PyTorch-DDP (https://arxiv.org/abs/2006.15704). In this fashion, the actual runtime may not be simply linear to the amount of network traffic. Perhaps, a more sophisticated cost model would consider both the computation power of the edge device and network condition during training, then model the cost including the mechanism of overlapping communication and computation to estimate the actual runtime.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
The idea of utilizing layer-wise model freezing for federated learning is an interesting idea, the paper includes reasonable empirical study and convergence analysis to illustrate the effectiveness of this proposed approach. 
Although some part of the cost model in the simulation is a little sloppy, I tend to advocate the acceptance of this paper.

This paper proposes a novel adaptive model aggregating schema under a federated learning setting, where it effective reduces the communication overhead without significantly sacrificing the generalization performance. Main Review:
Effectively reducing the communication overhead is an important problem for federated learning, with the recent advance of layer-wise model freezing training, this paper manages to extend this idea for network traffic reduction in federated learning. Concretely, I list the main strengths and minor weakness of this paper as below:

Advantages:

Disadvantages:

In this fashion, the actual runtime may not be simply linear to the amount of network traffic. Perhaps, a more sophisticated cost model would consider both the computation power of the edge device and network condition during training, then model the cost including the mechanism of overlapping communication and computation to estimate the actual runtime.

Summary Of The Review:

Rating:
8: accept, good paper
reviews.text:
Summary
This paper proposes a simple yet novel layer-wise adaptive aggregation strategy—effectively, freezing selected layers across rounds—to reduce communication in federated learning. The idea is well-motivated, the empirical study is reasonable and indicates that communication can be cut substantially with little loss in generalization performance, and the convergence analysis is tight and supports the approach. My primary concern is with the communication cost modeling in Section 6, which is overly simplified and does not capture realistic runtime behavior in modern distributed systems.

Strengths
- Conceptual novelty with practical relevance: leveraging layer-wise model freezing within federated learning to reduce network traffic is simple, elegant, and impactful.
- Empirical effectiveness: the experiments consistently show reduced communication overhead without notable degradation in accuracy, suggesting strong practical potential.
- Theoretical grounding: the tight convergence analysis provides credible support for the proposed method and helps explain the observed behavior.
- Overall, the combination of a clear idea, sound analysis, and reasonable empirical evidence makes for a compelling contribution.

Weaknesses
- The communication cost calculation in Section 6 is too simplistic. It assumes a direct, roughly linear relationship between communication volume and runtime, which is not realistic in modern settings.
- Contemporary systems often overlap communication with computation (e.g., techniques used in PyTorch-DDP; see arXiv:2006.15704), meaning that communication overhead can be partially or fully hidden within compute phases.
- The current model does not incorporate device-level compute capabilities or heterogeneous network conditions, both of which strongly influence end-to-end runtime and cost.

Suggestions for Improvement
- Develop a more sophisticated cost model that explicitly accounts for device computation power and heterogeneous network conditions, so that the reported savings more accurately reflect wall-clock performance.
- Incorporate mechanisms for communication-computation overlap into the model, capturing scenarios where communication is hidden or only partially exposed in runtime.
- Acknowledge and compare against system-level advances (e.g., PyTorch-DDP-style overlapping) to contextualize the method’s benefits under realistic training pipelines and to clarify when communication reductions translate into tangible runtime gains.

Overall Assessment
The core idea is novel and practically effective, supported by a tight convergence analysis and reasonable empirical results. While the communication cost modeling is unrealistic for practical systems and should be improved to reflect overlap and hardware/network heterogeneity, this limitation does not undercut the contribution of the algorithmic technique itself. I recommend acceptance.

Rating: 8 — accept, good paper

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposes an adaptive interval schema for layer-wise model aggregation in federated settings. The aim of the proposed method is to reduce communication costs by adaptively decreasing the frequency of layer-wise aggregation with consideration of model discrepancy.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:

1. It is an interesting idea to adjust the aggregation interval for layer-wise aggregation.
2. Detailed theoretical analysis and experiments are conducted.
3. The paper is well-written.


Weaknesses:

1. It is uncertain that the proposed method can really reduce communication costs. Due to the reduced frequency of aggregation and adopt layer-wise partial model aggregation strategy in each round, it is very likely that the proposed method will decrease the convergence speed of the global model. Therefore, the proposed method needs more communication rounds to achieve a convergence state, thus the total communication costs are still very high. Moreover, at each communication round, the client still needs to download the full model from the server. Therefore, the downloading communication costs haven't been improved. 
2. In the convergence analysis, Assumption 4 is a little tricky, and helps to avoid much detailed and further analysis. The convergence rate is O(1/m^½ K^½), which is loose compared to some convergence analysis of FedAvg, such as ON THE CONVERGENCE OF FEDAVG ON NON-IID DATA (https://arxiv.org/abs/1907.02189).
3. The experiment is insufficient to support their claims. For example, the only baseline is FedAvg, and it is necessary to choose some compression methods as a baseline. Moreover, the communication cost should consider the number of communication rounds to achieve a convergence state.


Questions and suggestions:

1. Algorithm 1 should explicitly state which steps are executed on the server or client. For example, line 3 should be on the client, and lines from 4 to 10 are on the server. Moreover, it should explicitly highlight when the communication occurs.
2. It is necessary to draw the convergence curves of the proposed methods and baselines.
3. It is unclear how to construct non-IID scenarios in the experiment setting.
4. There are some federated learning methods that only update parts of its model. This paper should also consider these baseline methods. 

http://proceedings.mlr.press/v139/collins21a/collins21a.pdf

https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.07623

Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes an adaptive interval schema for layer-wise model aggregation in federated settings. The aim of the proposed method is to reduce communication costs by adaptively decreasing the frequency of layer-wise aggregation with consideration of model discrepancy.

Main Review:
Strengths:

1. It is an interesting idea to adjust the aggregation interval for layer-wise aggregation.
2. Detailed theoretical analysis and experiments are conducted.
3. The paper is well-written.


Weaknesses:

1. It is uncertain that the proposed method can really reduce communication costs. Due to the reduced frequency of aggregation and adopt layer-wise partial model aggregation strategy in each round, it is very likely that the proposed method will decrease the convergence speed of the global model. Therefore, the proposed method needs more communication rounds to achieve a convergence state, thus the total communication costs are still very high. Moreover, at each communication round, the client still needs to download the full model from the server. Therefore, the downloading communication costs haven't been improved. 
2. In the convergence analysis, Assumption 4 is a little tricky, and helps to avoid much detailed and further analysis. The convergence rate is O(1/m^½ K^½), which is loose compared to some convergence analysis of FedAvg, such as ON THE CONVERGENCE OF FEDAVG ON NON-IID DATA (https://arxiv.org/abs/1907.02189).
3. The experiment is insufficient to support their claims. For example, the only baseline is FedAvg, and it is necessary to choose some compression methods as a baseline. Moreover, the communication cost should consider the number of communication rounds to achieve a convergence state.


Questions and suggestions:

1. Algorithm 1 should explicitly state which steps are executed on the server or client. For example, line 3 should be on the client, and lines from 4 to 10 are on the server. Moreover, it should explicitly highlight when the communication occurs.
2. It is necessary to draw the convergence curves of the proposed methods and baselines.
3. It is unclear how to construct non-IID scenarios in the experiment setting.
4. There are some federated learning methods that only update parts of its model. This paper should also consider these baseline methods. 

http://proceedings.mlr.press/v139/collins21a/collins21a.pdf

https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.07623

Summary Of The Review:
This paper proposes an interesting layer-wise model aggregation method to reduce communication cost while trying to keep the same performance with FedAvg. However, the proposed method’s design, theoretical analysis and experiments cannot support its claim.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary
This paper proposes a layer-wise adaptive aggregation strategy that adjusts aggregation intervals across layers. The idea is interesting and the paper is generally well-written, with a sizable theoretical component and empirical evaluation. However, I have substantive reservations about the claimed communication savings, the strength of the theoretical guarantees, and the completeness of the experimental evaluation and baselines. As a result, I view the contribution as marginally below the acceptance threshold.

Strengths
- The core idea—adjusting aggregation intervals in a layer-wise manner—is novel and intuitively appealing for federated learning.
- The paper is clearly written and easy to follow.
- Theoretical analysis and experiments are provided to support the approach.

Weaknesses
- Questionable communication savings in practice: Reducing aggregation frequency and aggregating only part of the model per round may slow convergence, potentially requiring more rounds to reach a target accuracy. Moreover, clients still appear to download the complete global model every round, so download-side communication volume remains unchanged. Without evidence that time/rounds-to-accuracy improves, the net communication benefit is unclear.
- Theory seems weak relative to the literature: The convergence analysis hinges on a tricky bounded-dissimilarity condition (Assumption 4), and the resulting rate O(1/(m^0.5 K^0.5)) appears loose compared to existing FedAvg analyses (e.g., arXiv:1907.02189).
- Experimental evaluation is not sufficiently compelling: Only FedAvg is used as a baseline. There are no comparisons against communication-efficient methods such as compression, nor against methods that update only parts of the model. Additionally, communication cost is reported per iteration or per round rather than in terms of the number of rounds required to reach a target accuracy, which is the more relevant metric for communication efficiency. Convergence curves are also missing. Finally, the construction of non-IID data partitions is not clearly described.

Suggestions for improvement
- Algorithm clarity: In Algorithm 1, explicitly delineate client versus server operations and mark communication points. For example, clarify that line 3 runs on the client, while lines 4–10 run on the server, and indicate where uploads/downloads occur.
- Evaluation metrics and plots: Report communication efficiency in terms of rounds-to-convergence or rounds-to-target accuracy, not only per-round traffic. Include convergence curves comparing the proposed method to baselines.
- Baselines: Add comparisons to methods that update only parts of the model (e.g., Collins et al., ICML 2021; arXiv:2102.07623) as well as compression-based methods for communication efficiency.
- Experimental setup: Explain clearly how non-IID scenarios are generated.
- Theory: Clarify the realism and implications of Assumption 4 and discuss how it affects practical applicability. If possible, tighten the convergence analysis or contextualize the rate relative to existing FedAvg results.

Overall assessment and recommendation
While the idea of layer-wise adaptive aggregation is promising and the paper is well-presented, the current version leaves key questions unresolved about actual communication savings, offers relatively weak theoretical guarantees, and lacks comprehensive empirical comparisons and appropriate communication-efficiency metrics. I therefore recommend a rating of 5: marginally below the acceptance threshold. Strengthening the baselines, measurement of rounds-to-accuracy, algorithmic clarity, and theoretical positioning would significantly improve the work.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: In this paper, the authors propose a layer-wise model aggregation scheme in federated learning cases to reduce the communication cost. Specifically, they quantified the model discrepancy between local models and global models and adaptively adjusted the aggregation interval in a layer-wise manner. By increasing the aggregation intervals and relaxing the aggregation frequency, the method can reduce the communication cost in federated learning cases. The experimental results show it can reduce the communication cost for IID data and non-IID data compared to FedAvg.
reviews.main_review:
They provide a communication-efficient federated optimization and relax the aggregation frequency by quantifying the model discrepancy between local models and global models and adaptively adjusting the aggregation interval in a layer-wise manner.

Some questions are the following.

1）The baseline is FedAvg, too few. Why not compare with other the periodic full aggregation scheme (e.g., FedProx, FedNova, SCAFFOLD, etc.), even some synchronized schemes? the synchronized schemes refer to,

[1] Chen, Yang, Xiaoyan Sun, and Yaochu Jin. "Communication-efficient federated deep learning with
layerwise asynchronous model update and temporally weighted aggregation." IEEE transactions on neural
networks and learning systems 31.10 (2019): 4229-4238.

[2] Xie, C., Koyejo, S., & Gupta, I. (2019). Asynchronous federated optimization. arXiv preprint
arXiv:1903.03934. 

2）FedLAMA seems to lead to some accuracy drop as the experiments show. They do not show the total time in terms of communication cost, just show the reduced ratio for communication. It is not clear about absolute earnings with sacrificing the accuracy. In some cases, especially in industry, the model's accuracy matters more than training time (e.g., the total training time is within several hours.)

reviews.text:
Summary
This paper proposes a communication-efficient federated optimization method that adaptively aggregates model layers at different intervals based on a measure of local–global model discrepancy. The idea is theoretically grounded and aims to reduce communication without substantially harming performance. Empirical results indicate reduced communication cost relative to FedAvg under both IID and non-IID settings. However, the empirical evaluation is not yet convincing due to limited baselines, observed accuracy drops, and the absence of absolute efficiency metrics, which together leave the practical benefits unclear.

Strengths
- A clear and principled approach to layer-wise adaptive aggregation for communication efficiency in federated learning.
- Theoretical analysis provides support for the method’s design.
- Experiments show reduced communication cost versus FedAvg across both IID and non-IID data.

Weaknesses
- Baseline coverage is too narrow. The comparisons are limited to FedAvg, omitting established federated optimization methods that also address communication and heterogeneity (e.g., FedProx, FedNova, SCAFFOLD).
- No comparisons to asynchronous or synchronized alternatives such as Chen et al. (2019) on layerwise asynchronous updates and temporally weighted aggregation, or Xie et al. (2019) on asynchronous federated optimization.
- The proposed FedLAMA exhibits accuracy drops in the reported experiments, raising concerns about effectiveness.
- Communication efficiency is reported only as reduction ratios; there are no absolute measurements of total communication volume or total training time.
- Given that accuracy often dominates in practical (e.g., industrial) deployments, the trade-off currently appears unfavorable or at least insufficiently quantified, making the real-world benefit uncertain.
- Overall, the experimental evidence does not yet convincingly demonstrate the method’s effectiveness and practicality.

Suggestions for Improvement
- Broaden the baseline set to include FedProx, FedNova, and SCAFFOLD to better situate the contribution among established federated optimization methods.
- Add comparisons to asynchronous/synchronized schemes, specifically Chen et al. (2019) and Xie et al. (2019), to contextualize the advantages of layer-wise adaptive aggregation.
- Report absolute metrics, including total training time and total communication volume (e.g., bytes transferred), in addition to reduction ratios.
- Provide a clear quantification of the communication–accuracy trade-off. For scenarios where accuracy is paramount, clarify whether and when the proposed method remains attractive, and if not, under what regimes it is preferable.

Overall Assessment
The paper presents an appealing, theoretically motivated idea and initial evidence of communication savings. However, the limited empirical scope (baselines restricted to FedAvg), accuracy degradation, and lack of absolute efficiency metrics make it difficult to assess real-world utility. With broader comparisons and more comprehensive reporting, the work could become much more compelling.

Decision
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold.
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
In this paper, the authors propose a layer-wise model aggregation scheme in federated learning cases to reduce the communication cost. Specifically, they quantified the model discrepancy between local models and global models and adaptively adjusted the aggregation interval in a layer-wise manner. By increasing the aggregation intervals and relaxing the aggregation frequency, the method can reduce the communication cost in federated learning cases. The experimental results show it can reduce the communication cost for IID data and non-IID data compared to FedAvg.

Main Review:
They provide a communication-efficient federated optimization and relax the aggregation frequency by quantifying the model discrepancy between local models and global models and adaptively adjusting the aggregation interval in a layer-wise manner.

Some questions are the following.

1）The baseline is FedAvg, too few. Why not compare with other the periodic full aggregation scheme (e.g., FedProx, FedNova, SCAFFOLD, etc.), even some synchronized schemes? the synchronized schemes refer to,

[1] Chen, Yang, Xiaoyan Sun, and Yaochu Jin. "Communication-efficient federated deep learning with
layerwise asynchronous model update and temporally weighted aggregation." IEEE transactions on neural
networks and learning systems 31.10 (2019): 4229-4238.

[2] Xie, C., Koyejo, S., & Gupta, I. (2019). Asynchronous federated optimization. arXiv preprint
arXiv:1903.03934. 

2）FedLAMA seems to lead to some accuracy drop as the experiments show. They do not show the total time in terms of communication cost, just show the reduced ratio for communication. It is not clear about absolute earnings with sacrificing the accuracy. In some cases, especially in industry, the model's accuracy matters more than training time (e.g., the total training time is within several hours.)

Summary Of The Review:
Thanks to the authors for their efforts for their theoretical analysis and experimentally proof of the proposed federated optimization scheme. The experimental results are a little weak to show the effectiveness and the practicality.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

Main Review:

2. For Lemma 5.2, there should be a constraint for $\eta$.

Summary Of The Review:
This paper proposed a new idea for reducing communication costs. But the theoretical analysis has some flaws.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary
The paper proposes an adaptive aggregation strategy for federated learning that selects aggregation intervals based on the optimization dynamics. The idea is novel and practically motivated, and the authors provide a convergence-rate analysis connecting aggregation intervals to convergence, alongside extensive experiments that show notable reductions in communication cost.

Strengths
- Addresses an important problem in federated learning with a fresh and useful idea: adaptively choosing aggregation intervals.
- Provides an explicit convergence-rate analysis that links aggregation intervals to convergence behavior.
- Empirical evaluation is extensive and convincingly demonstrates reduced communication overhead.

Weaknesses
- Theoretical analysis contains concrete errors that undermine the main claims:
  - Equation (15) is incorrect.
  - Equation (25) is incorrect.
- Lemma 5.2 is missing a necessary constraint on the learning rate η, which is essential for the stated guarantees to hold.
- The convergence guarantees are weaker than established baselines:
  - The upper bound is worse than that of standard FedAvg.
  - Linear speedup reportedly requires K = O(m^3) (as noted in Remark 1), whereas prior work [1] achieves linear speedup with K = O(m). This creates a significant gap relative to known results.

Suggestions for Improvement
- Correct the identified mistakes in the theoretical development, specifically Equation (15) and Equation (25).
- Add and clearly state the appropriate learning rate constraint in Lemma 5.2.
- Strengthen the convergence analysis to be on par with or better than standard FedAvg, and reconcile the discrepancy in linear speedup conditions with prior work, such as “On the Linear Speedup Analysis of Communication Efficient Momentum SGD for Distributed Non-Convex Optimization” [1]. In particular, clarify why K = O(m^3) is required in this framework and whether tighter bounds are attainable.

Overall Assessment
This paper makes a promising practical contribution through adaptive aggregation and shows solid empirical communication savings. However, the theoretical component has specific errors and currently provides weaker guarantees than standard baselines, with a particularly unfavorable condition for linear speedup compared to [1]. Given these issues, I find the paper marginally below the acceptance threshold at this time.

Rating: 5 (marginally below the acceptance threshold)

title: Stability based Generalization Bounds for Exponential Family Langevin Dynamics

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper studies the generalization of iterative noisy learning algorithms. First of all, the authors provide a new generalization bound based on the Hellinger distance. This bound is basically based on the Hellinger distance between the output of the algorithm when one of the points in the training set is replaced with a fresh sample. They they use this bound to provide a generalization error guarantee for a broad class of noisy iterative algorithm which is defined as W_t = W_{t-1} - eta_t, where eta_t conditioned on the past iterated follows an exponential family distribution which for instance include SGLD. For this broad class, they provide a unified generalization bound that includes SGLD and Noisy Sign-SGD. Finally, the authors provide numerical study of their bound for several benchmarks and show that their bound achieves better estimate of generalization error.
reviews.main_review:
- Using the generalization bound in Theorem: In proposition 1, the authors provide a generalization bound based on the distributional stability in terms of Hellinger distance. However, in most of the results in the paper they mainly use the upper bound based on KL divergence (Prop. 2). Is that correct?  

- The dependence of the learning rate on the optimization trajectory: In your bound alpha_t depends on the dataset and trajectory. It basically depends on some sort of Lipschitz constant of the loss function. For instance in SGLD, alpha_t depends on the learning rate and inverse temperatures. Therefore, I think it can limit us to use very small learning rates. Also, how did you make sure that this constraint is satisfied in your numerical studies?

- The comparison with Rodríguez-Gálvez et al.: Your bound is very similar to the generalization bound in Haghifam et al'2020. As you mentioned the bound in Haghifam et al'2020 is stated for LD algorithm. Recently, the bound in Haghifam et al'2020 is extended to SGLD in [1]. The bound in Rodríguez-Gálvez et al has worse dependence on n (1/sqrt(n)), however, they have a decaying factor in their bound to compensate using of the chain rule. It would be nice to numerically compare your bound with the bound in [1]. 

- Applying your framework for analyzing SGD:  What are the roadblocks for applying your bound to analyzing SGD?

- Surrogate loss: I think you should distinguish the loss function that we use to measure the generalization error with the loss function that is used for training. You should use different notations. 


[1] "On random subset generalization error bounds and the stochastic gradient langevin dynamics algorithm", Borja Rodríguez-Gálvez, Germán Bassi, Ragnar Thobaben, Mikael Skoglund

Summary Of The Review:
Very interesting and solid contribution!

Rating:
8: accept, good paper
reviews.text:
Summary and contribution
This paper proposes a stability-based generalization framework grounded in Hellinger distance, leading to a distributional stability notion and a unified analysis for a broad class of noisy iterative algorithms defined by W_t = W_{t-1} − η_t, where η_t (conditioned on past iterates) follows an exponential family distribution (EFLD). The framework covers important instances such as SGLD and Noisy Sign-SGD. The authors further provide empirical evidence on standard benchmarks that their bound offers a tighter estimate of generalization error than prior alternatives. Overall, I find the contribution solid, interesting, and relevant.

Strengths
- Introduces a new generalization bound via Hellinger distance (distributional stability), which is conceptually appealing and technically novel.
- Provides a unified treatment for a large class of noisy iterative methods (EFLD), explicitly including SGLD and Noisy Sign-SGD.
- Empirically, the proposed bounds appear more accurate than competing bounds in estimating generalization error on benchmarks.
- The theoretical and empirical components are well-aligned and make a coherent case for the approach.

Points for clarification and weaknesses
1) Use of Hellinger vs. KL bounds
- While Proposition 1 presents the core Hellinger-based bound, many of the subsequent results seem to rely primarily on an upper bound via KL divergence (Proposition 2). It would help to clarify where the Hellinger distance is used directly versus where the KL-based relaxation is invoked, and to justify this choice in terms of tightness and tractability. As it stands, it is not fully clear whether the main benefits stem from the Hellinger framework itself or from the KL upper bound.

2) Step-size constraints and α_t
- The parameter α_t depends on the dataset and optimization trajectory. For SGLD in particular, α_t depends on the learning rate and inverse temperature, potentially constraining learning rates to be very small. Please discuss how this dependence affects the feasible step-size schedules in practice and what it implies for the applicability of the theory to commonly used hyperparameters. Relatedly, it is unclear how the α_t-related constraint was enforced or validated in the experiments; documenting this would strengthen the empirical section.

3) Comparison to Rodríguez-Gálvez et al.
- The comparison with Rodríguez-Gálvez et al. (their extension of Haghifam et al., 2020, to SGLD) appears incomplete. Since their bound exhibits a 1/sqrt(n) dependence with a decaying factor derived via the chain rule, a numerical comparison to your bound for SGLD would be very informative. Including such a comparison would better situate the contribution relative to closely related work.

4) Applicability to SGD
- It would be helpful to clarify to what extent the framework can be applied to standard (non-noisy) SGD. If there are roadblocks—conceptual or technical—to extending your analysis to SGD, please spell them out explicitly.

5) Notation for losses
- The notation currently conflates the training (surrogate) loss with the loss used to evaluate generalization. Please use distinct notation to differentiate these, and clarify throughout which loss each result pertains to.

Suggestions for improvement
- Explicitly state when Proposition 1 (Hellinger-based) is used as the primary tool versus when Proposition 2 (KL upper bound) is employed, and provide rationale for this choice.
- Discuss the implications of α_t’s data- and trajectory-dependence for practical learning rates, and document how these constraints were satisfied in the reported experiments.
- Add a numerical comparison with the Rodríguez-Gálvez et al. bound for SGLD.
- Elaborate on the obstacles to applying the framework to standard SGD.
- Revise notation to clearly separate training/surrogate loss from the generalization/evaluation loss.

Overall assessment
The paper advances the theory of generalization for noisy iterative algorithms via a principled Hellinger-based stability analysis and demonstrates promising empirical tightness. The main critiques are clarifications and comparisons rather than fundamental issues. With the suggested clarifications—especially around the use of KL vs. Hellinger, the role of α_t, and the comparison to Rodríguez-Gálvez et al.—the paper would be further strengthened.

Recommendation: Accept (8).

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This paper improves generalization bounds based on the notion of stability. The notion of stability measures the effect of resampling a data point to the output of the algorithm. The paper improves known analyses in two directions: 
- It proposes to use the notion of expected stability rather than uniform stability, which should to be tighter.
- It generalizes the analysis to noises in an exponential family rather than Gaussian noise.
reviews.main_review:
This paper provides several technical improvements to a recent line of work that I am not familiar with. Thus I can hardly review that relevance  and novelty of these improvements (and I am thus putting a low confidence score). I am concerned by the fact that the article is hard to read in a self-contained way. The authors largely comment the articulation of their work with similar works, but almost never comment the relevance of their work for a wider community. 

Another concern is that the article provides two improvements listed above, but I could not perceive why it was coherent to present them in a common article. This might add some confusion to the paper. 

As a naive question, I would like to ask why expected stability bounds are preferred. It seems that they involve expectations that are hard to compute analytically, at best one can approximate these expectations by sampling. How should these bounds be used in the end? In practice, to choose stopping criteria for algorithms, for instance? Or in theory, to analyze the performance of some algorithms?

I found the generalization from Gaussian noise to exponential families novel and interesting.

In Section 2, I find difficult to determine what are your contributions and what was already known. For instance, Proposition 2 seems well-known right? Why not giving some references rather than proving it? Similarly, to what extent was Proposition 1 known?

Minor comments:
-  Section 2: "D be an unknown distribution OVER Z" (not "of")
- Why do you introduce the notation W_{0:(t-1)} = w_{0:(t-1)}? I am under the impression that this is twice the same object, but I might misunderstand.
- You sometimes use the notation KL(P,Q) and KL(P||Q).
- Conflict of notation between S_0 and S_n (for n=0!).
